# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define "clean milk production". List any four hygienic practices essential for clean milk production.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Clean milk production refers to milk obtained from healthy milch animals under hygienic conditions, free from dirt, pathogens, and extraneous matter, with low bacterial count (< 10⁴ CFU/mL) and normal composition.

**Four hygienic practices:**
1. Healthy herd — regular veterinary screening for TB, Brucellosis, Mastitis.
2. Clean animal — washing udder, flanks, and hind quarters with clean water and drying before milking.
3. Clean milker — short trimmed nails, washed hands, headgear, sanitized overalls.
4. Clean equipment — Stainless Steel 304 utensils, mechanical cleaning with 1% detergent and 200 ppm chlorine sanitizer; air-dry in sunlight.

*(Each practice: 0.5 mark; definition: 1 mark)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** What is "titratable acidity" of milk? Why is fresh milk slightly acidic though it appears neutral?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Titratable acidity (TA) of milk is the amount of alkali (typically 0.1 N NaOH) required to neutralize the acid present in 100 mL of milk, expressed as percentage lactic acid. Fresh cow milk has TA = 0.14 – 0.16 % LA.

Fresh milk has pH 6.5–6.7, slightly acidic due to:
1. **Casein phosphate** — phosphorylated amino acid residues release H⁺.
2. **Dissolved CO₂** from cow's metabolism forms carbonic acid (H₂CO₃).
3. **Citrate and phosphate buffers** also contribute.

*(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for reasons)*

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Module-1 14-mark questions have an **internal choice** — answer either Question A OR Question B. Both are given below; prepare both to be safe.

---

### **PART B Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the composition of cow milk in detail. Explain the role of each major constituent.

**(b) [7 Marks)** What are the thermal properties of milk? Explain specific heat, freezing point, and boiling point with engineering significance.

#### **Model Answer**

**(a) Composition of Cow Milk — 7 Marks**

Cow milk is a multi-phase colloidal system composed of:

1. **Water (87.0%)** — Acts as the dispersion medium. Most is "free water" available for microbial proliferation, hence the importance of refrigeration. (1 mark)

2. **Milk Fat (4.0%)** — Present as emulsified globules (1–10 µm) coated with the milk fat globule membrane (MFGM). Composition: ~97% triglycerides, ~2% unsaponifiable matter (cholesterol, vitamin A, D, E, carotenoids). Provides 50% of calories and all fat-soluble vitamins. (1.5 marks)

3. **Proteins (3.4%)** — Divided into caseins (80%, acid-precipitable) and whey proteins (20%, acid-soluble). Caseins include αs1 (38%), αs2 (10%), β (36%), and κ (13%) — exist as colloidal micelles stabilized by κ-casein hairs. Whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin, immunoglobulins, serum albumin) are heat-sensitive and provide immune protection. Provide essential amino acids. (2 marks)

4. **Lactose (4.8%)** — Disaccharide of glucose + galactose joined by β-1,4 glycosidic bond. Substrate for lactic acid bacteria. Source of galactose for infant brain development. (1 mark)

5. **Minerals / Ash (0.7%)** — Calcium, Phosphorus, Potassium, Sodium, Magnesium, Chlorine, Sulphur. Ca:P ratio ≈ 1.27:1, ideal for human bone formation. Iron content is low (defatted cow milk is anaemia-inducing in infants if not fortified). (1 mark)

6. **Vitamins** — Fat-soluble (A, D, E, K) and water-soluble (B-complex, C). Vitamin C is destroyed by pasteurization; pasteurized milk is therefore not a vitamin-C source. (0.5 mark)

**(b) Thermal Properties — 7 Marks**

1. **Specific Heat ($C_p$):** ~**3.93 kJ/(kg·K)** for whole cow milk. Determined by weighted mass fractions of constituents. Empirical formula:
   $$ C_p = 4.19 - 2.4 y_f - 1.4 y_{snf} \quad [\text{kJ/kg·K}] $$
   *Engineering significance:* Determines energy required to heat/cool milk — used in design of plate heat exchangers and refrigeration plants. (2.5 marks)

2. **Freezing Point:** Cow milk freezes at **−0.540 °C (Hortvet)**. The depression is due to dissolved lactose and salts (colligative property). *Engineering significance:* Used as an adulteration test — added water raises freezing point toward 0 °C. A value of −0.460 °C corresponds to ~15% added water. (2 marks)

3. **Boiling Point:** At 1 atm, milk boils at **100.15 °C** — only 0.05–0.15 °C above water because solutes are dilute. *Engineering significance:* Defines operation of vacuum evaporators (concentration plants operate at 60–70 °C under vacuum to preserve heat-sensitive nutrients). (2.5 marks)

---

### **PART B Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram the HTST pasteurization of milk. Discuss time-temperature combination and its bacteriological justification.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the operations involved in the procurement and preservation of raw milk from farm to processing plant.

#### **Model Answer**

**(a) HTST Pasteurization — 7 Marks**

**Principle:** Heating every particle of milk to **72 °C for 15 seconds** (HTST = High Temperature Short Time), followed by immediate cooling to 4 °C. The process destroys all pathogenic vegetative microorganisms while preserving sensory and nutritional quality.

**Equipment Flow Diagram (Plate Heat Exchanger + Holding Tube):**

```
Raw Milk 4°C → Raw Section (Pre-heating) → Heating Section (Steam) 
            → Holding Tube (72°C, 15s) → Flow Diversion Valve 
            → Regeneration Section (Cooling) → Cooling Section (2°C water)
            → Pasteurized Milk 4°C → Storage
```

**Time-Temperature Justification:**
- 72 °C × 15 s is mathematically derived from the D-value of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* (the most heat-resistant non-spore pathogen), which has $D_{72} \approx 1.5$ s.
- 15 s holding = 10× D-value = 10-log reduction, well above the 5-log safety margin required by Codex Alimentarius.
- $F_0$ value (reference to 72 °C) = 15 s → $P_0 = \int 10^{(T-72)/z} dt$ gives pasteurization equivalent.

**Bacteriological Effect:**
- 99.999% destruction of *M. tuberculosis*, *Salmonella*, *E. coli*, *Listeria*, *Coxiella*.
- 95–99% destruction of total mesophilic count.
- Spores (*Bacillus*, *Clostridium*) survive — but they don't germinate at 4 °C storage.

**Regeneration:** The outgoing hot pasteurized milk pre-heats incoming cold raw milk through the regeneration section, achieving **85–95% heat recovery** — saves substantial steam.

**Phosphatase test:** Negative (correct pasteurization). **Peroxidase test:** Positive (not overheated).

*(7 marks distributed as: 1.5 principle, 2.0 diagram & flow, 1.5 TT justification, 2.0 bacteriological effect)*

**(b) Procurement & Preservation of Raw Milk — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Milking at Farm:** Clean milking of healthy cows into SS-304 pails; first strips rejected (high bacterial load).

**Step 2 — Transport to Village Primary Co-operative (MPCS):** In 40 L cans (insulated) within 1 hour.

**Step 3 — Automatic Milk Collection Station (AMCS):** Sample taken for fat/SNF test (Gerber + lactometer), electronic weighing, immediate payment.

**Step 4 — Bulk Milk Cooling:** Within 2 hours of milking, milk cooled to **4 °C** in a **Bulk Milk Cooler (BMC)** of 500–3,000 L capacity using a direct expansion refrigeration system.

**Step 5 — Insulated Road Tanker Transport:** Stainless steel tankers (5,000 L capacity) maintain temperature at 4 °C with insulation and agitator.

**Step 6 — District Chilling Centre / Processing Dairy:** Milk received, tested for quality (organoleptic, COB, acidity, fat, SNF, antibiotic residues, urea, starch, formalin), and pumped to insulated storage silos.

**Preservation Principles:**
- **Temperature control** is primary — keep at ≤ 4 °C.
- **Lactoperoxidase system (LPS)** — activates natural antibacterial enzyme using H₂O₂ (30 ppm) + SCN⁻ (15 ppm); approved by Codex for developing countries.
- **Thermization** — sub-pasteurization at 63–65 °C / 15 s for stored raw milk.

**Quality Standards (FSSAI):**
- Total Plate Count < 10⁶ CFU/mL at plant receipt.
- TA < 0.18 % LA.
- Freezing Point ≤ −0.512 °C.
- No antibiotics, neutralizers, urea, starch, formalin, detergents.

*(Distribution: 1 milking, 1 collection, 1 cooling, 1 transport, 1 preservation principles, 1 standards, 1 diagram-quality matrix)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing TA with pH:** TA is % lactic acid (0.14–0.16); pH is 6.5–6.7. Mixing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** $C_p$ in kJ/(kg·K) ≠ kJ/(kg·°C) for marking — write units explicitly.
> 3. **Not drawing a flow diagram in 14-mark questions on pasteurization:** A labelled HTST diagram is worth ~2 marks; without it, answers lose even if content is correct.
> 4. **Writing "pasteurization was invented by Pasteur"** — Pasteur worked on wine; **Soxhlet** introduced pasteurization for milk (1886). Avoid this factual error.
> 5. **Skipping the numerical value of pH and TA** — KTU examiners expect both pH and TA together; only stating one is incomplete.
> 6. **Standardization formula confusion:** Many students confuse "Pearson's square method" with the algebraic mass-balance. Either is acceptable, but show the work.
> 7. **Confusing UHT with Sterilization:** UHT is continuous + aseptic pack (138 °C/4 s); Sterilization is in-bottle (115 °C/20 min). Both are different.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision before exam — read these bullets.**

### A. Dairy Development
- ✅ Operation Flood phases: I (1970–81), II (1981–85), III (1985–96)
- ✅ Architect: **Dr. Verghese Kurien** — Father of White Revolution
- ✅ NDDB HQ: **Anand, Gujarat**
- ✅ India: **World's No. 1 milk producer** (~230 MT/year)
- ✅ Top 5 milk producing states: UP, Rajasthan, Gujarat, MP, AP
- ✅ Per capita availability: ~**444 g/day** (2023-24)

### B. Milk Types & Composition
- ✅ Cow milk fat: 3.5–5.0%; Buffalo milk fat: 6.0–8.5%
- ✅ Cow milk protein: 3.4%; Buffalo milk protein: 4.5%
- ✅ Buffalo milk = **55%**, Cow milk = **43%**, Goat milk = **3%** of India's production
- ✅ Casein:Whey ratio = **80:20** in cow milk
- ✅ κ-casein stabilizes micelles; αs1, αs2, β-caseins aggregate with calcium phosphate
- ✅ Major whey proteins: β-Lactoglobulin (50%), α-Lactalbumin (20%)
- ✅ Lactose = Glucose + Galactose (β-1,4 bond)
- ✅ Lactose intolerance due to β-galactosidase deficiency

### C. Thermal Properties (must memorize)
- ✅ $C_p \approx$ **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)** for whole cow milk
- ✅ $C_p \approx$ **3.85 kJ/(kg·K)** for skim milk
- ✅ Freezing point: **−0.540 °C** (Hortvet)
- ✅ Boiling point: **100.15 °C** at 1 atm
- ✅ Density: **1030 kg/m³** at 20 °C
- ✅ Refractive index: **1.344–1.348**
- ✅ Calorific value: **62 kcal/100 g** (cow), **97 kcal/100 g** (buffalo)
- ✅ Surface tension: **50–55 dyne/cm**
- ✅ Conductivity: **0.56 W/(m·K)** at 20 °C

### D. Chemical Properties
- ✅ pH: **6.5–6.7** (mean 6.6)
- ✅ TA cow: **0.14–0.16 % LA**; Buffalo: **0.18–0.22 % LA**
- ✅ Curdling pH: **~4.6** (isoelectric of casein)
- ✅ Density range: **1.028–1.034 g/mL**
- ✅ Conductivity: **4.0–5.5 mS/cm** (higher in mastitis)

### E. Clean Milk Production
- ✅ TBC at farm exit: **< 10⁴ CFU/mL**
- ✅ SCC healthy milk: **< 2 × 10⁵ cells/mL**
- ✅ CMT test for mastitis
- ✅ SS-304 grade steel for utensils

### F. Procurement & Preservation
- ✅ Chill milk to **4 °C within 2 hours** of milking
- ✅ BMC (Bulk Milk Cooler) capacity 500–3,000 L
- ✅ Tanker capacity 5,000 L; insulation maintains 4 °C
- ✅ FSSAI standards for raw milk at reception
- ✅ LP-system uses H₂O₂ (30 ppm) + SCN⁻ (15 ppm)

### G. Processing
- ✅ **LTLT:** 63 °C / 30 min
- ✅ **HTST:** 72 °C / 15 s — most common
- ✅ **UHT:** 135–150 °C / 4–15 s + aseptic pack
- ✅ **Sterilization:** 108–115 °C / 20–30 min in-bottle
- ✅ **Homogenization:** 150–250 bar pressure; reduces globules < 1 µm
- ✅ **Standardization:** Pearson's square or mass-balance for fat/SNF
- ✅ **Separator speed:** 6,000–8,000 rpm (centrifugal cream separation)
- ✅ **Phosphatase test** = negative confirms pasteurization
- ✅ **Peroxidase test** = positive confirms gentle pasteurization
- ✅ **Regeneration efficiency** = 85–95% in HTST

### H. Milk Products
- ✅ **Ghee:** ~99.5% fat, water < 0.2%, Reichert value 28–32
- ✅ **Paneer:** Heat-acid coagulation at 70 °C with citric acid/lactic acid
- ✅ **Cheese:** Rennet coagulation at 30–35 °C, ripening
- ✅ **SMP:** Spray drying to < 4% moisture
- ✅ **Khoa:** Concentration to ~70% TS, used in sweets

### I. Engineering Formulas (must memorize)
- ✅ $TS = 100 - W$
- ✅ $C_p = 4.19 - 2.4 y_f - 1.4 y_{snf}$
- ✅ $Q = m C_p \Delta T$
- ✅ $\rho = 1033.6 - 0.18 T$
- ✅ Stokes: $v = 2r^2 g \Delta\rho / 9\eta$
- ✅ Standardization: $V_{\text{whole}} = V_{\text{target}} (F_{\text{target}} - F_{\text{skim}}) / (F_{\text{whole}} - F_{\text{skim}})$
- ✅ Added water %: $(\theta_{true} - \theta_{obs})/\theta_{true} \times 100$
- ✅ Calorific value: $9.11 F + 5.86 P + 3.95 L$ kcal/100 g

### J. Numerical Problem Templates for Practice

1. Heat load for pasteurizer — given $\dot{m}$, $T_{in}$, $T_{out}$, $C_p$ — find steam.
2. Standardization — given two milks of different fat %, target fat %, total volume — find proportions.
3. Adulteration — given freezing point — find % added water.
4. Paneer yield — given milk fat + casein, find expected paneer weight (~ 1 L milk → 200 g paneer typical).
5. Cooling time — given BMC capacity, milk temperature, refrigeration capacity — find cooling time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Flow Charts for Product Manufacture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT602 - DAIRY AND FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Flow Charts for Product Manufacture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Process Flow Chart (PFC)**, also called a **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)**, is a standardized graphical representation that documents the sequential flow of raw materials, intermediate products, finished products, energy, utilities, and waste streams through every unit operation involved in the manufacture of a food or dairy product. In the context of **PCAGT602 – Dairy and Food Engineering**, a PFC visually integrates **receiving → preprocessing → processing → packaging → storage → distribution**, allowing the engineer to track transformations such as pasteurization, homogenization, fermentation, separation, concentration, and drying.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A Process Flow Chart for product manufacture is a sequential, symbolic representation of all unit operations and process streams (raw materials, intermediates, products, by-products, effluents, and utilities) used to manufacture a specific dairy/food product, drawn using BIS/IS and ISO standardized symbols.*

The chart is constructed using standardized symbols governed by **IS 3232 (Indian Standard for Graphical Symbols for Process Flow Diagrams)** and **ISO 14617**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Process Flow Chart as a "Recipe Roadmap"** used by a chef in a five-star hotel kitchen:

- A chef never memorizes 30 steps verbally — instead, he pastes a **wall poster** showing arrows, vessels, and temperatures.
- Each **vessel icon** = a cooking pot, oven, or mixer.
- Each **arrow** = ingredients moving in (raw) or finished dish moving out.
- Each **labeled box** = a specific unit operation (e.g., "boil at 90°C for 15 min").
- The **head chef** can instantly locate where contamination, wastage, or delay occurs.

A dairy engineer uses the **same logic** but with industrial equipment: reception tanks → plate heat exchangers → homogenizers → packaging lines. The PFC is the **engineer's wall poster** for the entire plant.

## 1.3 Purpose of Process Flow Charts

A well-drawn PFC serves the following engineering functions:

- **Process Documentation:** Single source of truth for plant operations.
- **HACCP / FSSAI Compliance:** Identifies Critical Control Points (CCPs).
- **Mass & Energy Balance Foundation:** Provides the basis for all quantitative calculations.
- **Equipment Sizing:** Determines pipe sizes, pump capacities, and tank volumes.
- **Troubleshooting & Training:** Enables operators to follow logic during upsets.
- **Regulatory Audit:** Mandatory under FSSAI 2006 and ISO 22000 audits.

## 1.4 Standard Symbols Used in Food/Dairy PFCs

| Symbol Type | Visual Representation | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Process Vessel** | Rectangle with rounded corners | Storage tank, mixing tank, balance tank |
| **Unit Operation** | Rectangular box (labelled) | Pasteurizer, separator, evaporator |
| **Stream / Flow** | Solid line with arrowhead | Material or product flow direction |
| **Utility Line** | Dashed line with arrow | Steam, water, chilled water, refrigerant |
| **Instrumentation** | Circle with letter code (T, P, F, L) | Temperature, Pressure, Flow, Level sensor |
| **Control Valve** | Bow-tie symbol on line | Regulation of flow/pressure |
| **Pump / Compressor** | Circle with internal triangle | Material transport device |
| **Heat Exchanger** | Two parallel plates or shell-tube icon | Heating, cooling, regeneration |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examinations, students are expected to **draw PFCs by hand** using these standardized symbols — memorize the symbol set before the exam.

## 1.5 Why Process Flow Charts Are Critical in Dairy & Food Plants

In dairy and food engineering, the product is **perishable, biological, and safety-critical**. Unlike chemical plants, the engineer must additionally track:

- **Microbiological load** (TPC, coliform counts) at each stage.
- **Temperature-time history** (cold chain integrity).
- **Moisture and water activity (a_w)** changes.
- **Cross-contamination** between raw and finished product streams.

A PFC captures all these vectors, making it the **backbone of process design** in any dairy/food factory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Process Flow Documentation

KTU follows the **standard three-tier hierarchy** used in process industries:

### Tier 1 — Block Flow Diagram (BFD)
- **Coarsest level.** Shows only major process blocks (e.g., "Reception", "Processing", "Packaging").
- Used in feasibility reports and plant layout studies.

### Tier 2 — Process Flow Chart (PFC) / Process Flow Diagram (PFD)
- **Mid-level detail.** Shows every unit operation with mass and energy streams.
- Includes operating conditions (T, P, flow rates).
- **This is what KTU Module 2 focuses on.**

### Tier 3 — Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)
- **Highest level of detail.** Shows every pipe, valve, instrument, and control loop.
- Used for construction and commissioning.

## 2.2 Logical Steps to Construct a PFC

A KTU-evaluated PFC must follow this **structured construction logic**:

1. **Define Product & Yield Target** — e.g., 10,000 L/day of pasteurized toned milk.
2. **List Raw Materials** — Raw milk, sugar, additives, packaging material.
3. **Enumerate Unit Operations** in chronological order (receiving → ... → dispatch).
4. **Identify Stream Types** — Raw input, intermediate, finished product, by-product, waste, utilities.
5. **Assign Operating Conditions** — Temperature (°C), Pressure (kPa), Flow rate (L/h or kg/h).
6. **Insert Quality Control Points** — Sampling, testing, CCPs.
7. **Add Utilities** — Steam, chilled water, CIP (Clean-in-Place) loops.
8. **Verify Mass Balance Closure** — Σ(inputs) = Σ(outputs) + Σ(losses).
9. **Cross-check with FSSAI/HACCP Plan.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The PFC construction is supported by quantitative laws. The following equations are repeatedly asked in KTU exams:

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $M_{in} = M_{out} + M_{loss}$ | Overall mass balance | kg/h |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{in} \cdot C_{p,in} \cdot T_{in} + Q = \dot{m}_{out} \cdot C_{p,out} \cdot T_{out}$ | Energy balance (single stream) | kW |
| 3 | $\eta_{process} = \dfrac{M_{finished}}{M_{raw}} \times 100$ | Process yield (%) | % |
| 4 | $F_{pasteurization} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot v}{L}$ | Residence time factor for HTST | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat exchanger duty | W (watts) |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | °C or K |
| 7 | $L_{required} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot v_{max}}$ | Pipe sizing for product flow | m |
| 8 | $TDP_{milk} = -0.539^{\circ}\text{C}$ (constant) | True freezing point of milk | °C |
| 9 | $SNF_{\%} = \dfrac{\rho - 1}{\Delta} \times 100$ | Solids-not-fat estimation | % |
| 10 | $Q_{steam} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{milk} \cdot C_{p, milk} \cdot \Delta T}{h_{fg}}$ | Steam requirement in pasteurizer | kg/h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Formula numbers **1, 3, 5, and 6** appear in nearly every Module 2 numerical question. Memorize the LMTD equation in its full logarithmic form — partial marks are lost for missing the natural logarithm.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the PFC Framework

The PFC is not merely an academic diagram. It is **the working document** for:

- **Dairy cooperatives** (e.g., Amul, Mother Dairy) — daily production planning.
- **FSSAI Auditors** — verification of CCP placement.
- **Equipment vendors** — sizing of plate heat exchangers (PHE), homogenizers.
- **Plant managers** — root-cause analysis during product recalls.
- **Energy auditors** — steam and refrigeration load estimation.

## 2.5 Application to Specific Dairy/Food Products

The PFC structure varies depending on whether the product is:

- **Liquid & Homogeneous** — milk, juice, beverages.
- **Semi-solid** — yogurt, cheese, butter.
- **Dry/particulate** — milk powder, instant coffee.
- **Fermented** — curd, cheese, idli/dosa batter.
- **Concentrated** — condensed milk, tomato paste.

KTU Module 2 expects students to draw PFCs for **at least three** of the following: (a) Pasteurized milk, (b) Butter, (c) Cheese, (d) Yogurt/Dahi, (e) Skimmed Milk Powder (SMP), (f) Ghee, (g) Ice cream.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Mass Balance for a Pasteurized Milk Plant

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Part B Question]**
> *A dairy plant receives 50,000 kg/day of raw milk at 4°C. During clarification, 0.1% is lost as sludge. During standardization, 4% is removed as cream and processed separately. After pasteurization, 0.5% is lost as evaporation. Calculate the quantity of pasteurized standardized milk produced per day. Also compute the overall process yield.*

### Step 1 — Establish the baseline input

$$
M_{raw} = 50{,}000 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

The raw milk enters as the **single input stream**.

### Step 2 — Apply clarification loss

The clarifier (centrifugal separator) removes **dirt, somatic cells, and sediment**.

$$
M_{after\_clarification} = M_{raw} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{L_{clarifier}}{100}\right)
$$

Substituting $L_{clarifier} = 0.1\% = 0.001$:

$$
M_{after\_clarification} = 50{,}000 \times (1 - 0.001) = 50{,}000 \times 0.999 = 49{,}950 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply standardization loss (cream removal)

$$
M_{after\_standardization} = M_{after\_clarification} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{L_{standardization}}{100}\right)
$$

$$
M_{after\_standardization} = 49{,}950 \times (1 - 0.04) = 49{,}950 \times 0.96 = 47{,}952 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply pasteurization loss (evaporation)

$$
M_{pasteurized\_milk} = M_{after\_standardization} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{L_{pasteurization}}{100}\right)
$$

$$
M_{pasteurized\_milk} = 47{,}952 \times (1 - 0.005) = 47{,}952 \times 0.995 = 47{,}712.24 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Overall Process Yield

$$
\eta_{process} = \dfrac{M_{finished}}{M_{raw}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{process} = \dfrac{47{,}712.24}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 95.42\%
$$

### Step 6 — Final Answer Statement

> **Final Answer:** The plant produces **47,712.24 kg/day** of pasteurized standardized milk, with an overall process yield of **95.42%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marker Expectation:** Always quote the yield to **two decimal places** and explicitly state the units. Examiners award the 1-mark "unit and presentation" component only when this discipline is followed.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Steam Requirement in HTST Pasteurizer

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Part B Question]**
> *Calculate the steam requirement (kg/h) for heating 10,000 kg/h of milk from 4°C to 72°C in an HTST pasteurizer. Assume milk specific heat $C_p = 3.93$ kJ/kg·K and latent heat of saturated steam $h_{fg} = 2,260$ kJ/kg. Assume 90% thermal efficiency.*

### Step 1 — Calculate temperature rise

$$
\Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in} = 72^{\circ}\text{C} - 4^{\circ}\text{C} = 68^{\circ}\text{C} = 68 \ \text{K}
$$

(For temperature **differences**, °C and K are numerically identical.)

### Step 2 — Calculate total heat load

$$
Q_{required} = \dot{m}_{milk} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
Q_{required} = 10{,}000 \times 3.93 \times 68 = 2{,}672{,}400 \ \text{kJ/h}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to kW (optional, for clarity)

$$
Q_{required} = \dfrac{2{,}672{,}400}{3600} = 742.33 \ \text{kW}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply efficiency correction

The heat exchanger is only 90% efficient, so the actual heat input from steam must be larger:

$$
Q_{actual} = \dfrac{Q_{required}}{\eta_{thermal}} = \dfrac{2{,}672{,}400}{0.90} = 2{,}969{,}333.33 \ \text{kJ/h}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate steam mass flow

$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{h_{fg}} = \dfrac{2{,}969{,}333.33}{2{,}260} = 1{,}313.86 \ \text{kg/h}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Answer

> **Final Answer:** Steam required $\approx$ **1,313.86 kg/h** of saturated steam to heat the milk stream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to divide by $\eta_{thermal}$. If the question says "assume X% efficiency," **always include the division step**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — LMTD for a Plate Heat Exchanger

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2, Part A Question]**
> *Hot water enters an HX at 85°C and leaves at 45°C. Cold milk enters at 4°C and leaves at 60°C. Calculate LMTD.*

### Step 1 — Identify the four end temperatures

$$
T_{h,in} = 85^{\circ}\text{C}, \quad T_{h,out} = 45^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

$$
T_{c,in} = 4^{\circ}\text{C}, \quad T_{c,out} = 60^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the two-end temperature differences

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 85 - 60 = 25^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 45 - 4 = 41^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the LMTD formula

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{25 - 41}{\ln\left(\dfrac{25}{41}\right)} = \dfrac{-16}{\ln(0.6098)} = \dfrac{-16}{-0.4943}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = 32.37^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

> **Final Answer:** $\Delta T_{LMTD} \approx$ **32.37°C** — this is the effective driving temperature difference used in the HX design equation $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative Sign Convention:** Although we get a negative numerator and a negative logarithm, the LMTD is **always taken as positive** in dairy HX calculations. The absolute magnitude of the driving force is what matters for heat transfer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PFC Components vs. Information Captured

The following matrix summarizes **what data the PFC captures** at each unit operation level. This is a frequently asked KTU theory question.

| PFC Component | Material Streams | Energy/Utility Streams | Control Points (CCPs) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Raw Milk Reception** | Raw milk IN; rejected milk OUT | CIP chemicals; chilled water | Tanker temperature check |
| **Clarification** | Skim + Cream OUT; sludge OUT | Water for seal | Visual inspection |
| **Standardization** | Standardized milk OUT; cream OUT | None | Fat % verification |
| **Pasteurization (HTST)** | Pasteurized milk OUT | Steam IN; hot water OUT | **CCP** — T = 72°C, τ = 15 s |
| **Homogenization** | Homogenized milk OUT | Cooling water | Pressure = 200 bar |
| **Packaging** | Finished milk OUT | None | Seal integrity test |
| **Cold Storage** | Finished goods OUT | Refrigeration | **CCP** — T ≤ 4°C |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** When asked to draw a PFC and annotate it, always mark the **CCPs in red or with a star symbol** — this fetches bonus marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dairy PFC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[RM01: Raw Milk Tanker] --> B1[UO01: Reception Dock]
    B1 --> C1[UO02: Chilled Storage Tank]
    C1 --> D1[UO03: Clarifier Separator]
    D1 --> E1[UO04: Plate Heat Exchanger]
    E1 --> F1[UO05: Homogenizer]
    F1 --> G1[UO06: Pasteurizer HTST 72C 15s]
    G1 --> H1[UO07: Cooling Section PHE]
    H1 --> I1[UO08: Storage Silo]
    I1 --> J1[UO09: Packaging Line]
    J1 --> K1[UO10: Cold Room 4C]
    K1 --> L1[FP01: Finished Goods Dispatch]

    W1[BY01: Sludge to ETP] -.-> D1
    W2[BY02: CIP Chemicals Loop] -.-> B1
    W3[UT01: Steam Supply] -.-> E1
    W4[UT02: Chilled Water] -.-> H1

    classDef ccp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    class G1,K1 ccp
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:** Solid arrows = material flow. Dashed arrows = utility/waste streams. **Yellow-highlighted nodes** are the Critical Control Points (CCPs) identified for HACCP compliance.

---

## 4.2 Detailed PFC — Pasteurized Toned Milk (10,000 L/day)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INTAKE ["INTAKE SECTION"]
        N1[Tanker Reception 4C] --> N2[Weighing Tank]
        N2 --> N3[Plate Cooler Pre-cool]
        N3 --> N4[Raw Milk Silo 5000 L]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS ["PROCESSING SECTION"]
        N4 --> O1[Clarifier 6000 Lph]
        O1 --> O2[Standardizer Fat 3.5%]
        O2 --> O3[PHE Heater Section]
        O3 --> O4[Homogenizer 200 bar]
        O4 --> O5[HTST Holding Tube 15s]
        O5 --> O6[Flow Diversion Valve]
        O6 --> O7[PHE Cooling Section 4C]
        O7 --> O8[Storage Tank 3000 L]
    end

    subgraph PACK ["PACKAGING SECTION"]
        O8 --> P1[Inline Filler 500 mL]
        P1 --> P2[Sealing Machine]
        P2 --> P3[Date Coding Station]
        P3 --> P4[Crate Loading]
    end

    subgraph COLD ["COLD CHAIN"]
        P4 --> Q1[Cold Room 4C]
        Q1 --> Q2[Refrigerated Van Dispatch]
    end

    S1[Steam Line 1.5 bar] -.-> O3
    S2[Chilled Water 2C] -.-> O7
    S3[Sludge to ETP] -.-> O1
    S4[Rejected Milk UHT] -.-> N1

    classDef ccpNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:4px,color:#FFF
    classDef utilNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    class O5,O6,Q1 ccpNode
    class S1,S2,S3,S4 utilNode
```

**Process Conditions Captured:**

| Equipment | Operating Condition | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Plate Cooler | T_out = 10°C | Initial cooling |
| Clarifier | 6,000 L/h capacity | Sediment removal |
| HTST Holder | **72°C / 15 s** | **CCP-1: Pathogen kill** |
| Flow Diversion Valve | T < 72°C → divert back | **CCP-2: Re-pasteurization** |
| Homogenizer | **200 bar** | Fat globule size reduction |
| Cold Room | **T ≤ 4°C** | **CCP-3: Cold chain** |

---

## 4.3 Detailed PFC — Cheese (Cheddar) Manufacture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Whole Milk] --> B[Standardization 3.5% Fat]
    B --> C[HTST Pasteurization 72C 15s]
    C --> D[Cooling to 31C]
    D --> E[Addition of Starter Culture 2%]
    E --> F[Ripening 30 to 45 min]
    F --> G[Addition of Rennet 0.002%]
    G --> H[Curd Formation 30 min]
    H --> I[Curd Cutting]
    I --> J[Cooking 38C 30 min]
    J --> K[Whey Drainage]
    K --> L[Cheddaring 38C 2 h]
    L --> M[Milling and Salting]
    M --> N[Pressing 12 h]
    N --> O[Vacuum Packaging]
    O --> P[Curing 4C 3 to 6 months]
    P --> Q[Final Cheese Block]

    BY1[Whey to Whey Protein Plant] -.-> K
    UT1[Steam] -.-> C
    UT2[Chilled Water] -.-> D

    classDef ccpNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    class C,P ccpNode
```

> [!TIP]
> In cheese-making PFC, **whey is a valuable by-product**, not a waste. Always route it to a **whey processing unit** for protein recovery (WPC-80, lactose). KTU examiners mark this positively.

---

## 4.4 Detailed PFC — Yogurt / Dahi Manufacture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Milk Reception] --> B[Filtration]
    B --> C[Standardization 3.5% Fat]
    C --> D[Dry Solids Standardization 11%]
    D --> E[Homogenization 100 bar 60C]
    E --> F[Heat Treatment 90C 10 min]
    F --> G[Cooling to 42C]
    G --> H[Inoculation Starter 2%]
    H --> I[Incubation 42C 4 to 6 h]
    I --> J{pH Check 4.5?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Cooling to 5C]
    J -->|No| I
    K --> L[Fruit Addition Optional]
    L --> M[Packaging Cup or Pouch]
    M --> N[Cold Storage 4C]
    N --> O[Distribution]

    UT1[Steam 2 bar] -.-> F
    UT2[Chilled Water] -.-> K
    UT3[CIP Loop] -.-> A

    classDef ccpNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    class F,J,N ccpNode
```

**Process Highlights:**

- **CCP-1:** Heat treatment at 90°C/10 min eliminates competing microflora.
- **pH endpoint (4.5)** is the **fermentation CCP** — checked with pH meter.
- **Cold break** to 5°C stops fermentation (otherwise post-acidification in storage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The decision diamond (`pH Check`) is a **logical control node**. KTU students are expected to include decision nodes whenever a quality gate exists. Drawing only linear flow without gates results in **2-mark deduction**.

---

## 4.5 PFC — Butter Manufacturing (Continuous Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ Pasteurized Cream 35 to 40% Fat] --> B[Ageing Tank 8C 12 h]
    B --> C[Continuous Churn]
    C --> D[Butter Grains]
    D --> E[Separation of Buttermilk]
    E --> F[Washing]
    F --> G[Working Salting]
    G --> H[Vacuum De-aeration]
    H --> I[Printing Molding]
    I --> J[Wrapping]
    J --> K[Hardening 0C]
    K --> L[Cold Storage Dispatch]

    BY1[Buttermilk to Recombined Milk] -.-> E
    UT1[Chilled Water] -.-> B
    UT2[Steam for Cleaning] -.-> C

    classDef ccpNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    class A,K ccpNode
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
> *Define a Process Flow Chart. List any **six standardized symbols** used in drawing a PFC with their engineering meaning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Process Flow Chart (PFC)** is a sequential, symbolic representation of all unit operations and material/utility streams used to manufacture a specific dairy or food product, drawn using BIS/IS standardized symbols. **(1 mark)**

The six standard symbols are:

1. **Rectangle (rounded corners)** → Process vessel / storage tank.
2. **Rectangular box with operation name** → Unit operation (e.g., pasteurizer).
3. **Solid line with arrowhead** → Material/product flow.
4. **Dashed line with arrowhead** → Utility stream (steam, water).
5. **Circle with letter code** → Instrumentation (T = temperature, P = pressure).
6. **Bow-tie symbol** → Control valve on pipeline.

**(2 marks — 1/3 mark each symbol; remaining mark for correct labeling.)**

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
> *Differentiate between a Block Flow Diagram (BFD), Process Flow Chart (PFC), and Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID). Mention the level of detail and typical use of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | BFD | PFC | P&ID |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Detail Level** | Coarsest | Intermediate | Highest |
| **Unit Operations Shown** | Major blocks only | All operations | Every pipe, valve, instrument |
| **Numerical Data** | None | T, P, flow rates | Full dimensions, materials |
| **Use Case** | Feasibility, plant layout | Process design, mass balance | Construction, commissioning, safety |

**(3 marks — 1 mark per correct comparison row.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *Module 2 carries an internal choice. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

> *(a)* Draw a neat **Process Flow Chart** for the manufacture of **Pasteurized Toned Milk (10,000 L/day)** indicating all major unit operations, material streams, utility streams, and Critical Control Points (CCPs). **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* For the plant in part (a), the following data is given:
> - Raw milk input = **10,000 kg/day** at 4°C
> - Clarifier loss = **0.1%**
> - Cream separated in standardization = **4%** of clarified milk
> - Pasteurization evaporation loss = **0.5%**
>
> Calculate the **daily output** of pasteurized toned milk and the **overall process yield**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]**

**Mark Allocation Breakdown:**
- Correct sequence of unit operations (4 marks)
- Correct stream labeling (2 marks)
- CCP identification (1 mark)

**Expected PFC Sequence:**

1. Raw milk reception (4°C) → 2. Chilled storage silo → 3. Clarifier → 4. Standardizer → 5. Plate Heat Exchanger (preheating) → 6. Homogenizer (200 bar) → 7. HTST Pasteurizer (72°C / 15 s) → 8. Flow Diversion Valve → 9. PHE cooling (4°C) → 10. Storage tank → 11. Packaging line → 12. Cold room (≤4°C) → 13. Dispatch.

**Streams to Show:**
- Raw milk (IN), Skim milk, Cream (OUT), Pasteurized milk, Steam (dashed), Chilled water (dashed), CIP loop, Sludge to ETP (dashed).

**CCPs:**
- **CCP-1:** HTST holder — 72°C for 15 seconds minimum.
- **CCP-2:** Flow diversion valve — auto-divert if T < 72°C.
- **CCP-3:** Cold room storage — T ≤ 4°C.

*(Examiner expects a clear, labeled diagram. Hand-drawn neatness matters — full 7 marks awarded only when symbols follow IS 3232.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Baseline Input**

$$
M_{raw} = 10{,}000 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — After Clarifier (0.1% loss)**

$$
M_1 = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.001) = 9{,}990 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 3 — After Standardization (4% cream removal)**

$$
M_2 = 9{,}990 \times (1 - 0.04) = 9{,}590.4 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 4 — After Pasteurization (0.5% evaporation loss)**

$$
M_3 = 9{,}590.4 \times (1 - 0.005) = 9{,}542.45 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Process Yield**

$$
\eta_{process} = \dfrac{9{,}542.45}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 95.42\%
$$

**Final Answer Statement:**
> *The plant produces **9,542.45 kg/day** of pasteurized toned milk with an overall yield of **95.42%**.*

**Mark Allocation:**
- 1 mark — correct baseline.
- 2 marks — sequential application of all three losses.
- 2 marks — correct yield formula and substitution.
- 1 mark — final answer with units and yield statement.
- 1 mark — overall presentation and unit discipline.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Students often apply losses in the **wrong order** — always follow the PFC sequence.
> 2. Many students **forget to apply the second loss to the output of the first** — this gives 0 marks for subsequent steps.
> 3. Yield must be expressed in **%**, not as a decimal (0.9542 is wrong; 95.42% is correct).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> *(a)* Draw a **Process Flow Chart** for the manufacture of **Cheese (Cheddar type)** from standardized milk. Label all major unit operations and identify the CCPs. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* A cheese plant processes **20,000 kg/day** of standardized milk (3.5% fat). The cheese yield is **10 kg cheese per 100 kg milk**. The whey recovered has **6.8% total solids**. Calculate:
> - (i) Daily cheese production.
> - (ii) Daily whey volume if whey density = **1.025 g/mL**.
> - (iii) Total solids recovered in whey. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]**

**Expected PFC Sequence (Cheddar):**

1. Raw milk reception → 2. Standardization (3.5% fat) → 3. HTST pasteurization (72°C / 15 s) → 4. Cooling to 31°C → 5. Starter culture addition (2%) → 6. Ripening (30–45 min) → 7. Rennet addition (0.002%) → 8. Coagulation (30 min) → 9. Curd cutting → 10. Cooking (38°C / 30 min) → 11. Whey drainage → 12. Cheddaring (2 h at 38°C) → 13. Milling and salting → 14. Pressing (12 h) → 15. Vacuum packaging → 16. Curing (3–6 months at 4°C).

**Streams to Show:**
- Whey by-product line to whey processing unit.
- Steam and chilled water utility lines.
- Salt addition stream.

**CCPs:**
- **CCP-1:** Pasteurization — 72°C/15 s for pathogen kill.
- **CCP-2:** Curing storage — 4°C temperature control.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Daily Cheese Production**

$$
M_{cheese} = 20{,}000 \times \dfrac{10}{100} = 2{,}000 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily Whey Mass**

By mass balance (assuming milk density ≈ 1.030 kg/L for simplicity):

$$
M_{whey} = M_{milk} - M_{cheese} - M_{evaporation}
$$

Ignoring evaporation (problem does not state):

$$
M_{whey} \approx 20{,}000 - 2{,}000 = 18{,}000 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily Whey Volume**

$$
V_{whey} = \dfrac{M_{whey}}{\rho_{whey}} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{1{,}025} = 17.56 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

(Equivalently = **17,560 L/day**)

**Step 4 — Total Solids in Whey**

$$
M_{solids} = M_{whey} \times \dfrac{TS_{\%}}{100} = 18{,}000 \times 0.068 = 1{,}224 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Daily cheese production = **2,000 kg/day**.
- (ii) Daily whey volume = **17.56 m³/day (17,560 L/day)**.
- (iii) Total solids in whey = **1,224 kg/day**.

**Mark Allocation:**
- 2 marks for cheese calculation.
- 2 marks for whey volume (mass-to-volume using density).
- 2 marks for solids calculation.
- 1 mark for unit consistency.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (Cheese PFC):**
> 1. Forgetting to **route whey to a by-product unit** — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. Confusing **ripening (acid development)** with **coagulation (rennet action)** — these are two separate unit operations.
> 3. Not labeling the **pressing** step — pressing removes residual whey and determines final moisture.
> 4. Missing the **3–6 month curing step** — this is unique to cheese and **must appear** in the PFC.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

> **Important Things to Remember (High-Density Bullet Summary)**

- **Definition:** PFC = sequential symbolic diagram of unit operations + streams + utilities for a specific dairy/food product.
- **Three Tiers:** BFD (coarse) → PFC (mid) → P&ID (detailed).
- **PFC follows IS 3232 / ISO 14617** standardized symbols — memorize them.
- **Always include utility streams** (steam, chilled water, CIP) as dashed lines.
- **Always mark CCPs** in red or with star symbols — bonus marks in valuation.
- **Decision nodes (diamonds)** for pH, temperature, fat % checks — not optional.
- **Mass balance closure** is mandatory: $\Sigma M_{in} = \Sigma M_{out} + \Sigma M_{loss}$.
- **Overall yield formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{M_{finished}}{M_{raw}} \times 100\%$.
- **LMTD equation:** $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ — always positive magnitude.
- **HTST conditions for milk:** **72°C for 15 seconds** (legal benchmark in India).
- **Whey is a by-product**, not waste — route to WPC/lactose recovery.
- **Yogurt CCPs:** Heat treatment (90°C/10 min), pH endpoint (4.5), cold storage (≤4°C).
- **Cheese CCPs:** Pasteurization (72°C/15 s), curing temperature (4°C).
- **Common products tested:** Pasteurized milk, Butter, Cheese, Yogurt, SMP, Ghee, Ice cream.
- **Always quote** numerical answers with **units** and **two decimal places**.
- **Drawing neatness + labeled streams + CCP marking = full marks** in KTU PFC questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** Practice drawing **at least three PFCs by hand** (Pasteurized Milk, Butter, Cheese) within 25 minutes each. The valuation script rewards speed + neatness + correct sequence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unit Operation of Various Dairy and Food Processing Systems, Principles and Equipment Related to Receiving of Milk, Pasteurization, Sterilization, Homogenization, Centrifugation and Cream Separation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT602 - DAIRY AND FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unit Operation of Various Dairy and Food Processing Systems, Principles and Equipment Related to Receiving of Milk, Pasteurization, Sterilization, Homogenization, Centrifugation and Cream Separation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Unit Operations" in Dairy and Food Engineering?

In the language of chemical and food engineering, a **Unit Operation** is a basic, physical step that transforms a raw agricultural commodity (like milk, grains, fruits, meat) into a finished, shelf-stable, palatable product. Each step is essentially a *building block* — a fundamental physical process such as heating, cooling, separating, mixing, size reduction, or pumping — that can be combined in a sequence (a "process line") to manufacture a product.

In the dairy industry, the *mother raw material* is **raw milk**, which is highly perishable. The job of the dairy engineer is to apply the right unit operations, in the right order, using the right equipment, so the milk becomes safe, nutritious, and marketable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard Wording):**
> *"Unit operations are the physical steps involved in the conversion of raw food materials into processed, preserved, and value-added products. They include receiving, pasteurization, sterilization, homogenization, centrifugation, and cream separation — each governed by well-defined principles of heat transfer, mass transfer, fluid mechanics, and thermodynamics."*

## 1.2 The Six Pillars of Dairy Processing — Quick Overview

| # | Unit Operation | Plain-English Meaning | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Receiving of Milk** | The "greeting desk" of a dairy plant | Like a hospital's admission desk — temperature, weight, and health are checked before the patient is admitted |
| 2 | **Pasteurization** | Mild heating to kill pathogens | Like taking a hot water bath to kill germs on your skin |
| 3 | **Sterilization** | Intense heating to make the product commercially sterile | Like the autoclave that sterilizes surgical instruments |
| 4 | **Homogenization** | Squeezing fat droplets to make them tiny and uniform | Like using a strong pressure washer to break water droplets into a fine mist |
| 5 | **Centrifugation** | Spinning fast to separate substances of different densities | Like a salad spinner throwing off water, but on a *super-charged* scale |
| 6 | **Cream Separation** | Using a centrifuge to pull out fat-rich cream | Like letting oil float on water, only 10,000 times faster |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Dairy Plant as a "Treatment Pipeline"

Imagine a patient arriving at a hospital:
1. **Triage / Reception (Receiving)** — weight, temperature, sample taken.
2. **Mild Sanitation (Pasteurization)** — a warm bath that kills surface germs but keeps the patient healthy.
3. **Major Sterilization (Sterilization)** — full surgical scrub, eliminating *all* microbes.
4. **Smoothing Out (Homogenization)** — turning lumpy blood into smooth serum.
5. **Spinning Test (Centrifugation)** — putting blood in a centrifuge to separate plasma from red cells.
6. **Fat Extraction (Cream Separation)** — skimming the lipid layer off the top.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for KTU Exams:**
> Almost every KTU question is a *process-tracing* question. If you can remember this 6-step pipeline, you can answer any descriptive question on the topic.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics Used Throughout

These appear repeatedly in KTU calculations, so internalize them:

- **Pasteurization temperature:** **72 °C** for **15 seconds** (HTST) — universal standard.
- **UHT temperature:** **138 °C** for **2–4 seconds**.
- **Homogenization pressure:** **2,000 – 2,500 psi** (≈ **140 – 175 bar**) for milk.
- **Cream separator bowl speed:** **6,000 – 9,000 rpm**.
- **Standard milk fat:** **3.5 %**; Standard SNF (Solids-Not-Fat): **8.5 %**.
- **Density of milk fat:** ≈ **0.93 g/cm³** at 20 °C.
- **Density of skim milk:** ≈ **1.035 g/cm³** at 20 °C.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Receiving of Milk — Principles & Equipment

### 2.1.1 Why is "Receiving" a Critical Control Point?

Raw milk is a high-moisture, nutrient-rich medium — a perfect breeding ground for bacteria. The moment milk leaves the udder, the microbial clock starts ticking. **The receiving platform is the First Line of Defense** in a dairy plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Line:**
> *"The quality of finished products can never be better than the quality of raw milk received."*

### 2.1.2 Sequence of Operations at the Receiving Bay

1. **Visual & Odour Inspection** — colour (white/creamy), free from clots, off-odours.
2. **Weighing** — using a weigh bowl / flow meter; logged into the intake register.
3. **Sampling** — representative sample for laboratory tests.
4. **Platform Tests:**
   - **Clot-on-Boiling (COB) test** — detects acidic, sour milk (alcohol test or COB).
   - **Titratable Acidity (TA)** — should be **≤ 0.14 % lactic acid** for fresh milk.
   - **Sediment test** — checks cleanliness.
   - **Antibiotic residue test** — Delvotest or similar.
5. **Cooling** — Rapid cooling to **≤ 4 °C** within **2 hours** of milking.
6. **Filtration / Clarification** — removes dust, hair, somatic cells.

### 2.1.3 Equipment Used at Receiving

- **Weigh bowl** (mechanical / electronic)
- **Plate / tubular coolers** (chilled water or glycol)
- **Inline filters** (cloth or stainless steel mesh)
- **Raw milk silos** (insulated stainless steel, **5,000 – 50,000 L** capacity)
- **Positive displacement pumps** (sanitary lobe or centrifugal)

---

## 2.2 Pasteurization — Principles & Equipment

### 2.2.1 The Underlying Principle

Louis Pasteur (1860s) discovered that heating wine to ~60 °C killed spoilage organisms. The same logic was extended to milk. The goal is:

> **To destroy pathogenic vegetative microorganisms (especially *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Brucella*, *Salmonella*, *E. coli*, *Listeria*) while preserving nutritional and sensory quality.**

The thermal death kinetics follow a logarithmic relationship:

$$ \frac{N}{N_0} = 10^{-t/D} $$

where *N* = survivors, *N₀* = initial count, *t* = time at temperature, *D* = decimal reduction time (time to kill **90 %** of microbes at a given temperature).

### 2.2.2 Time–Temperature Standards (Globally Accepted)

| Method | Temperature | Time | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LTLT (Vat / Batch)** | **63 °C** | **30 min** | Small dairies, traditional products |
| **HTST (Continuous)** | **72 °C** | **15 s** | Most modern dairies (the global standard) |
| **HHST (Higher Heat)** | **89 °C** | **1 s** | Extended-shelf-life fluid milk |
| **UHT (Ultra High Temp)** | **138 °C** | **2–4 s** | Sterile shelf-stable milk |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked "Explain HTST pasteurization", always draw a *plate heat exchanger flow path* (Section 4) and mention the **phosphatase test** as the official control test (alkaline phosphatase is inactivated when pasteurization is correctly done).

### 2.2.3 Pasteurizer Components

A continuous HTST pasteurizer has **five distinct sections** (study this — it comes every KTU paper):

1. **Regeneration (Pre-heating)** — incoming raw milk is pre-heated by outgoing hot pasteurized milk (heat recovery of **≥ 90 %** is common).
2. **Heating** — final heating to **72 °C** using hot water.
3. **Holding** — the **holding tube** of calculated length, ensuring exactly **15 s** of residence time.
4. **Regeneration (Cooling)** — pasteurized milk gives heat to incoming raw milk.
5. **Cooling** — final cooling to **≤ 4 °C** using chilled water / glycol.

### 2.2.4 Key Equipment in Pasteurization

- **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** — most common; compact, high heat transfer.
- **Tubular Heat Exchanger** — used for viscous / particulate products.
- **Scraped Surface Heat Exchanger (SSHE)** — for sticky, viscous, or crystallizing products (ice cream mix, ghee, chocolate).
- **Vacuum Pasteurizer / Vacreator** — for cream, to remove off-flavours.
- **Batch Pasteurizer (Vat)** — double-jacketed stainless vat with slow agitator.

### 2.2.5 Pasteurization Effectiveness (F-Value Concept)

The **F-value** integrates the lethality over the entire time–temperature profile, referenced to a standard temperature (e.g., *Tᵣ* = 72 °C for pasteurization):

$$ F_T^{T_r} = \int_0^t 10^{\frac{T(t) - T_r}{Z}} \, dt $$

For constant temperature, this simplifies to:

$$ F = t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - T_r}{Z}} $$

where *Z* = temperature change required to alter *D* by a factor of 10 (for *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, **Z ≈ 4.4 °C**).

---

## 2.3 Sterilization — Principles & Equipment

### 2.3.1 Objective

To produce **commercially sterile** products, i.e., products free from microorganisms capable of growing under defined storage conditions — even if a few thermophilic spores survive, they cannot grow in the product environment.

### 2.3.2 In-Container Sterilization (Retort Processing)

Cans/bottles are sealed and heated in **autoclaves / batch retorts** or **continuous hydrostatic sterilizers**. The F₀ concept (referenced to **121.1 °C, Z = 10 °C**) is used:

$$ F_0 = t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - 121.1}{Z}} $$

A typical target for low-acid canned foods: **F₀ = 3 to 15 minutes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Difference between Pasteurization and Sterilization:**
> - **Pasteurization** → kills *pathogens* (vegetative) only; refrigerated shelf life.
> - **Sterilization** → kills *all* (including spores); ambient shelf life.

### 2.3.3 UHT / Aseptic Processing

Milk is heated to **138 – 150 °C for 2 – 6 s**, then cooled and packed in **pre-sterilized, hermetically sealed containers** under aseptic conditions — no need for refrigeration.

**UHT methods:**

| Method | Heat Transfer Mode | Holding Time |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Steam Injection / Infusion)** | Steam mixed with milk | **2 – 4 s** |
| **Indirect (Plate / Tubular PHE)** | Heat through wall | **4 – 6 s** |

Direct UHT produces better-quality milk (less cooked flavour) but is more capital-intensive.

### 2.3.4 Sterilization Equipment

- **Batch retorts** (static, agitating, rotary, hydrostatic)
- **Continuous rotary sterilizers**
- **Direct UHT plants** — APV, Tetra Pak, GEA
- **Indirect UHT plants** — Tubular or Plate
- **Aseptic packaging lines** — Tetra Brik, Tetra Rex, Combibloc

---

## 2.4 Homogenization — Principles & Equipment

### 2.4.1 What is Homogenization?

Milk is naturally an **emulsion**: fat globules (≈ **0.1 – 10 µm**, mean **3.5 µm**) dispersed in water. Over time, they **cream up** (float). Homogenization forces the milk under high pressure through a narrow orifice, breaking large globules into **≤ 1 µm** — so they stay suspended permanently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Outcome:** Homogenized milk does not separate, has a **whiter appearance** (more light scattering), a **richer mouthfeel**, and improved digestibility (more surface area for lipase action).

### 2.4.2 The Physics of Homogenization

A high-pressure pump (typically a **three- or five-piston positive-displacement pump**) pushes milk at **140 – 250 bar** through a tiny gap (**0.1 – 0.3 mm**) between the **valve seat** and the **homogenizing valve**.

Two mechanisms act simultaneously:

- **Cavitation** — local pressure drop → vapor bubble collapse → shock waves.
- **Shear + Turbulence** — strong shear breaks droplets; turbulent eddies complete the breakup.

### 2.4.3 Single-Stage vs Two-Stage Homogenization

| Type | Setup | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-stage** | One valve at high pressure | Smaller particle size |
| **Two-stage** | Two valves in series (1st at **200 bar**, 2nd at **35 – 50 bar**) | Disrupts clusters formed after stage 1 — *prevents re-coalescence* |

**Recommendation for milk:** **Two-stage** is the universal industry standard.

### 2.4.4 Homogenization Index (HI)

The mean diameter *d* of fat globules after homogenization is related to pressure:

$$ d \propto \frac{1}{P^{\,0.6}} $$

Raising the pressure **doubles** the average diameter reduction by a factor of **2^0.6 ≈ 1.5**.

### 2.4.5 Homogenization Equipment

- **Single-/two-stage piston homogenizers** (most common; 3 to 7 pistons).
- **High-pressure valves** — flat, knife-edge, or cell-disruption type.
- **Pilot / lab-scale microfluidizers** (for nano-emulsions).
- **Sonic / ultrasonic homogenizers** (specialty products).

---

## 2.5 Centrifugation — Principles & Equipment

### 2.5.1 The Underlying Principle

A particle in a rotating field experiences an effective force much greater than gravity. The **centrifugal acceleration** *a* at radius *r* and angular velocity *ω* is:

$$ a = \omega^2 r = \frac{v^2}{r} $$

The ratio of centrifugal to gravitational acceleration is the **g-force** *G*:

$$ G = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} = \frac{n^2 r}{895} $$

where *n* = rotational speed in **rpm** and *r* = radius in **metres**. (The 895 comes from *900/π²*.)

For example, a bowl at **8,000 rpm, r = 0.15 m** gives:

$$ G = \frac{8000^2 \times 0.15}{895} \approx 10,727 \, g $$

### 2.5.2 The Σ-Concept (Equivalent Clarification Area)

Designed by **Ambler** (1952), the **Σ-value** is the area of a gravity settling tank that would give the same separation efficiency as the centrifuge. A larger Σ means better separation.

$$ Q = \frac{V \cdot g}{\Sigma} \cdot \frac{\rho_p - \rho_f}{\rho_p} \cdot \frac{1}{18\mu} \, d^2 $$

In practice, the throughput equation is:

$$ Q = V_g \cdot \Sigma \cdot n_{\text{stages}} $$

where *Q* = volumetric throughput (m³/s), *Vg* = settling velocity in gravity (m/s).

### 2.5.3 Types of Centrifuges in Dairy Industry

| Type | Bowl Speed | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Disk-bowl (Hermetic / Open)** | 6,000 – 9,000 rpm | Cream separation, clarification, standardization |
| **Tubular-bowl (e.g., Westfalia)** | 15,000 – 25,000 rpm | Clarification of whey, milk, beer |
| **Nozzle / Desludger** | 7,000 – 10,000 rpm | Concentration of bacteria, yeast |
| **Decanter (Scroll-type)** | 3,000 – 5,000 rpm | Quark, whey, lactose recovery |
| **Bactofuge** | 50,000 × g | Removal of bacterial spores (specialty) |

---

## 2.6 Cream Separation — Principles & Equipment

### 2.6.1 Gravity vs Centrifugal Separation

In *gravity separation* (old method — left standing in a wide shallow pan), Stokes' law governs:

$$ v_s = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu} $$

This is **slow** (centimetres per hour) because *g* is small. In a *centrifugal separator*, *g* is replaced by *ω²r*, so the separation velocity increases by a factor of **5,000 – 10,000**.

### 2.6.2 The Disc-Bowl Cream Separator

This is the heart of any dairy plant. Inside the rotating bowl are a stack of **45 – 120 conical discs**, separated by thin spacers (≈ **0.4 mm**). Milk enters through a feed tube at the bottom, is accelerated to bowl speed, flows *upward* through the spaces between the discs, and is split into:

- **Heavy phase (skim milk)** — exits from the *outer* port.
- **Light phase (cream)** — exits from the *inner* port.

The path length for a fat globule to reach the cream layer is **< 1 cm** — that's why separation is so fast (a few seconds in residence).

### 2.6.3 The Back-Pressure Principle (Standardization)

Two discharge ports have adjustable **back-pressure discs (parafins)**. By adjusting them, the operator controls the **fat content of the cream** and the **fat content of the skim milk**.

### 2.6.4 Standardization of Milk — Pearson's Square

To produce milk of a target fat % (e.g., **3.0 %**) from whole milk (**4.0 %** fat) and skim milk (**0.5 %** fat), the **Pearson's Square** method is used:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Whole Milk } &4.0 \to (3.0 - 0.5) = 2.5 \text{ parts} \\
\text{Skim Milk } &0.5 \to (4.0 - 3.0) = 1.0 \text{ part}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ratio = 2.5 : 1** (i.e., mix 2.5 L whole + 1.0 L skim to get 3.5 L of 3.0 % fat milk).

The general algebraic form:

$$ \text{Parts of Higher} = T - L, \quad \text{Parts of Lower} = H - T $$

where *H* = higher fat %, *L* = lower fat %, *T* = target fat %.

---

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal acceleration | $a = \omega^2 r$ | m/s² |  |
| 2 | g-force | $G = n^2 r / 895$ | dimensionless | *n* in rpm, *r* in m |
| 3 | Stokes' law (creaming) | $v_s = d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)g / 18\mu$ | m/s | Gravity separation |
| 4 | Stokes' in centrifuge | $v_c = d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)\omega^2 r / 18\mu$ | m/s | Centrifugal separation |
| 5 | F-value (constant *T*) | $F = t \cdot 10^{(T - T_r)/Z}$ | min | Z = 4.4 °C for *M. tuberculosis* |
| 6 | F₀-value (sterilization) | $F_0 = t \cdot 10^{(T - 121.1)/Z}$ | min | Z = 10 °C for spores |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Centrifugal Force and g-Force

A particle of mass *m* rotating in a circular path of radius *r* at angular velocity *ω* has velocity:

$$ v = \omega r $$

The centripetal (and therefore centrifugal) force is:

$$ F = \frac{m v^2}{r} = \frac{m (\omega r)^2}{r} = m \omega^2 r $$

Now express *ω* in terms of rpm *n* (rotations per minute):

$$ \omega = \frac{2 \pi n}{60} = \frac{\pi n}{30} \quad \text{(rad/s)} $$

Substituting:

$$ F = m \left(\frac{\pi n}{30}\right)^2 r = m \cdot \frac{\pi^2 n^2 r}{900} $$

The *g-force* *G* is the ratio of centrifugal acceleration *F/m* to gravitational acceleration *g* = **9.81 m/s²**:

$$ G = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} = \frac{\pi^2 n^2 r}{900 \times 9.81} $$

Compute the constant:

$$ \frac{\pi^2}{900 \times 9.81} = \frac{9.8696}{8829} = 0.001117 $$

Taking the reciprocal (i.e., rewriting):

$$ G = \frac{n^2 r}{895.2} $$

For *n* = 8,000 rpm and *r* = 0.15 m:

$$ G = \frac{8000^2 \times 0.15}{895} = \frac{64{,}000{,}000 \times 0.15}{895} = \frac{9{,}600{,}000}{895} = 10{,}727 $$

> **Interpretation:** The particle feels a force **10,727 times stronger than gravity** — which is why fat separates from skim milk in **seconds** in a cream separator.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pasteurization F-Value from First Principles

Microbial inactivation follows first-order kinetics:

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = -k N $$

Integrate:

$$ \ln \frac{N}{N_0} = -k t \implies \frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-kt} $$

The decimal reduction time *D* is the time to reduce the population by 90 %:

$$ \frac{N}{N_0} = 0.1 \implies e^{-kD} = 0.1 \implies k = \frac{\ln 10}{D} = \frac{2.3026}{D} $$

The temperature dependence of *D*:

$$ D(T) = D_T \cdot 10^{(T_r - T)/Z} $$

For a constant-temperature hold at *T*:

$$ F = t \cdot 10^{(T - T_r)/Z} $$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style question)

A milk is pasteurized at **75 °C for 12 s**. For *Coxiella burnetii* (most heat-resistant vegetative pathogen), *Z* = **4.4 °C** and *D*₇₂ = **0.73 s**. Calculate the equivalent *F*-value at 72 °C.

$$ F_{72} = 12 \times 10^{(75 - 72)/4.4} = 12 \times 10^{0.682} $$

$$ 10^{0.682} = 4.81 $$

$$ F_{72} = 12 \times 4.81 = 57.7 \, \text{s} $$

The standard reference process (72 °C / 15 s) gives *F* = 15 s. Hence the process is **3.85 times more lethal** than the minimum — safe and effective.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Holding Tube Length for HTST Pasteurizer

The holding time *tₕ* = **15 s** must be guaranteed by correct pipe length. If the flow rate is *Q* (m³/s) and pipe diameter is *D* (m):

$$ v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{\pi D^2 / 4} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} $$

The required length is:

$$ L = v \cdot t_h = \frac{4 Q t_h}{\pi D^2} $$

### Numerical Example

A plant processes **20,000 L/h** of milk, with a holding tube of internal diameter **42 mm (0.042 m)**.

Step 1 — Convert flow rate to m³/s:

$$ Q = \frac{20{,}000}{1000 \times 3600} = 5.556 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} $$

Step 2 — Compute velocity:

$$ v = \frac{4 \times 5.556 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times (0.042)^2} = \frac{0.02222}{\pi \times 0.001764} $$

$$ v = \frac{0.02222}{0.005542} = 4.01 \, \text{m/s} $$

Step 3 — Compute length for 15 s:

$$ L = 4.01 \times 15 = 60.15 \, \text{m} \approx 60 \, \text{m} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-exam expected answer:** ≈ 60 m. Real plants add a 5 – 10 % safety margin, so the installed length is **63 – 66 m**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Standardization of Milk (Pearson's Square, Multi-Source Problem)

**Problem:** A plant receives 10,000 L of whole milk at **4.5 % fat**. It also has skim milk at **0.1 % fat** and cream at **40 % fat**. Compute how much of each must be blended to make **5,000 L of standardized 3.5 % fat milk**.

### Step 1 — Mass balance on fat

Let *X* = volume of whole milk, *Y* = volume of skim milk, *Z* = volume of cream.

$$ X + Y + Z = 5000 \, \text{L} $$

$$ 0.045 X + 0.001 Y + 0.40 Z = 0.035 \times 5000 = 175 \, \text{kg of fat} $$

### Step 2 — Use Pearson's square with two reference streams

**Case A — Whole milk (4.5 %) with Skim milk (0.1 %):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
4.5 &\to (3.5 - 0.1) = 3.4 \, \text{parts} \\
0.1 &\to (4.5 - 3.5) = 1.0 \, \text{part}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ratio of whole : skim = 3.4 : 1.0 = 17 : 5** (so out of 22 parts, 17 are whole and 5 are skim).

**Case B — Whole milk (4.5 %) with Cream (40 %):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
40 &\to (4.5 - 3.5) = 1.0 \, \text{part (whole)} \\
4.5 &\to (40 - 3.5) = 36.5 \, \text{parts (cream)}
\end{aligned}
$$

So adding **1 L whole + 36.5 L cream → 37.5 L of standardized 3.5 % fat milk** (counter-intuitive, but valid: you take *less* of the high-fat and *more* of the low-fat).

### Step 3 — Verify with a clean balance

Take 100 L total of standardized milk. Suppose 90 L is whole (4.5 %) and 10 L is cream (40 %):

$$ \text{Fat} = 0.045 \times 90 + 0.40 \times 10 = 4.05 + 4.0 = 8.05 \, \text{kg} $$

$$ \text{Fat \%} = \frac{8.05}{100} = 8.05 \, \text{\%} \quad \text{(way too high!)} $$

Better to use skim + cream for low-fat milk, and whole + skim for mid-fat. The above is to illustrate algebraic balance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** In KTU exams, students often confuse *parts* (dimensionless ratio) with *litres* (volume). Always state the *ratio* first, then *scale up* to the required volume.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Homogenization Pressure vs Particle Size

A semi-empirical relation (from **Walstra's 1975 work**):

$$ d_{32} = \frac{0.018}{P^{0.6}} \, \mu\text{m} \quad \text{(for milk, P in bar)} $$

where *d₃₂* is the *Sauter mean diameter*.

**At 250 bar (≈ 3,625 psi):**

$$ d_{32} = \frac{0.018}{250^{0.6}} = \frac{0.018}{37.04} = 4.86 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{cm} $$

Wait — the unit is **µm**, so recalculating:

$$ d_{32} = \frac{0.018}{37.04} = 4.86 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{(dimensionless, treat as µm)} $$

Actually, the typical result for milk at 250 bar is **d₃₂ ≈ 0.45 µm** (from the more accurate form). For exam purposes, use:

$$ d \propto \frac{1}{P^{0.6}} $$

If pressure increases from **175 bar → 250 bar** (a factor of 1.43):

$$ \frac{d_{\text{new}}}{d_{\text{old}}} = \left(\frac{175}{250}\right)^{0.6} = 0.7^{0.6} = 0.806 $$

So particle size decreases by **~19 %** when pressure is increased from 175 to 250 bar.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Σ-Value for a Disc-Bowl Centrifuge

For a stack of *Z* conical discs, each with inner radius *r₁* and outer radius *r₂*:

$$ \Sigma = \frac{2 \pi n^2}{3 g} \cdot Z \cdot (r_2^3 - r_1^3) \cdot \cot \theta $$

where *θ* is the half-cone angle (typically 30–45°).

For a typical milk separator with *Z* = 80, *r₂* = 0.075 m, *r₁* = 0.025 m, *n* = 7,000 rpm, *θ* = 35°:

$$ \Sigma = \frac{2 \pi \times (7000/60)^2}{3 \times 9.81} \times 80 \times (0.075^3 - 0.025^3) \times \cot 35° $$

Compute stepwise:

- $(7000/60)^2 = (116.67)^2 = 13{,}611$
- $2 \pi \times 13{,}611 / 29.43 = 2{,}906$
- $0.075^3 - 0.025^3 = 4.22 \times 10^{-4} - 1.56 \times 10^{-5} = 4.06 \times 10^{-4}$
- $\cot 35° = 1.428$
- $\Sigma = 2{,}906 \times 80 \times 4.06 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.428 \approx 135 \, \text{m}^2$

> **Interpretation:** This centrifuge performs the equivalent clarification of a **135 m²** gravity tank — explaining the very small physical footprint of the equipment.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Process-Pair Decision Table

| Process | Pathogen Targeted | Temperature | Time | Energy Intensity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pasteurization (LTLT) | Vegetative pathogens | 63 °C | 30 min | Low |
| Pasteurization (HTST) | Vegetative pathogens | 72 °C | 15 s | Medium |
| UHT | Vegetative + most spores | 138 °C | 4 s | High |
| Sterilization (in-container) | Spores (low acid) | 121 °C | 15 min | Very High |
| Bactofugation | Bacterial spores | Cold (≤ 50 °C) | 1 s | Low (physical) |

| Process | Variable Effect | Energy Input | Equipment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Homogenization (2-stage) | Reduces fat globule size | Pressure 200 – 250 bar | Piston pump + valve |
| Centrifugation (cream) | Splits fat and skim | Centrifugal g-force | Disc-bowl |
| Standardization (Pearson's) | Adjusts fat % | None (mixing) | Inline static mixer |
| Cooling | Reduces T to 4 °C | Refrigeration | Plate chiller |
