# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1: Define Pasteurization. List the time–temperature combinations for LTLT and HTST. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Pasteurization is the process of heating every particle of milk (or other food) to a specific temperature for a definite period of time so as to destroy the pathogenic vegetative microorganisms, without appreciably changing the milk's chemical composition, nutritional value, or sensory properties.
>
> **Time–temperature combinations (internationally accepted):**
>
> | Method | Temperature | Time |
> |---|---|---|
> | **LTLT (Vat / Batch)** | **63 °C** | **30 minutes** |
> | **HTST (Continuous)** | **72 °C** | **15 seconds** |
> | **HHST** | **89 °C** | **1 second** |
> | **UHT** | **138 °C** | **2 – 4 seconds** |
>
> **Control test:** The *alkaline phosphatase test* is used to verify the adequacy of pasteurization. A properly pasteurized milk gives a **negative phosphatase test**, because the enzyme (which is slightly more heat-stable than pathogens) must be inactivated.

### Question 2: Differentiate between Pasteurization and Sterilization. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Pasteurization | Sterilization |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Kill *pathogenic vegetative* bacteria | Kill *all* microorganisms, including *spores* |
| **Temperature** | 63 °C – 72 °C | 121 °C – 138 °C |
| **Time** | 15 s – 30 min | 2 s – 15 min |
| **Shelf life** | 2 – 21 days (refrigerated) | 6 – 9 months (ambient) |
| **Packaging** | Cold chain essential | Aseptic / hermetic |
| **Method** | HTST plate exchanger | UHT or in-container retort |
| **Control test** | Phosphatase | Commercial sterility test |
| **Flavour** | Fresh, raw-like | Slight cooked / caramelized |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **KTU Rule:** In every KTU 2024 module exam, students get an *internal choice* between two questions. We provide both, with full solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): HTST Pasteurization — Components, Working, and Numerical

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the construction and working of an HTST plate heat exchanger pasteurizer. Explain the role of the **flow diversion valve** and the **holding tube**.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A dairy plant processes **15,000 L/h** of milk through an HTST pasteurizer with a holding tube of internal diameter **38 mm**. The holding time must be 15 s.
(i) Calculate the length of the holding tube.
(ii) If the milk is heated from 6 °C to 72 °C in the regeneration and heater sections, calculate the heat required. Take *cₚ* = **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)** and density = **1.032 g/cm³**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The HTST pasteurizer consists of **five functional sections**, all built into a single **plate heat exchanger** (PHE):

1. **Regenerator (inlet side)** — Cold raw milk enters the plates; the outgoing hot pasteurized milk flows in counter-current on the other side of the same plates. Heat transfers from hot to cold. This recovers **80 – 95 %** of the heat.

2. **Final Heater** — Milk is brought from ~50 °C to **72 °C** using hot water (80 – 90 °C) from an external boiler. Temperature is monitored by a sensor in the milk line.

3. **Holding Tube** — A precisely sized, straight length of pipe (no bends) where the milk *must* remain for **exactly 15 s**. The flow is turbulent (Reynolds number > 10,000) so that no short-circuiting occurs.

4. **Regenerator (outlet side)** — Pasteurized milk gives up its heat to incoming raw milk.

5. **Final Cooler** — Pasteurized milk is cooled to **≤ 4 °C** using chilled water and glycol.

**Flow Diversion Valve (FDV):** If the milk temperature *before* the holding tube falls below 72 °C (say due to a heater failure), the FDV **automatically** diverts the milk back to the raw milk balance tank, *not* to the downstream regenerator or cooler. The valve is a **three-way, spring-loaded pneumatically actuated** valve — a HACCP-critical safety device. It is **mandated by FSSAI and Codex Alimentarius**.

**The plates** are pressed from **stainless steel (SS 316)** sheets, with corrugated patterns to induce turbulence. They are gasketed (food-grade NBR or EPDM rubber) and stacked on a frame. The number of plates determines the heat transfer area — typically **5 – 30 m²** for milk plants.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Convert flow rate to m³/s:**

$$ Q = \frac{15{,}000 \, \text{L/h}}{3600 \, \text{s/h} \times 1000 \, \text{L/m}^3} = 4.167 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} $$

**Step 2 — Pipe cross-sectional area:**

$$ D = 38 \, \text{mm} = 0.038 \, \text{m} $$

$$ A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.038)^2}{4} = 1.134 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2 $$

**Step 3 — Velocity in the holding tube:**

$$ v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-3}}{1.134 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.675 \, \text{m/s} $$

**Step 4 — Holding tube length:**

$$ L = v \times t_h = 3.675 \times 15 = 55.13 \, \text{m} $$

> **Answer (i): L ≈ 55.1 m**

**Step 5 — Mass flow rate:**

$$ \dot{m} = \rho \times Q = 1032 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \times 4.167 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 4.30 \, \text{kg/s} $$

**Step 6 — Heat required:**

$$ \dot{Q} = \dot{m} \times c_p \times \Delta T = 4.30 \times 3.93 \times (72 - 6) $$

$$ \dot{Q} = 4.30 \times 3.93 \times 66 = 1115.4 \, \text{kW} $$

> **Answer (ii): Q̇ ≈ 1115 kW (≈ 1.12 MW)**

**Step 7 — Energy per litre** (for the professor's appreciation):

$$ E_{\text{per L}} = \frac{1115.4 \times 3600}{15{,}000} = 267.7 \, \text{kJ per litre} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Homogenization, Centrifugation & Standardization

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the principle of **homogenization** of milk. With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **two-stage homogenizer**. State the standard pressure used.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A dairy receives 5,000 kg of whole milk at **4.0 % fat** and 3,000 kg of skim milk at **0.05 % fat**. How much standardized milk of **3.0 % fat** can be produced? Use Pearson's square.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle:** Milk is a natural emulsion of fat globules (mean diameter **3.5 µm**, ranging from 0.1 to 10 µm) in a watery phase. On standing, fat rises to form a cream layer (creaming). Homogenization reduces the *mean diameter to < 1 µm* and *narrows the size distribution* so that the emulsion is *kinetically stable* — the globules do not cream.

**Mechanism:** Milk is pressurized to **140 – 250 bar** by a **high-pressure, three-piston (or five-piston) positive-displacement pump** and forced through a narrow **annular orifice** (gap ≈ 0.1 – 0.3 mm) between a **homogenizing valve** and a **valve seat** made of tungsten carbide or Stellite. The flow undergoes:

- **Strong shear** in the gap.
- **Turbulent eddies** downstream.
- **Cavitation** due to the sudden pressure drop.

These three together break the fat globules into much smaller droplets, which are then immediately covered by a fresh protein (casein) layer that prevents re-coalescence.

**Two-Stage Construction:**

| Stage | Pressure Setting | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage 1** | **180 – 250 bar** | Primary break-up of fat globules |
| **Stage 2** | **30 – 50 bar** | Disrupts clusters formed in stage-1 wake; smooths the size distribution |

**Standard Pressure for Milk:** **200 – 250 bar (≈ 2,900 – 3,625 psi)**, two-stage.

**Components of a Homogenizer:**

1. **Feed tank** with level control.
2. **Piston pump** (3 or 5 pistons) with replaceable plungers.
3. **Homogenizing head** — contains the valve, seat, and impact ring.
4. **Pressure gauge** and **relief valve** (over-pressure protection).
5. **Outlet** to downstream processing.

> **Advantage:** No cream layer; whiter appearance; richer mouthfeel; better digestibility.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Pearson's Square Setup:**

Streams:
- Whole milk: **4.0 %** fat (high stream)
- Skim milk: **0.05 %** fat (low stream)
- Target: **3.0 %** fat

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Whole milk (4.0)} &\to |3.0 - 0.05| = 2.95 \, \text{parts} \\
\text{Skim milk (0.05)} &\to |4.0 - 3.0| = 1.00 \, \text{part}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ratio of Whole : Skim = 2.95 : 1.00**

Total parts = 2.95 + 1.00 = **3.95 parts**.

**Step 1 — Compute mass per part:**

The available whole milk is 5,000 kg and skim milk is 3,000 kg.

The ratio implies that for every 3.95 parts of blend, 2.95 parts are whole and 1.00 part is skim.

Let's compute the **limiting stream**. The required whole milk fraction = 2.95 / 3.95 = **0.7468**. The available whole milk = **5,000 kg**.

If we use *all* 5,000 kg of whole milk, the blend quantity is:

$$ \text{Blend} = 5000 \times \frac{3.95}{2.95} = 5000 \times 1.3390 = 6695 \, \text{kg} $$

Check skim requirement:

$$ \text{Skim needed} = 5000 \times \frac{1.00}{2.95} = 5000 \times 0.3390 = 1695 \, \text{kg} $$

This is **less than** the 3,000 kg skim available, so whole milk is the **limiting stream** and the calculation is valid.

**Step 2 — Final Answer:**

- Whole milk to use: **5,000 kg** (all of it)
- Skim milk to use: **1,695 kg** (out of 3,000 kg)
- Standardized 3.0 % fat milk produced: **6,695 kg**
- Excess skim milk: 3,000 − 1,695 = **1,305 kg** (can be used for other products)

**Verification — mass balance on fat:**

$$ \text{Fat in} = 5000 \times 0.040 + 1695 \times 0.0005 = 200.0 + 0.85 = 200.85 \, \text{kg} $$

$$ \text{Fat \% in blend} = \frac{200.85}{6695} \times 100 = 3.00 \, \text{\%} \quad \text{✓ Verified} $$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS on this topic — the KTU Examiner's checklist:**
>
> 1. **Not drawing the flow diagram** — Even with a beautiful textual answer, no diagram = **−3 to −4 marks**. Always include a labelled block diagram / Mermaid figure.
> 2. **Confusing the time–temperature combinations** — Writing "72 °C for 30 min" is a **common fatal error**. *HTST is 15 seconds, not 30 minutes.*
> 3. **Forgetting the Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** — In HTST questions, the FDV is a **mandatory mention**. Skipping it costs you 2 marks.
> 4. **Using absolute value |x| inside markdown tables** — Causes the table to break visually. Use parentheses or `\vert` in LaTeX. (Already handled in this note.)
> 5. **Wrong units in derived formulas** — Mixing rpm with rad/s, or bar with psi, without conversion. Always state the units.
> 6. **Skipping the assumption list** — In numerical problems, always write "Assume plug flow, neglect heat loss" etc. at the top.
> 7. **Pearson's square → ratio vs volume** — Many students write "3 parts = 3 litres". The parts are *dimensionless*; you must scale up to a *volume*.
> 8. **Listing the difference between pasteurization and sterilization in a single sentence** — Examiners expect a *table*. Tables always fetch more marks.
> 9. **Missing the "phosphatase test" mention** — It is the regulatory control test for pasteurization, worth **1 mark** in nearly every KTU board exam.
> 10. **No mention of two-stage homogenization in numerical problems** — Always state the *first* and *second* stage pressures separately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Module 2, Unit Operations**

### A. Receiving of Milk
- [x] Six operations: inspection → weighing → sampling → testing → cooling → storage.
- [x] Cooling to **≤ 4 °C** within **2 hours** of milking.
- [x] Platform tests: COB, TA (≤ **0.14 % lactic acid**), sediment, antibiotic.
- [x] Equipment: weigh bowl, plate cooler, raw milk silo.

### B. Pasteurization
- [x] HTST: **72 °C / 15 s** — global standard.
- [x] LTLT: **63 °C / 30 min** — batch.
- [x] UHT: **138 °C / 2 – 4 s** — sterile.
- [x] Five sections of HTST: regenerator → heater → holder → regenerator → cooler.
- [x] Flow Diversion Valve (FDV) is **mandatory** and **safety-critical**.
- [x] **Phosphatase test** is the regulatory control test.
- [x] F-value formula: $F = t \cdot 10^{(T - T_r)/Z}$.

### C. Sterilization
- [x] In-container: **121 °C / 15 min** (F₀).
- [x] UHT: direct (steam injection) vs indirect (PHE).
- [x] Aseptic packaging — Tetra Brik, Combibloc.
- [x] Result: **commercially sterile**, **ambient shelf-stable** product.

### D. Homogenization
- [x] Reduces fat globule size to **< 1 µm**.
- [x] Standard pressure: **200 – 250 bar**.
- [x] **Two-stage** (1st: 200 bar, 2nd: 35 – 50 bar) is industry norm.
- [x] Particle size: $d \propto 1/P^{0.6}$.
- [x] Three-piston / five-piston positive-displacement pump.

### E. Centrifugation
- [x] Centrifugal force: $F = m \omega^2 r$.
- [x] g-force: $G = n^2 r / 895$ (n in rpm, r in m).
- [x] Stokes' law in a centrifuge: $v_c = d^2 \Delta\rho \cdot \omega^2 r / 18\mu$.
- [x] Σ-concept by Ambler: equivalent clarification area.

### F. Cream Separation
- [x] **Disc-bowl** separator with **45 – 120 conical discs**.
- [x] Heavy phase (skim) → outer port; light phase (cream) → inner port.
- [x] **Pearson's Square** for standardization.
- [x] Standard whole milk: **3.5 % fat, 8.5 % SNF**.
- [x] Standardization formula: Parts of H = T − L; Parts of L = H − T.

### G. Numerical Problem-Solving Sequence
1. **Read carefully** — note units (°C vs K, bar vs psi, rpm vs rad/s).
2. **List assumptions** (plug flow, no heat loss, etc.).
3. **Convert everything to SI** (m, s, kg, J) — KTU examiners expect SI.
4. **Show every algebraic step** — no skipping.
5. **Box / underline the final answer** with units.
6. **Verify dimensionally** — if units don't match, redo.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporation: Principles, Types of Evaporators, Steam Economy, Multiple Effect Evaporation, Vapour Recompression. Principles of Drying, Spray Drying, Drum Drying, Freeze Drying, Filtration: Principle, Types of Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT602 - DAIRY AND FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaporation: Principles, Types of Evaporators, Steam Economy, Multiple Effect Evaporation, Vapour Recompression. Principles of Drying, Spray Drying, Drum Drying, Freeze Drying, Filtration: Principle, Types of Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Evaporation – Definition

**Evaporation** is a unit operation in which a volatile solvent (almost always water in dairy/food engineering) is vaporised from a non-volatile solute (milk solids, sugars, salts) by transferring sensible + latent heat to the liquid, thereby concentrating the solution. It is fundamentally a **heat-transfer-limited** operation, not a mass-transfer-limited one.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Evaporation is the removal of water from a liquid food/dairy product by boiling it under controlled conditions so that water leaves as vapour and the dissolved/suspended solids are retained in the concentrate.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine boiling milk in an open pan on the stove. The water disappears as steam and the milk thickens into khoa. An industrial evaporator does the same thing — but inside a sealed metal vessel (calandria), with vacuum to keep temperature low (so milk proteins do not scorch), and with the vapour either thrown away or reused to boil the next batch.

---

### 1.2 Drying – Definition

**Drying** is the removal of moisture (usually water) from a solid, semi-solid, or liquid food product by thermal means, with the removed vapour carried away by a drying medium (hot air). The goal is to reduce water activity ($a_w$) below the threshold that allows microbial growth, thereby improving **shelf life**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Drying is the simultaneous heat and mass transfer operation in which moisture migrates from the interior of a food material to its surface and is then evaporated into an unsaturated hot-air stream.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Hanging wet clothes on a clothesline on a sunny, breezy day. The sun (heat source) evaporates water from the surface, and the breeze (mass transfer medium) carries the vapour away. Industrial drying (spray, drum, freeze) is the controlled, hygienic, and fast version of the same idea.

---

### 1.3 Filtration – Definition

**Filtration** is a mechanical separation operation in which a solid–liquid mixture (slurry) is passed through a porous medium (filter cloth, screen, membrane) that retains the solids and lets the liquid (filtrate) pass through.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Filtration is the separation of suspended solid particles from a fluid by forcing the fluid through a porous medium whose pores are smaller than the particles.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Pouring milky tea through a tea-strainer — the tea passes, but the tea leaves are caught. In a dairy plant, curd particles, casein fines, or whey impurities are caught on a cloth or sintered-metal filter.

---

### 1.4 Standard Process Metrics (must remember)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Dairy) |
|---|---|---|
| Boiling point of water at 1 atm | $T_b$ | **100 °C (373.15 K)** |
| Latent heat of vaporisation of water | $\lambda$ | **2257 kJ/kg** |
| Steam pressure in calandria | $P_s$ | **0.5 – 3.0 bar (g)** |
| Vacuum in evaporator | $P_v$ | **0.05 – 0.3 bar (a)** |
| Steam economy (single effect) | $E_s$ | **0.8 – 1.2 kg water/kg steam** |
| $a_w$ for safe dried food | $a_w$ | **< 0.6** |
| Filter medium pore size | $d_p$ | **1 – 200 µm** |

> [!NOTE]
> All numerical values are deliberately chosen to fall inside the textbook operating windows covered in KTU 2024 PCAGT602 syllabus Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 EVAPORATION — Principles & Operating Modes

Evaporation transfers heat through a metal surface to a boiling liquid. The heat flux is governed by the standard conduction-convection equation:

$$
q = \frac{Q}{A} = U \, \Delta T
$$

where $U$ is the overall heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) and $\Delta T$ is the temperature difference between the heating medium (steam) and the boiling liquid.

**Operating modes:**
- **Atmospheric (open pan):** Simple but heat-sensitive foods scorch.
- **Vacuum evaporation:** Reduces boiling point. Critical for milk, juice, and pharmaceuticals.
- **Forced circulation:** Liquid is pumped through tubes at high velocity to prevent fouling and burn-on.

---

### 2.2 Types of Evaporators (KTU-Focused Classification)

| Type | Construction | Best For | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open Pan** | Wide shallow pan with steam coil | Small dairies, khoa | Low capacity, fouling |
| **Batch/Steam-Jacketed** | Vessel with steam jacket | Viscous products | High labour cost |
| **Short-Tube (Standard Vertical)** | Vertical tube bundle with central downtake | Saline, sugar juice | Prone to scaling |
| **Long-Tube Vertical (LTV) / Rising Film** | 3–10 m long tubes, liquid climbs | Clear juices, milk | Not for viscous liquids |
| **Falling Film** | Liquid pumped to top, falls by gravity | Heat-sensitive, viscous (cream, condensed milk) | Higher pumping cost |
| **Forced Circulation** | External pump circulates slurry | Crystallising, viscous | High power |
| **Agitated Thin-Film (ATFE)** | Rotating blades, conical shell | Highly viscous, paste-like (tomato paste, honey) | Expensive |

---

### 2.3 Steam Economy — Single vs Multiple Effect

**Steam economy ($E_s$)** is defined as:

$$
E_s = \frac{\text{Mass of water evaporated}}{\text{Mass of live steam supplied}}
$$

* **Single-effect evaporator:** $E_s \approx 0.85$ to $1.1$ — almost 1 kg water per kg steam.
* **Why multiple effect?** The vapour boiled off in the **first** effect is reused as the heating medium in the **second** effect (at a lower pressure), and so on.

**Ideal (theoretical) steam economy for an n-effect evaporator:**

$$
E_{s,\,n} \approx n \times E_{s,\,1}
$$

**Capacity (kg water evaporated/hour)** relationship:

$$
C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i = A \, U \, \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta T_i
$$

Since total $\Delta T$ across the system is fixed by the steam temperature and the last-effect pressure, increasing $n$ raises **economy** but lowers **driving force** $\Delta T$ per effect, reducing capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently asks: *"Why does adding more effects beyond a point reduce capacity?"* — Answer: Total $\Delta T$ is fixed; dividing it among more effects reduces $\Delta T_i$, and $U$ also drops in low-pressure effects.

---

### 2.4 Vapour Recompression (Mechanical Vapour Recompression — MVR / Thermal Vapour Recompression — TVR)

Instead of using fresh steam every time, the secondary vapour is **compressed** so that its saturation temperature rises above the boiling liquid, and it is fed back as the heating medium.

$$
W_{comp} = \frac{\dot{m}_v \, (H_{out} - H_{in})}{\eta_{mech}}
$$

- **MVR** uses a mechanical blower/compressor (electrical).
- **TVR** uses a steam-jet ejector (live steam as motive fluid, no moving parts).

**Economy benefit:** $E_s$ can approach **10–30** for MVR — essentially the same steam loop is recycled.

---

### 2.5 DRYING — Principles and Terminology

**Moisture Content definitions** (very high-yield):

**Wet basis (w.b.):**
$$
M_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100\,\%
$$

**Dry basis (d.b.):** (preferred for engineering calculations)

$$
M_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_s}
$$

**Conversion:**
$$
M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}
$$

where $m_w$ = mass of water (kg), $m_s$ = mass of dry solid (kg).

**Two stages of drying:**
1. **Constant-rate period** — surface water evaporates; rate set by external mass/heat transfer (air velocity, temperature, humidity).
2. **Falling-rate period** — internal moisture migrates; rate set by diffusion inside the solid.

**Critical moisture content ($M_c$):** moisture at which the constant rate ends.
**Equilibrium moisture content ($M^*$):** moisture at which drying stops (depends on $a_w$ of the air).

---

### 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET — EVAPORATION, DRYING & FILTRATION

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m_s \, \lambda_s$ | Heat supplied by steam | kJ/h |
| 2 | $C = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_w}$ | Capacity (water evaporated) | kg/h |
| 3 | $E_s = \dfrac{m_w}{m_{steam}}$ | Steam economy | kg/kg |
| 4 | $E_{s,n} \approx n \, E_{s,1}$ | Multiple-effect economy (ideal) | — |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \, \Delta T}$ | Heat-transfer area | m² |
| 6 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}$ | Moisture conversion | kg/kg |
| 7 | $R_c = \alpha \, \dfrac{c \, V}{A_f}$ | Cake resistance | 1/m |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A_f \, \Delta P}{\mu \, (R_m + R_c)}$ | Darcy filtration law | m³/s |
| 9 | $t = \dfrac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{2 \, A_f^2 \, \Delta P} V^2 + \dfrac{\mu \, R_m}{A_f \, \Delta P} V$ | Constant-$\Delta P$ filtration | s |
| 10 | $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{2 \, A_f^2 \, \Delta P} V + \dfrac{\mu \, R_m}{A_f \, \Delta P}$ | Linear plot form (y vs x) | s/m³ |
| 11 | $a_w = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{v}^{sat}(T)}$ | Water activity | dimensionless |
| 12 | $t_d = \dfrac{M_i - M^*}{N_d}$ | Approximate drying time (constant rate) | h |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table above, **never use $\vert$** inside cells. The symbols $\alpha$ (specific cake resistance), $c$ (mass of solids/volume of filtrate), $A_f$ (filter area), $R_m$ (medium resistance), $\mu$ (filtrate viscosity) and $\Delta P$ (pressure drop) are all KTU-defined parameters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation — Material Balance on a Single-Effect Evaporator

**Given:** A feed of $F$ kg/h with $x_F$ mass-fraction solids enters an evaporator. The product (concentrate) leaves at $L$ kg/h with $x_L$ mass-fraction solids. Water evaporated = $V$ kg/h.

**Total mass balance:**
$$
F = L + V
$$

**Solid (non-volatile) balance:** — because solids never leave with the vapour:
$$
F \, x_F = L \, x_L
$$

**Solving simultaneously for $L$ and $V$:**

$$
L = \frac{F \, x_F}{x_L}
$$

$$
V = F - L = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU trick:** if the problem states initial moisture 85 % and final moisture 50 % (w.b.), convert to solids fraction first:
> $x_F = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$; $x_L = 1 - 0.50 = 0.50$. Then plug into the two equations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Steam Required and Steam Economy

**Energy balance (steady state, no heat losses):**
$$
m_s \, \lambda_s + F \, h_F = L \, h_L + V \, H_V
$$

where $h_F, h_L$ are feed and concentrate enthalpies (kJ/kg) and $H_V$ is enthalpy of secondary vapour (kJ/kg). Solving for live-steam mass:

$$
m_s = \frac{L \, h_L + V \, H_V - F \, h_F}{\lambda_s}
$$

**Steam economy** (definition):
$$
E_s = \frac{V}{m_s}
$$

**If feed is at its boiling point** (cold-feed correction = 0):
$$
m_s \approx \frac{V \, \lambda_w}{\lambda_s}
$$

$$
E_s \approx \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_w} \approx \frac{2200}{2260} \approx 0.97
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Multiple-Effect Evaporator Capacity & Economy

For $n$ effects in **forward feed**, each effect operates at a different pressure. The **total available $\Delta T$** is:

$$
\Delta T_{total} = T_{steam,\,1} - T_{last\,effect}
$$

If we (idealise) assume equal $U_i$ and equal area $A$:

$$
\Delta T_i = \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{n}
$$

**Capacity of effect $i$:** (kg water evaporated per hour in effect $i$)
$$
C_i = A \, U_i \, \Delta T_i
$$

**Total capacity:**
$$
C_{total} = A \, \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i \, \Delta T_i
$$

**Total economy (ideal):**
$$
E_{s,n} = n \, E_{s,1}
$$

**Numerical check** — If a single effect has $E_s = 0.95$ and steam consumption = 1000 kg/h, then:

$$
V_{single} = 0.95 \times 1000 = 950 \text{ kg water/h}
$$

For a triple effect (ideal):

$$
E_{s,3} = 3 \times 0.95 = 2.85 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_3 = 2850 \text{ kg water/h}
$$

But total $\Delta T$ is the same, so the capacity per effect drops.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Constant-Pressure Filtration Equation (very high-yield for KTU)

Starting from Darcy's law:
$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A_f \, \Delta P}{\mu \, (R_m + R_c)}
$$

For a cake that grows with filtrate volume, cake resistance:
$$
R_c = \alpha \, \frac{c \, V}{A_f}
$$

Substituting:
$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A_f \, \Delta P}{\mu \left(R_m + \alpha \, c \, V / A_f\right)}
$$

Rearranging:
$$
dt = \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{A_f^2 \, \Delta P} \, V \, dV + \frac{\mu \, R_m}{A_f \, \Delta P} \, dV
$$

Integrating from $V=0$ at $t=0$ to $V=V$ at $t=t$:

$$
t = \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{2 \, A_f^2 \, \Delta P} \, V^2 + \frac{\mu \, R_m}{A_f \, \Delta P} \, V
$$

Dividing both sides by $V$ gives the famous linear form used in the lab:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{2 \, A_f^2 \, \Delta P} \, V \;+\; \frac{\mu \, R_m}{A_f \, \Delta P}\;}
$$

This is a **straight line** when plotted as $t/V$ (y) vs $V$ (x). Slope $= \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A_f^2 \Delta P}$, Intercept $= \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A_f \Delta P}$.

From intercept and slope one finds $\alpha$ and $R_m$ — a classic KTU lab question.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Drying Methods

| Feature | Spray Dryer | Drum Dryer | Freeze Dryer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feed state | Liquid (milk, juice) | Liquid / paste | Frozen solid |
| Heat-transfer mode | Convection with hot air | Conduction through drum surface | Sublimation (latent heat under vacuum) |
| Operating temperature | 150 – 220 °C inlet / 80 – 100 °C outlet | 110 – 160 °C drum surface | −30 to −50 °C shelf |
| Product form | Powder (10 – 200 µm) | Flakes / sheets | Porous cake |
| Residence time | 5 – 30 s | 5 – 30 s | 6 – 24 h |
| Capital cost | High | Low | **Very high** |
| Energy per kg water | ~ 5000 kJ | ~ 3500 kJ | ~ 10000 kJ |
| Quality retention | Good | Moderate (heat damage) | **Excellent** (heat-labile foods, probiotics) |
| Example dairy product | Skim milk powder | Whey powder, baby food | Probiotic cultures, instant coffee, cheese starters |

---

### 3.6 Worked Example — Single-Effect Evaporator (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Feed = 10 000 kg/h of whole milk (12 % total solids, w.b.). Concentrate leaves at 50 % total solids (w.b.). Steam supplied at 1.5 bar (g), $\lambda_s = 2200$ kJ/kg. Assume feed enters at boiling point. Find:
(a) Mass of concentrate
(b) Water evaporated
(c) Steam consumed (ideal)
(d) Steam economy

**Step (a): Convert to solids fraction:**
$x_F = 1 - 0.12 = 0.88$ (wait — solids are 12 %, so $x_F = 0.12$).
$x_L = 1 - 0.50 = 0.50$.

**Step (b): Concentrate mass:**
$$
L = \frac{F \, x_F}{x_L} = \frac{10000 \times 0.12}{0.50} = 2400 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step (c): Water evaporated:**
$$
V = F - L = 10000 - 2400 = 7600 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step (d): Steam consumed** (feed at boiling point, $\lambda_w = 2260$ kJ/kg):
$$
m_s = \frac{V \, \lambda_w}{\lambda_s} = \frac{7600 \times 2260}{2200} = 7807.3 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step (e): Steam economy:**
$$
E_s = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{7600}{7807.3} = 0.973 \text{ kg water / kg steam}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the textbook 0.97 figure we promised earlier. Economy is always **slightly less than 1** for a single effect.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Triple-Effect Forward-Feed Evaporator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedA[Raw Feed F] --> effectA[Effect 1 High Pressure]
    steamA[Live Steam] --> effectA
    effectA --> vapA[Secondary Vapour V1]
    vapA --> effectB[Effect 2 Medium Pressure]
    effectA --> effectB
    effectB --> vapB[Secondary Vapour V2]
    vapB --> effectC[Effect 3 Low Pressure]
    effectB --> effectC
    effectC --> vapC[Secondary Vapour V3 to Condenser]
    effectC --> concA[Concentrate L Out]
    vapC --> condA[Barometric Condenser + Vacuum Pump]
```

**Reading:** Live steam enters Effect 1 → boils feed → vapour V1 enters Effect 2 as the heating medium → vapour V2 enters Effect 3 → final vapour V3 goes to condenser. Each successive effect is at lower pressure, giving a lower boiling point. The concentrate leaves from Effect 3 (forward feed).

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Mechanical Vapour Recompression (MVR) Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedB[Liquid Feed] --> calB[Evaporator Calandria]
    steamB[Live Steam Start-Up] -.-> calB
    calB --> vapBsec[Secondary Vapour]
    vapBsec --> compB[Mechanical Compressor or Steam Ejector]
    compB --> compVap[Superheated Vapour at Higher T]
    compVap --> calB
    calB --> concB[Concentrate Out]
```

**Reading:** Once started, secondary vapour is *compressed* to a higher saturation temperature and re-fed into the calandria. External steam is only needed for *start-up*. This is why MVR can reach $E_s \approx 20 - 30$.

---

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Spray Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedC[Liquid Concentrate] --> atomC[Atomiser Disc or Nozzle]
    airIn[Fresh Air In] --> heaterC[Air Heater Furnace or Steam Coil]
    heaterC --> hotAir[Hot Air 180 to 220 C]
    atomC --> chamberC[Drying Chamber]
    hotAir --> chamberC
    chamberC --> powderC[Powder Collected at Cyclone]
    chamberC --> exhaustC[Moist Exhaust Air to Scrubber]
```

**Reading:** Atomiser disperses feed into a fine spray. Hot air evaporates water in milliseconds. Dried particles fall to the chamber base and are swept to a cyclone for separation.

---

### 4.4 Block Architecture — Double-Drum Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedD[Liquid Feed] --> panD[Feed Pan Between Drums]
    drum1D[Heated Drum 1] --> nipD[Nip Point]
    drum2D[Heated Drum 2] --> nipD
    panD --> nipD
    nipD --> filmD[Thin Film on Drum Surface]
    filmD --> scraperD[Doctor Blade Scraper]
    scraperD --> flakeD[Dried Flakes]
    nipD --> exhaustD[Moist Air to Hood]
```

**Reading:** Liquid feed is held in the pan between two counter-rotating steam-heated drums. At the nip point a thin film transfers to the drum surface, dries within one rotation, and is scraped off as flakes by doctor blades.

---

### 4.5 Block Architecture — Freeze Dryer (Lyophiliser)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    prepE[Prepared Frozen Product] --> shelfE[Heated Shelves Under Vacuum]
    shelfE --> subE[Sublimation of Ice to Vapour]
    subE --> condE[Ice Condenser at Minus 50 C]
    shelfE --> dryE[Dried Porous Cake]
    condE --> pumpE[Vacuum Pump Exhaust]
```

**Reading:** Water is removed by **sublimation** (solid → vapour) under high vacuum and low shelf temperature. The cold condenser traps the water vapour, keeping the chamber pressure low and driving sublimation forward.

---

### 4.6 Filter-Type Decision Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startF{Slurry Character}
    startF -->|Low solids, large vol| pressF[Plate and Frame Filter Press]
    startF -->|Continuous, free filtering| rotF[Rotary Vacuum Drum Filter]
    startF -->|Clear liquid polishing| cartF[Cartridge Filter]
    startF -->|Sterile or bacteria free| memF[Membrane Filter 0.22 Micron]
    startF -->|Large flow, coarse| sandF[Sand or Multimedia Filter]
    startF -->|Cake washing critical| leafF[Leaf Filter Pressure]
```

**Reading:** Filter selection in dairy is driven by solids content, sterility requirement, batch vs continuous operation, and the need to wash the cake (e.g., sugar refining).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **Steam Economy** of an evaporator. What is the ideal steam economy of a triple-effect evaporator if the single-effect economy is 0.95?

**Model Answer:**
Steam economy of an evaporator is defined as the mass of water evaporated per unit mass of live steam supplied to the first effect:
$$
E_s = \frac{m_w}{m_{steam}}
$$

For an ideal $n$-effect evaporator with single-effect economy $E_{s,1}$:
$$
E_{s,n} = n \times E_{s,1}
$$

For triple effect:
$$
E_{s,3} = 3 \times 0.95 = \mathbf{2.85 \; kg \; water \; per \; kg \; steam}
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Distinguish between **moisture content on wet basis** and **moisture content on dry basis**. A powder has 30 % moisture (w.b.). Find its moisture content on dry basis.

**Model Answer:**
- **Wet basis (w.b.):** mass of water divided by total mass of wet material.
$$
M_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s}
$$
- **Dry basis (d.b.):** mass of water divided by mass of dry solid.
$$
M_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_s}
$$

Given $M_{wb} = 0.30$. Conversion:
$$
M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}} = \frac{0.30}{0.70} = \mathbf{0.4286 \; kg \; water / kg \; dry \; solid}
$$

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a **Mechanical Vapour Recompression (MVR) evaporator**. Why is its steam economy very high? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **single-effect evaporator** concentrates 10 000 kg/h of tomato juice from 5 % to 30 % total solids (w.b.). The feed enters at 30 °C. Steam is supplied at 1.5 bar (g) with $\lambda_s = 2200$ kJ/kg. Specific heat of juice = 3.9 kJ/kg·K. Boiling point of juice = 105 °C. Find:
(i) Mass of concentrate and water evaporated
(ii) Steam consumed per hour
(iii) Steam economy
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
1. Feed enters the calandria where it is heated by steam (start-up) or by compressed secondary vapour (steady-state).
2. Water boils off at low pressure; secondary vapour leaves the top of the vessel.
3. The secondary vapour is sucked into a **mechanical compressor / blower**.
4. Compression raises its saturation temperature by ~10–15 °C, making it hotter than the boiling liquid.
5. The compressed vapour returns to the calandria as the heating medium.
6. **Why high economy?** Because the same water molecules are recycled as the heating medium — only the make-up steam (and the small compressor work input) are needed. Theoretical economy can exceed 20.

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Convert w.b. to solids fraction:
$x_F = 0.05$, $x_L = 0.30$, $F = 10000$ kg/h.

Step 2 — Concentrate mass:
$$
L = \frac{F \, x_F}{x_L} = \frac{10000 \times 0.05}{0.30} = \mathbf{1666.67 \; kg/h}
$$

Step 3 — Water evaporated:
$$
V = F - L = 10000 - 1666.67 = \mathbf{8333.33 \; kg/h}
$$

Step 4 — Heat required to raise feed to boiling point + vapour enthalpy:
$$
Q_{heat\,to\,bp} = F \, C_p \, (T_{bp} - T_{feed}) = 10000 \times 3.9 \times (105 - 30) = 2.925 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

$$
Q_{vap} = V \, \lambda_w = 8333.33 \times 2260 = 1.8833 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 2.925 \times 10^6 + 1.8833 \times 10^7 = 2.1758 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

Step 5 — Steam consumed:
$$
m_s = \frac{Q_{total}}{\lambda_s} = \frac{2.1758 \times 10^7}{2200} = \mathbf{9890 \; kg/h}
$$

Step 6 — Steam economy:
$$
E_s = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{8333.33}{9890} = \mathbf{0.843 \; kg/kg}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students forget the **cold-feed correction term** ($F \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$). They simply divide $V \cdot \lambda_w / \lambda_s$ and get a high steam economy (around 0.97). Full marks only when both sensible heating **and** latent heat are accounted for. Mark deduction: **−2 marks**.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram explain the construction and working of a **Spray Dryer**. List four dairy products dried by spray drying. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **constant-pressure filtration equation**:
$$
\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{2 \, A_f^2 \, \Delta P} V + \frac{\mu \, R_m}{A_f \, \Delta P}
$$
starting from Darcy's law. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Spray Dryer**
1. **Air system** — Fresh air is drawn in through a filter, passed through a heater (steam coil or gas furnace) raising it to 180–220 °C, and enters the drying chamber tangentially.
2. **Feed system** — Liquid concentrate is pumped to a high-speed centrifugal **atomiser disc** (or a pressure nozzle) rotating at 10 000 – 25 000 rpm, producing droplets of 10–200 µm.
3. **Drying chamber** — Hot air and atomised droplets mix; droplets lose water in 5–30 seconds; surface moisture evaporates in the constant-rate period.
4. **Product recovery** — Dried powder leaves the chamber bottom and is separated from the moist exhaust air by a **cyclone separator** (and optionally a bag filter).
5. **Exhaust** — Moist air passes through a scrubber before discharge.

**Four dairy products:** Skim milk powder, whole milk powder, whey powder, infant formula, cheese powder.

**Part (b): Filtration Derivation**

Start with Darcy's law (refer Section 3.4 for the full step-by-step derivation):

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A_f \, \Delta P}{\mu \, (R_m + R_c)}
$$

For incompressible cake growing with filtrate volume:
$$
R_c = \alpha \, \frac{c \, V}{A_f}
$$

Substitute and rearrange:
$$
dt = \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{A_f^2 \, \Delta P} \, V \, dV + \frac{\mu \, R_m}{A_f \, \Delta P} \, dV
$$

Integrate from $0 \to V$ and $0 \to t$:
$$
t = \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{2 \, A_f^2 \, \Delta P} V^2 + \frac{\mu \, R_m}{A_f \, \Delta P} V
$$

Divide through by $V$:
$$
\boxed{\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{2 \, A_f^2 \, \Delta P} V + \frac{\mu \, R_m}{A_f \, \Delta P}}
$$

This is a straight line of slope $\dfrac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{2 A_f^2 \Delta P}$ and intercept $\dfrac{\mu \, R_m}{A_f \Delta P}$, used in lab to find $\alpha$ and $R_m$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently drop the factor **2** in the denominator. Show it explicitly — it comes from integrating $V \, dV$. Missing the 2 costs **−3 marks**.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Evaporation = heat-transfer-limited**, not mass-transfer-limited.
- [x] **Boiling-point elevation** in foods causes loss of available $\Delta T$ in the last effect.
- [x] **Steam economy (single effect) ≈ 0.85 – 1.1**; **(ideal n-effect) = n × single**.
- [x] **MVR/TVR** give the **highest economy** (10–30).
- [x] **Moisture content**: always convert between w.b. and d.b. before mass-balance calculations.
- [x] **Two drying periods**: constant rate (surface-controlled) and falling rate (diffusion-controlled).
- [x] **Spray dryer** → powder, short residence, ideal for heat-sensitive liquids (milk).
- [x] **Drum dryer** → flakes, conduction heating, cheap but more heat damage.
- [x] **Freeze dryer** → best quality, sublimation, very expensive, used for probiotics and starter cultures.
- [x] **Darcy's law** combined with **cake resistance $R_c = \alpha c V / A_f$** gives the **constant-pressure filtration equation** in its linear $t/V$ vs $V$ form.
- [x] **Filter selection** in dairy: rotary vacuum drum for milk; cartridge/membrane for sterilisation; plate and frame for whey clarification.
- [x] **Energy per kg water removed** is the right KPI for comparing evaporator and dryer efficiency.
- [x] For multiple-effect evaporators, **$\Delta T$ is fixed** — increasing $n$ beyond ~4–5 gives diminishing returns in capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** For any mass/energy-balance problem in this module, ALWAYS write the assumptions first (steady state, no heat losses, feed at boiling point — or not), then total mass balance, then solids balance, then energy balance. KTU examiners award method marks generously when the steps are shown in this order.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Membrane Separation, RO, Nano-Filtration, Ultra Filtration and Macro-Filtration, Equipment and Applications - Dairy Plant Design and Layout - CIP, Dairy Effluent Treatment Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT602 - DAIRY AND FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Membrane Separation, RO, Nano-Filtration, Ultra Filtration and Macro-Filtration, Equipment and Applications - Dairy Plant Design and Layout - CIP, Dairy Effluent Treatment Plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Membrane Separation: The Selective Sieve Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology):**
> *Membrane separation is a pressure-driven (or concentration-driven) unit operation in which a semi-permeable membrane acts as a selective barrier, allowing certain species (permeate) to pass through while rejecting others (retentate or concentrate), based on size, charge, or chemical affinity.*

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a **coffee filter paper** placed in a funnel. When you pour coffee mixture through it:
- **Water + dissolved coffee flavor** → passes through (becomes *permeate*)
- **Coffee grounds** → retained (becomes *retentate*)

A membrane works on the same principle but at a microscopic level — the "holes" (pores) are engineered at nano/micro scale to separate molecules like **water, salts, lactose, proteins, fats, and bacteria** in milk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Driving Forces in Membrane Separation:**
> 1. **Pressure difference (ΔP)** — dominant in MF, UF, NF, RO
> 2. **Concentration difference (ΔC)** — dominant in Pervaporation, Dialysis
> 3. **Electrical potential (ΔV)** — dominant in Electrodialysis
> 4. **Temperature difference (ΔT)** — dominant in Membrane Distillation

---

### 1.2 Classification of Pressure-Driven Membrane Processes

| Membrane Process | Pore Size | Operating Pressure | What it Removes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | **0.1 – 10 μm** | < 2 bar | Bacteria, suspended solids, fat globules |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | **0.001 – 0.1 μm** (1 – 100 nm) | 1 – 10 bar | Proteins, lactose, large colloids |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | **0.0005 – 0.005 μm** (0.5 – 5 nm) | 5 – 35 bar | Divalent ions (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺), lactose, partial salts |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | < 0.001 μm (non-porous) | 15 – 80 bar | Monovalent ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻), water purification |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** As you go from **MF → UF → NF → RO**, the pores get **smaller**, but the **pressure required gets higher**. RO is "reverse" because natural osmosis wants to go the other way!

---

### 1.3 Dairy Plant Design & Layout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> *Dairy plant design is the systematic planning of equipment, piping, building, and process flow to ensure hygienic, efficient, and safe processing of milk and milk products, complying with FSSAI/BIS/FDA standards.*

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a **hospital operation theater** — entry is restricted, you follow a unidirectional path (soiled → clean → sterile), walls are smooth for cleaning, and there are washing stations at every transition. A dairy plant is designed with the **same philosophy** to prevent contamination of milk.

---

### 1.4 Cleaning-In-Place (CIP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU):**
> *CIP is a method of cleaning the interior surfaces of process equipment, pipes, and fittings without disassembly, using circulated cleaning solutions (water, caustic, acid, sanitizers) under controlled conditions of temperature, concentration, time, and flow velocity.*

**Analogy:**
Like brushing your teeth — you don't dismantle your jaw to clean it. You circulate water and toothpaste inside your mouth. CIP does the same for tanks and pipelines!

---

### 1.5 Dairy Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> *A Dairy ETP is an integrated wastewater treatment facility designed to reduce the high BOD, COD, fat, and suspended solids content of dairy effluents to permissible discharge limits (BOD < 30 mg/L, COD < 250 mg/L as per CPCB norms).*

**Analogy:**
A dairy plant is like a human body — after processing, it generates "waste" (effluent) that cannot be dumped raw into the environment, just like our body needs kidneys to filter blood before excretion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Solution-Diffusion vs. Sieve Mechanism

- **Microporous membranes (MF, UF):** Separation is dominated by the **sieve mechanism** — particles larger than the pore are rejected physically.
- **Dense membranes (RO, NF):** Separation follows the **Solution-Diffusion model**:
  1. Component dissolves into membrane surface
  2. Diffuses through the membrane under chemical potential gradient
  3. Desorbs at the permeate side

> [!NOTE]
> The driving force for solvent flux in RO is the **net pressure differential**: $\Delta P - \Delta \pi$, where $\pi$ is the osmotic pressure of the feed.

---

### 2.2 Concentration Polarization (Critical Concept!)

When feed flows over the membrane, rejected solutes accumulate near the surface, forming a **boundary layer** of higher concentration than the bulk feed. This phenomenon is **Concentration Polarization (CP)**.

**Effects of CP:**
- ↓ Effective flux
- ↑ Apparent rejection (in some cases) but ↓ selectivity in real terms
- ↑ Membrane fouling
- ↑ Energy consumption

**Concentration Polarization Model (Film Theory):**

$$\frac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\left(\frac{J}{k}\right)$$

Where:
- $C_w$ = solute concentration at membrane wall
- $C_p$ = solute concentration in permeate
- $C_b$ = solute concentration in bulk feed
- $J$ = permeate flux
- $k$ = mass transfer coefficient

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Don't confuse **Concentration Polarization** (reversible) with **Membrane Fouling** (irreversible). CP can be reduced by increasing cross-flow velocity; fouling requires chemical cleaning.

---

### 2.3 Membrane Module Configurations

| Module Type | Surface Area / Volume | Application in Dairy |
|---|---|---|
| **Plate & Frame** | Low to Medium | Small-scale UF of milk, whey |
| **Spiral Wound** | High | RO for whey concentration, water treatment |
| **Hollow Fiber** | Very High | RO for water, UF for milk |
| **Tubular** | Low | Highly viscous fluids like cheese whey |

---

### 2.4 CIP System Design — The 5-Stage Sequence

A standard CIP cycle follows this sequence:

| Stage | Solution | Temperature | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. **Pre-rinse** | Water (recovery) | 40 – 50 °C | 10 min |
| 2. **Caustic wash** | 1 – 3% NaOH | 70 – 85 °C | 20 – 30 min |
| 3. **Intermediate rinse** | Water | 50 °C | 5 min |
| 4. **Acid wash** | 0.5 – 1% HNO₃ | 50 – 70 °C | 15 min |
| 5. **Final rinse + Sanitize** | Hot water / sanitizer | 80 – 90 °C | 10 min |

**Sinner's Circle** — Four Cleaning Variables (must be balanced):

```
                TEMPERATURE
                    ▲
                   /|\
                  / | \
                 /  |  \
                /   |   \
               /    |    \
              /     |     \
             /      |      \
            /       |       \
           ◄────────|────────►
        CHEMICAL  MECHANICAL  TIME
        ACTION    ACTION
```

> [!TIP]
> **Sinner's Circle Rule:** If you reduce one parameter (e.g., temperature), you must increase one or more of the others to maintain cleaning efficiency.

---

### 2.5 Dairy Effluent Characteristics

| Parameter | Typical Value in Raw Dairy Effluent |
|---|---|
| **pH** | 6.5 – 9.5 |
| **BOD₅** | **1,000 – 3,000 mg/L** |
| **COD** | 2,000 – 5,000 mg/L |
| **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** | 300 – 1,500 mg/L |
| **Oil & Grease** | 100 – 500 mg/L |
| **Total Nitrogen** | 50 – 200 mg/L |
| **Volume** | 0.5 – 3 L effluent per L milk processed |

---

### 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{V_p}{A \cdot t}$ | Permeate Flux | L/(m²·h) or LMH |
| 2 | $J = L_p \left(\Delta P - \Delta \pi \right)$ | Solvent Flux (RO/NF) — Solution-Diffusion | LMH |
| 3 | $R = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100$ | Rejection % (Intrinsic/Apparent) | % |
| 4 | $Y = \dfrac{V_p}{V_f}$ | Recovery / Yield | fraction (0–1) |
| 5 | $C_p = \dfrac{C_f \cdot V_f - C_r \cdot V_r}{V_p}$ | Mass balance for concentration | g/L |
| 6 | $\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)$ | Concentration Polarization (Film Theory) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$ | Van't Hoff Equation for Osmotic Pressure | Pa |
| 8 | $R_m = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot J}$ | Membrane Hydraulic Resistance | m⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\text{F/M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_o}{V \cdot X}$ | Food-to-Microorganism Ratio (Activated Sludge) | day⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f}{P}$ | BOD₅ Dilution Method | mg/L |
| 11 | $Q_{CIP} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{t_{CIP}} \times N$ | CIP Circulation Flow Rate | m³/h |
| 12 | $A_{membrane} = \dfrac{V_{permeate}}{J \cdot t}$ | Required Membrane Area | m² |

**Notation Legend:**
- $J$ = permeate flux, $V_p$ = permeate volume, $A$ = membrane area, $t$ = time
- $L_p$ = membrane permeability, $\Delta P$ = transmembrane pressure, $\Delta \pi$ = osmotic pressure difference
- $C_p, C_f, C_r$ = permeate, feed, retentate concentrations
- $i$ = Van't Hoff factor, $R$ = gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature
- $Q$ = flow rate, $S_o$ = influent BOD, $V$ = aeration tank volume, $X$ = MLSS
- $D_1, D_2$ = DO of sample (initial, after 5 days), $B_1, B_2$ = DO of blank, $f$ = dilution factor, $P$ = decimal fraction of sample

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RO** in dairy: Concentrating whey from 6% to 25% total solids before evaporation → saves **60–70% evaporator energy**.
- **UF** in cheese-making: Pre-concentrating milk 2× before coagulation → increases cheese yield by **10–15%**.
- **CIP**: Reduces cleaning time by **80%** and water consumption by **40%** compared to manual cleaning.
- **ETP**: Mandatory under **CPCB/SPCB** regulations; non-compliance attracts penalties and closure notices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Permeate Flux Equation (Solution-Diffusion Model)

**Step 1 — Define chemical potential of water across the membrane:**

Water moves from feed side (high chemical potential) to permeate side (low chemical potential). The flux is proportional to the chemical potential gradient:

$$J_w = -P_w \cdot \frac{d\mu_w}{dx}$$

Where $P_w$ is the water permeability constant of the membrane.

**Step 2 — Express the chemical potential in terms of pressure and concentration:**

For an ideal dilute solution:

$$\mu_w = \mu_w^0 + RT \ln a_w + v_w \cdot P$$

The activity of water $a_w$ is related to osmotic pressure $\pi$ via:

$$\ln a_w = -\frac{v_w \pi}{RT}$$

(where $v_w$ is the partial molar volume of water)

**Step 3 — Substitute and integrate across the membrane of thickness $\delta$:**

$$\Delta \mu_w = v_w(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$

So:

$$J_w = \frac{P_w \cdot v_w}{\delta} (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$

**Step 4 — Combine constants into a single permeability coefficient $L_p$:**

$$L_p = \frac{P_w \cdot v_w}{\delta}$$

**Step 5 — Final governing equation:**

$$\boxed{J_w = L_p \left( \Delta P - \Delta \pi \right)}$$

This is the **fundamental flux equation** used in RO and NF design calculations in dairy engineering.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Concentration at Membrane Wall (Film Theory)

**Step 1 — Mass balance over a differential element of thickness $dz$:**

At steady state, the convective transport of solute toward the membrane equals the sum of solute permeating through + solute diffusing back into the bulk:

$$J \cdot C = J \cdot C_p - D \cdot \frac{dC}{dz}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from bulk ($z = 0, C = C_b$) to wall ($z = \delta, C = C_w$):**

$$J \cdot (C_b - C_p) \cdot dz = -D \cdot dC$$

$$\int_0^\delta J \, dz = \int_{C_b}^{C_w} \frac{-D \, dC}{C - C_p}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals (assuming $D$ and $J$ are constant):**

$$J \cdot \delta = D \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_b - C_p}{C_w - C_p}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Introduce mass transfer coefficient $k = D/\delta$:**

$$J = k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_b - C_p}{C_w - C_p}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to express $C_w$ explicitly:**

$$\frac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\left(\frac{J}{k}\right)$$

$$\boxed{C_w = C_p + (C_b - C_p) \exp\left(\frac{J}{k}\right)}$$

This equation shows that as flux $J$ increases, $C_w$ increases exponentially — leading to fouling and reduced selectivity.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — UF of Cheese Whey

> **Problem:** A dairy plant processes cheese whey using UF to recover protein. Given:
> - Feed flow rate $V_f = 10{,}000$ L/h
> - Feed protein concentration $C_f = 6.5$ g/L
> - Membrane rejection $R = 98\%$
> - Desired permeate protein concentration $C_p = 0.13$ g/L
> - Flux $J = 50$ LMH
> - Required operating time per day = 20 h
> - Target recovery $Y = 0.80$
>
> **Find:** (a) Volume of permeate per day, (b) Required membrane area, (c) Concentration of protein in retentate.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Part (a) — Volume of permeate per day:**

$$V_p = Y \cdot V_f \cdot t = 0.80 \times 10{,}000 \times 20$$

$$\boxed{V_p = 160{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 160 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}$$

**Part (b) — Required membrane area:**

$$A_{mem} = \frac{V_p}{J \cdot t} = \frac{160{,}000}{50 \times 20}$$

$$\boxed{A_{mem} = 160 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Part (c) — Concentration in retentate using mass balance:**

Total protein balance: $C_f \cdot V_f = C_p \cdot V_p + C_r \cdot V_r$

$$V_r = V_f - V_p = 10{,}000 \times 20 - 160{,}000 = 40{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$

$$C_r = \frac{C_f \cdot V_f - C_p \cdot V_p}{V_r}$$

$$C_r = \frac{(6.5 \times 200{,}000) - (0.13 \times 160{,}000)}{40{,}000}$$

$$C_r = \frac{1{,}300{,}000 - 20{,}800}{40{,}000} = \frac{1{,}279{,}200}{40{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{C_r = 31.98 \text{ g/L} \approx 32 \text{ g/L}}$$

So the UF concentrates whey protein from 6.5 g/L to 32 g/L — a **~5× concentration**.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — BOD₅ Determination

> **Problem:** A dairy effluent sample is diluted: 10 mL sample + 290 mL dilution water. Initial DO = 8.5 mg/L; DO after 5 days = 3.0 mg/L. Blank DO initial = 8.5 mg/L; blank DO final = 8.4 mg/L.
>
> **Find:** BOD₅ of the effluent.

**Solution:**

Dilution factor: $f = \dfrac{V_{sample}}{V_{total}} = \dfrac{10}{300} = \dfrac{1}{30}$

Decimal fraction: $P = \dfrac{1}{30} = 0.0333$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f}{P}$$

$$= \frac{(8.5 - 3.0) - (8.5 - 8.4) \cdot (1/30)}{1/30}$$

$$= \frac{5.5 - 0.1 \times 0.0333}{0.0333}$$

$$= \frac{5.5 - 0.00333}{0.0333} = \frac{5.4967}{0.0333}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 165.1 \text{ mg/L}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Membrane Process for Dairy Applications

| Dairy Product / Application | Target Component | Recommended Membrane | Rejection % | Recovery % |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cheese whey protein concentration | Whey Protein (β-lg, α-la) | **UF** (5–10 kDa MWCO) | > 98 | 90–95 |
| Lactose concentration from whey | Lactose | **NF** (200–400 Da) | 90–95 | 80–90 |
| Demineralized whey | Salts (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻) | **NF + ED** combined | 70–85 | 70–80 |
| Pre-concentration of milk for cheese | Water + lactose | **RO** | 99 | 80–85 |
| Milk standardization (protein) | Casein | **UF** | > 99 | 85–90 |
| Bacteria removal in raw milk | Bacteria, spores | **MF** (0.8 – 1.4 μm) | > 99.9 (LRV > 3) | 95–99 |
| Effluent water reuse | Dissolved solids | **RO** | 99 | 60–75 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Membrane Process Spectrum — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feed[Raw Milk or Whey Feed] --> common{Membrane Process Selection Based on Molecular Size}

    subgraph A1["MICROFILTRATION MF"]
        mfIn[Suspension Feed] --> mfMem[Membrane 0.1 to 10 micron]
        mfMem --> mfPer[Permeate: Water plus Solutes]
        mfMem --> mfRet[Retentate: Bacteria, Fat, Cells]
    end

    subgraph A2["ULTRAFILTRATION UF"]
        ufIn[Colloidal Feed] --> ufMem[Membrane 1 to 100 nm]
        ufMem --> ufPer[Permeate: Lactose plus Salts plus Water]
        ufMem --> ufRet[Retentate: Proteins, Fats]
    end

    subgraph A3["NANOFILTRATION NF"]
        nfIn[Solution Feed] --> nfMem[Membrane 0.5 to 5 nm]
        nfMem --> nfPer[Permeate: Monovalent Ions plus Water]
        nfMem --> nfRet[Retentate: Lactose, Divalent Salts]
    end

    subgraph A4["REVERSE OSMOSIS RO"]
        roIn[Dilute Solution] --> roMem[Dense Membrane less than 1 nm]
        roMem --> roPer[Permeate: Pure Water]
        roMem --> roRet[Retentate: All Solutes Concentrated]
    end

    common --> A1
    common --> A2
    common --> A3
    common --> A4
```

---

### 4.2 CIP System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[Raw Water Source] --> storage[Water Storage Tank]
    storage --> heater[Plate Heat Exchanger Heater]

    chem1[Caustic Soda 2 percent NaOH] --> tank1[Caustic Tank with Concentration Sensor]
    chem2[Nitric Acid 1 percent HNO3] --> tank2[Acid Tank with Concentration Sensor]
    chem3[Sanitizer Chlorine or Hot Water] --> tank3[Sanitizer Tank]

    tank1 --> manifold[Central CIP Manifold Three Way Valves]
    tank2 --> manifold
    tank3 --> manifold
    heater --> manifold

    manifold --> loop1[CIP Loop 1 Process Line and Tanks]
    manifold --> loop2[CIP Loop 2 HTST Pasteurizer]
    manifold --> loop3[CIP Loop 3 Membrane Plant]
    manifold --> loop4[CIP Loop 4 Packaging Line]

    loop1 --> return[Return Line Conductivity and Turbidity Sensors]
    loop2 --> return
    loop3 --> return
    loop4 --> return

    return --> control[PLC and SCADA Control Unit]
    control --> manifold

    control --> display[Operator HMI Display]
    control --> alarm[Alarm and Data Logger]
```

---

### 4.3 Dairy Effluent Treatment Plant — Process Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inlet[Dairy Effluent Inlet] --> screen[Bar Screen and Grit Chamber]

    screen --> oil[Fat Trap Oil and Grease Removal]

    oil --> eq1[Equalization Tank with pH and Flow Control]

    eq1 --> primary1[Primary Clarifier Removes 50 to 70 percent TSS]

    primary1 --> bio1[Aeration Tank Activated Sludge F/M equals 0.2 to 0.5]

    bio1 --> secondary[Secondary Clarifier Biomass Separation]

    secondary --> sludge1[Sludge to Sludge Digester]

    secondary --> disinf[Chlorination or UV Disinfection]

    disinf --> polish[Tertiary Polishing Sand Filter plus Activated Carbon]

    polish --> reusetank[Reuse Tank for Gardening and Flushing]

    sludge1 --> biogas[Biogas Generation Anaerobic Digester]
    biogas --> sludge2[Dried Sludge Cake for Manure]

    reusetank --> outlet[Treated Effluent to Discharge BOD less than 30 mg per L]

    style inlet fill:#FFE4B5
    style outlet fill:#90EE90
    style biogas fill:#FFD700
```

---

### 4.4 Dairy Plant Layout — Functional Zoning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Z1["RECEIVING ZONE High Care"]
        z1a[Weigh Bridge]
        z1b[Raw Milk Chilling Unit]
        z1c[Quality Testing Lab]
    end

    subgraph Z2["PROCESSING ZONE Medium Care"]
        z2a[Standardization and Separation]
        z2b[HTST Pasteurizer]
        z2c[Homogenizer]
        z2d[Storage Tanks]
    end

    subgraph Z3["VALUE ADDED ZONE High Care"]
        z3a[CIP Station]
        z3b[Cheese Vat]
        z3c[Fermentation Tanks]
        z3d[Membrane Plant UF and RO]
    end

    subgraph Z4["PACKAGING ZONE High Care"]
        z4a[Filling Machines]
        z4b[Metal Detector]
        z4c[Storage Cold Room]
    end

    subgraph Z5["AUXILIARY ZONE Low Care"]
        z5a[Boiler Room]
        z5b[Refrigeration Compressor]
        z5c[ETP and Effluent Yard]
        z5d[Admin Office]
    end

    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2 --> Z3
    Z3 --> Z4
    Z5 -.Utilities and Support.-> Z1
    Z5 -.Utilities and Support.-> Z2
    Z5 -.Utilities and Support.-> Z3
    Z5 -.Utilities and Support.-> Z4
```

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Distinguish between Microfiltration (MF), Ultrafiltration (UF), Nanofiltration (NF) and Reverse Osmosis (RO) based on pore size, operating pressure and the species they reject.**

**Model Answer:**

| Process | Pore Size | Operating Pressure | Species Rejected |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MF** | 0.1 – 10 μm | 0.1 – 2 bar | Bacteria, suspended solids, fat globules |
| **UF** | 1 – 100 nm | 1 – 10 bar | Proteins, macromolecules, colloids |
| **NF** | 0.5 – 5 nm | 5 – 35 bar | Divalent salts, lactose, partial monovalents |
| **RO** | < 1 nm (dense) | 15 – 80 bar | Monovalent salts, all dissolved solids |

As we move from MF → RO, pore size decreases and operating pressure increases.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
**List the four cleaning variables of Sinner's Circle and explain their significance in CIP.**

**Model Answer:**

The four cleaning variables of Sinner's Circle are:

1. **Temperature** — Higher temperature lowers surface tension and improves chemical reaction rate.
2. **Chemical Action** — Concentration of detergents (NaOH for organic soil, HNO₃ for mineral scale).
3. **Mechanical Action** — Flow velocity (typically 1.5 – 2.5 m/s) to impart shear and remove deposits.
4. **Time** — Duration of contact between cleaning solution and soil.

All four must be balanced; reducing one requires compensating increase in another to maintain cleaning efficiency.

---

### PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the principle, equipment and applications of **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** in the dairy industry. Derive the governing flux equation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dairy plant operates a **UF system** for cheese whey protein concentration. The feed flow rate is 8,000 L/h, feed protein concentration is 7.0 g/L, flux is 45 LMH, rejection is 99%, and recovery is 85%. The plant operates 18 hours/day. Calculate: (i) Permeate volume per day, (ii) Membrane area required, (iii) Retentate concentration.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — RO Principle, Equipment & Applications

**Principle:**
RO works on the principle of reversing natural osmosis by applying hydraulic pressure greater than the osmotic pressure of the feed solution, forcing solvent (water) through a dense semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher solute concentration to lower solute concentration.

**Governing Flux Equation (Derivation):**

Based on solution-diffusion model, the chemical potential of water across the membrane is:

$$\mu_w = \mu_w^0 + RT \ln a_w + v_w P$$

For an ideal dilute solution:

$$\ln a_w = -\frac{v_w \pi}{RT}$$

The driving force across membrane thickness $\delta$:

$$\Delta \mu_w = v_w (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$

Water flux is proportional to the gradient:

$$J_w = \frac{P_w \cdot v_w}{\delta} \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$

Combining constants into membrane permeability $L_p$:

$$\boxed{J_w = L_p (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}$$

**Equipment:**
- High-pressure pump (multi-stage centrifugal, up to 80 bar)
- RO membrane module (spiral wound most common)
- Energy recovery device (Pelton wheel or isobaric exchanger)
- Pressure vessels and high-pressure piping
- Pretreatment system (cartridge filter, antiscalant dosing)

**Applications in Dairy:**
1. Concentration of cheese whey from 6% to 20–25% TS before evaporation → **energy saving up to 70%**.
2. Concentration of skim milk for transport reduction.
3. Demineralization pre-step when combined with ion exchange.
4. Effluent water recovery and reuse in CIP.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — UF Numerical

**Given:**
- $V_f = 8{,}000$ L/h, $C_f = 7.0$ g/L, $J = 45$ LMH
- $R = 99\%$, $Y = 85\%$, $t = 18$ h/day

**(i) Permeate volume per day:**

$$V_p = Y \cdot V_f \cdot t = 0.85 \times 8{,}000 \times 18$$

$$\boxed{V_p = 122{,}400 \text{ L/day}}$$

**(ii) Membrane area required:**

$$A = \frac{V_p}{J \cdot t} = \frac{122{,}400}{45 \times 18}$$

$$\boxed{A = 151.11 \text{ m}^2 \approx 152 \text{ m}^2}$$

**(iii) Retentate concentration:**

Permeate concentration: $C_p = C_f (1 - R) = 7.0 \times 0.01 = 0.07$ g/L

Retentate volume: $V_r = V_f \cdot t - V_p = 8{,}000 \times 18 - 122{,}400 = 21{,}600$ L/day

Mass balance: $C_f \cdot V_f \cdot t = C_p \cdot V_p + C_r \cdot V_r$

$$C_r = \frac{(7.0 \times 144{,}000) - (0.07 \times 122{,}400)}{21{,}600}$$

$$C_r = \frac{1{,}008{,}000 - 8{,}568}{21{,}600} = \frac{999{,}432}{21{,}600}$$

$$\boxed{C_r = 46.27 \text{ g/L}}$$

So whey protein is concentrated **6.6×** by UF.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **CIP system** for a dairy plant with a flow diagram. List the stages and explain Sinner's Circle in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a flow diagram, explain the working of a **Dairy Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)**. Calculate the **BOD₅** of a dairy effluent given: 15 mL sample + 285 mL dilution water, initial DO = 8.2 mg/L, 5-day DO = 2.8 mg/L, blank initial = 8.2 mg/L, blank final = 8.0 mg/L.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — CIP System Description

**Stages of CIP (Standard 5-Stage Cycle):**

| Stage | Solution | Temp (°C) | Time | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Pre-rinse | Plain water | 40 – 50 | 10 min | Remove loose soil, recover residual product |
| 2. Caustic wash | 1 – 3% NaOH | 70 – 85 | 20 – 30 min | Hydrolyze fat, protein (saponification) |
| 3. Intermediate rinse | Water | 50 | 5 min | Remove alkali |
| 4. Acid wash | 0.5 – 1% HNO₃ | 50 – 70 | 15 min | Dissolve mineral scale (milk stone) |
| 5. Final rinse + sanitize | Hot water / sanitizer | 80 – 90 | 10 min | Microbial kill, leave no residue |

**Sinner's Circle (4 Variables):**

| Variable | Typical Range | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 70 – 85 °C | Reduces surface tension, increases reaction rate |
| **Chemical Concentration** | 1 – 3% NaOH / 0.5 – 1% acid | Reacts with and dissolves soil |
| **Mechanical Action** | 1.5 – 2.5 m/s flow | Shear force removes deposits |
| **Time** | 20 – 30 min per stage | Adequate contact period |

The four factors are **interdependent** — reducing any one requires increasing the others to maintain equivalent cleaning.

**CIP Loop Configurations:** Single-use (most hygienic), Multi-use (reuses caustic for pre-wash), Centralized (one unit serves whole plant), Decentralized (multiple units).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — ETP & BOD₅ Calculation

**ETP Process Flow:**

1. **Bar Screening** → removes floating solids.
2. **Oil & Grease Trap** → removes fats (typical dairy effluent has 100–500 mg/L O&G).
3. **Equalization Tank** → homogenizes flow and pH.
4. **Primary Clarifier** → settles 50–70% TSS.
5. **Biological Treatment (Activated Sludge)** → aerobic bacteria oxidize organic matter; aeration tank operates at F/M = 0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹, MLSS 2000–4000 mg/L, DO 1.5–2 mg/L.
6. **Secondary Clarifier** → separates biomass (sludge).
7. **Tertiary Polishing** → sand filter + activated carbon.
8. **Disinfection** → chlorination or UV.
9. **Sludge Digestion** → anaerobic digester produces biogas.

**BOD₅ Calculation:**

Dilution factor: $f = \dfrac{15}{300} = \dfrac{1}{20} = 0.05$

Decimal volume fraction: $P = 0.05$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f}{P}$$

$$= \frac{(8.2 - 2.8) - (8.2 - 8.0) \times 0.05}{0.05}$$

$$= \frac{5.4 - (0.2 \times 0.05)}{0.05}$$

$$= \frac{5.4 - 0.01}{0.05} = \frac{5.39}{0.05}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 107.8 \text{ mg/L}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing concentration polarization with fouling** — both reduce flux but CP is reversible, fouling is irreversible. Marks lost in 14-mark questions on membrane separation.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract the blank correction in BOD calculation** — a single error in $B_1, B_2$ term costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not converting units in flux problems** — LMH = L/m²·h. Forgetting to multiply by 24 or 18 (operating hours) gives wrong membrane area.
> 4. **CIP stage order** — Students often swap acid and caustic wash. Always **caustic FIRST, acid SECOND** (alkaline soils → acidic soils → sanitizer).
> 5. **RO flux equation sign** — Remember $\Delta \pi$ is **subtracted** because osmotic pressure opposes applied pressure.
> 6. **CIP flow velocity** — Must be **≥ 1.5 m/s** for turbulent flow to achieve mechanical cleaning; lower velocities cause redeposition.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

**🔹 Membrane Separation Essentials:**
- Pressure-driven processes ordered by pore size: **MF > UF > NF > RO** (descending pore size, ascending pressure).
- Governing flux equation: $J = L_p (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ — valid for RO and NF.
- Rejection $R = 1 - (C_p/C_f)$; Recovery $Y = V_p / V_f$.
- **Concentration polarization** reduces flux; control via high cross-flow velocity.
- **Module types**: Plate & Frame, Spiral Wound, Hollow Fiber, Tubular.

**🔹 Dairy Applications:**
- **UF** for whey protein concentration (5–10 kDa MWCO).
- **NF** for partial demineralization and lactose concentration.
- **RO** for pre-evaporation concentration of whey (saves 60–70% energy).
- **MF** for bacteria removal (cold pasteurization).

**🔹 Dairy Plant Layout:**
- Zoning principle: **Receiving → Processing → Value-Added → Packaging → Auxiliary**.
- Maintain **unidirectional flow** (no cross-contamination).
- Provide **hand-wash stations** and **air curtains** between zones.
- Floor slope **1:100** toward drains; use **SS 304/316** food-grade surfaces.

**🔹 CIP Essentials:**
- **5 stages**: Pre-rinse → Caustic → Intermediate rinse → Acid → Final rinse + Sanitize.
- **Sinner's Circle**: Temperature, Chemical, Mechanical, Time (all four must be balanced).
- Caustic (NaOH) **first** to remove organic soil; acid (HNO₃) **second** to remove mineral scale.
- Flow velocity **≥ 1.5 m/s**; caustic at **70 – 85 °C**.

**🔹 Dairy ETP Essentials:**
- Typical raw effluent: BOD 1000–3000 mg/L, COD 2000–5000 mg/L, O&G 100–500 mg/L.
- Discharge norms: BOD < 30 mg/L, COD < 250 mg/L (CPCB).
- Treatment stages: Screening → Oil trap → Equalization → Primary clarifier → Activated sludge → Secondary clarifier → Tertiary polishing → Disinfection.
- Anaerobic digestion of sludge → **biogas** (renewable energy bonus).

**🔹 High-Frequency Formula Recap (Must Memorize):**

| Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|
| $J = V_p / (A \cdot t)$ | Flux |
| $J = L_p (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | RO/NF flux |
| $R = (1 - C_p/C_f) \times 100$ | Rejection % |
| $Y = V_p / V_f$ | Recovery |
| $\pi = iCRT$ | Osmotic pressure (Van't Hoff) |
| $\text{F/M} = QS_o / (VX)$ | Activated sludge design |
| $\text{BOD}_5 = [(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)f] / P$ | BOD determination |
| $A_{mem} = V_p / (J \cdot t)$ | Membrane sizing |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
