> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT602 - DAIRY AND FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Manufacture of Milk Powder, Processing of Milk Products - Condensed Milk - Skim Milk - Butter Milk - Flavoured Milk, Whey, Casein, Yoghurt and Paneer, Manufacture of Butter: Principles, Butter Churn

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Dairy Processing Engineering

Dairy processing engineering deals with the transformation of raw milk into a wide spectrum of value-added products by applying the principles of **heat transfer**, **mass transfer**, **mechanical separation**, and **biochemistry**. The products covered in this module range from dehydrated forms (milk powder) to concentrated forms (condensed milk), separated forms (skim milk, butter, whey, casein), and fermented/coagulated forms (yoghurt, paneer).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This module is the heaviest in terms of process flow diagrams. Students must master the **unit operations** involved (evaporation, drying, separation, churning, fermentation) for both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

## 1.2 Milk Powder (Dried Milk)

### Formal Definition
**Milk powder** is the product obtained by the removal of water from milk (whole, skim, or partly skimmed) by means of a dehydration process, resulting in a solid product with a moisture content typically **less than 4 % (w/w)** and a water activity ($a_w$) **below 0.6**, which imparts a long shelf life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Main Industrial Processes:**
> 1. **Roller (Drum) Drying** – older, gives scorable powders.
> 2. **Spray Drying** – modern, gives instant/high-quality powders (e.g., whole milk powder, skim milk powder, infant formula).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of milk powder as **"milk in slow-motion"** — the water is removed, but the fat, protein, lactose, and minerals are preserved. Just as you can rehydrate **instant coffee powder** with hot water to get coffee, milk powder can be reconstituted with water to give fluid milk. The drying process essentially **locks in the nutrition** in a compact, shelf-stable form.

---

## 1.3 Condensed Milk

### Formal Definition
**Condensed milk** is a concentrated milk product obtained by partial removal of water from milk by evaporation, with or without the addition of sugar. There are two main types:
- **Sweetened Condensed Milk (SCM)** – contains added sugar (acts as a preservative, $a_w \approx 0.83$).
- **Unsweetened Condensed Milk (Evaporated Milk)** – sterilized in cans.

> [!NOTE]
> **Composition (FSSAI / Codex Standard):** Total milk solids **not less than 28 %**, milk fat **not less than 9 %** (for whole condensed milk).

### Conceptual Analogy
Condensed milk is essentially **"milk that has been boiled down"** — like reducing a gravy to make it thicker. The addition of sugar in sweetened condensed milk is similar to **preserving fruit with honey** — the high osmotic pressure inhibits microbial growth.

---

## 1.4 Skim Milk

### Formal Definition
**Skim milk** is the milk from which a sufficient amount of milk fat has been removed to reduce the fat content to **less than 0.5 % (m/m)** of the total product.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of skim milk as **"milk without its cream layer"** — like drinking coffee that has been through a fat separator. It retains all the protein, lactose, and minerals but is leaner in calories. It is the base for making **casein, whey products, and skim milk powder**.

---

## 1.5 Buttermilk

### Formal Definition
**Buttermilk** is the fluid by-product obtained during the churning of cream into butter. In the traditional sense, it is the aqueous phase left after fat globules are aggregated into butter grains. The modern product is often made by **fermenting skim milk** with lactic acid bacteria (*Lactococcus* spp.).

> [!NOTE]
> Composition: Total solids **≈ 9 %**, Fat **≈ 0.5 %**, Protein **≈ 3.4 %**, Lactic acid **≈ 0.6 – 0.8 %**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Buttermilk is the **"tea that remains after brewing"** analogy — when you make butter (like brewing a drink), the flavorful liquid left behind is buttermilk. The lactic acid gives it a tangy flavor, similar to how sourdough bread is tangy.

---

## 1.6 Flavoured Milk

### Formal Definition
**Flavoured milk** is a value-added beverage prepared by mixing milk (whole, skim, or recombined) with permitted sweetening agents, flavoring agents, permitted food color, and stabilizers, followed by pasteurization/UHT treatment and packaging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSSAI Standard:** Fat content should be at least **3.0 %** (whole milk flavoured), and cocoa/chocolate solids (for chocolate milk) should be **at least 1.0 %**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Flavoured milk is **"milk in disguise"** — a base nutrient vehicle (milk) is enhanced with sensory appeal (chocolate, strawberry, vanilla, mango). It is a key vehicle for fortification and a profitable SKU in dairy processing.

---

## 1.7 Whey

### Formal Definition
**Whey** is the liquid remaining after the coagulation of milk by rennet or by acid during cheese/casein manufacture. It contains about **50 % of the solids of original milk**, including **lactose (≈ 4.8 %)**, **whey proteins (≈ 0.6 %)**, and **minerals**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Whey is the **"spent wash" of cheese making** — the greenish liquid that floats above the curd. It was once discarded as waste, but is now recognized as a valuable source of **high-biological-value proteins** (used in body-building supplements).

---

## 1.8 Casein

### Formal Definition
**Casein** is the major protein fraction of milk (≈ **80 % of total milk protein**), precipitated by acidification to its isoelectric point (**pH 4.6**). It is used in plastics, adhesives, paint, pharmaceuticals, and food.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two types of casein:**
> - **Rennet Casein** – precipitated by enzymatic coagulation (used for cheese plastics).
> - **Acid Casein** – precipitated by mineral/organic acids (used as food ingredient).

### Conceptual Analogy
Casein is **"milk's building block"** — when milk turns sour, the curds that form are essentially casein. It is the protein that gives milk its white color and is responsible for the structure of cheese and paneer.

---

## 1.9 Yoghurt

### Formal Definition
**Yoghurt** is a fermented milk product obtained by the controlled fermentation of milk (whole, partly skimmed, skimmed, or reconstituted) with a mixed culture of **Streptococcus thermophilus** and **Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus**. The pH is lowered to **≈ 4.4 – 4.6**, and the product has a characteristic gel structure and acidic taste.

### Conceptual Analogy
Yoghurt is **"milk's tangy transformation"** — the friendly bacteria consume lactose and produce lactic acid, which thickens the milk and gives it a tangy flavor, much like a sourdough starter transforms flour.

---

## 1.10 Paneer

### Formal Definition
**Paneer** is an Indian indigenous acid-coagulated milk product obtained by coagulating hot milk (typically **80 – 90 °C**) with citric acid, lactic acid, or sour whey, followed by draining of whey and pressing of the curd.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSSAI Standard:** Moisture **not more than 70 %**, Fat on dry matter basis **not less than 50 %**, Protein **not less than 9 %** (in dry matter).

### Conceptual Analogy
Paneer is the **"Indian cottage cheese"** — when hot milk meets acid (lemon juice or vinegar), the protein (casein) curdles and forms a soft solid. It is the Indian cousin of queso fresco or farmer's cheese.

---

## 1.11 Butter

### Formal Definition
**Butter** is a water-in-oil emulsion made by churning cream (or separating butter grains from buttermilk), working the grains, and adding salt. It contains at least **80 % milk fat**, **16 % water**, and **2 % solids-not-fat** (FSSAI / Codex).

### Conceptual Analogy
Butter is **"cream in disguise"** — the agitation (churning) breaks the fat globule membrane and causes the fat to clump together, separating from the watery buttermilk. It is essentially a phase inversion from oil-in-water (cream) to water-in-oil (butter).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manufacture of Milk Powder – Process Engineering

### 2.1.1 Spray Drying Process Flow

The industrial manufacture of milk powder follows these logical steps:

1. **Pretreatment of milk** – Standardization to required fat/SNF ratio.
2. **Preheating / Pasteurization** – 72 °C for 15 s (HTST) or 90 °C for 5 min.
3. **Concentration (Evaporation)** – Multi-effect evaporator, concentrates milk from 12 % TS to **45 – 52 % TS** (precondensate).
4. **Homogenization** – Two-stage homogenizer at **150 – 200 bar** to break fat globules and prevent free-fat in powder.
5. **Spray Drying** – Atomization in drying chamber, inlet air **180 – 220 °C**, outlet air **80 – 100 °C**.
6. **Instantization (Agglomeration)** – For instant whole milk powder, particles are agglomerated to improve wettability.
7. **Packaging** – Under nitrogen to prevent oxidation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pre-concentrate before spray drying?** Direct drying of milk to powder is uneconomical (water to be evaporated is high). Pre-concentration reduces water to be removed by ~50 %, saving substantial energy.

### 2.1.2 Roller (Drum) Drying

- Milk is applied as a thin film onto steam-heated rotating metal drums.
- Drying time is short (**2 – 4 s**).
- Powder is scorable from the drum and ground.
- Disadvantage: Higher heat damage, less soluble powder, used for **animal feed** and **industrial applications**.

---

## 2.2 Condensed Milk – Engineering Principles

### 2.2.1 Manufacturing Steps

1. **Standardization of milk** to desired fat:SNF ratio.
2. **Preheating** to 82 – 95 °C (activates -SH groups to prevent age-thickening).
3. **Addition of sugar** (for sweetened condensed milk) – typically **18 – 20 %** of finished product.
4. **Evaporation** under vacuum (multiple-effect) at 45 – 55 °C.
5. **Cooling** with seeding of lactose crystals (if SCM) to prevent sandiness.
6. **Packaging** in sterile containers.

### 2.2.2 Key Concept: Role of Sugar in SCM

- Sugar reduces **water activity** ($a_w$).
- Below $a_w = 0.86$, microbial growth is inhibited.
- **Preservative index** of sugar is critical; insufficient sugar leads to spoilage.

---

## 2.3 Skim Milk Processing

- **Mechanical separation** in a centrifugal cream separator (typically **6,000 – 8,000 rpm**).
- Standardization of fat to **< 0.5 %**.
- Skim milk is the base for **casein, whey, and skim milk powder**.

---

## 2.4 Buttermilk Engineering

Two main types:
1. **Natural buttermilk** – byproduct of butter churning.
2. **Cultured buttermilk** – skim milk inoculated with lactic cultures, fermented until pH 4.5.

---

## 2.5 Flavoured Milk Processing

**Key unit operations:**
1. Mixing milk + sugar (8–10 %) + stabilizer (0.1 – 0.2 %) + flavor/color.
2. **Homogenization** (single or two-stage at 150 bar) to prevent fat separation.
3. **Pasteurization** (72 °C/15 s) or **UHT** (135 – 150 °C for 2 – 4 s).
4. **Cooling and packaging** (aseptic for UHT).

---

## 2.6 Whey Processing

- **Whey → Whey protein concentrate (WPC)** by ultrafiltration.
- **Whey → Whey protein isolate (WPI)** by ion exchange.
- **Whey → Lactose** by crystallization.

---

## 2.7 Casein Manufacture

**Two principal methods:**
1. **Acid casein** – HCl or H₂SO₄ added to skim milk to **pH 4.6**, curd washed and dried.
2. **Rennet casein** – Enzyme rennin (chymosin) added, pH adjusted to **6.5 – 6.7**, curd dried.

> [!NOTE]
> Drying of casein: moisture reduced to **< 10 %** in a fluidized bed or tunnel dryer.

---

## 2.8 Yoghurt Processing

**Process steps:**
1. Standardization of milk to **3.5 % fat, 12 – 14 % MSNF**.
2. **Heat treatment** – 90 – 95 °C for **5 – 10 min** (denatures whey proteins, improves body).
3. **Homogenization** at 50 – 100 bar.
4. **Cooling to 42 – 45 °C** (optimum for culture growth).
5. **Inoculation** with **2 – 3 % starter culture** (S. thermophilus + L. bulgaricus).
6. **Incubation** at 42 °C for 4 – 6 h until pH 4.5.
7. **Cooling to < 10 °C** to arrest fermentation.
8. **Flavoring/fruit addition** (for stirred/fruit yoghurt).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two types:** **Set yoghurt** (fermented in final pack) and **Stirred yoghurt** (fermented in bulk, then fruit mixed).

---

## 2.9 Paneer Manufacturing

**Standard process:**
1. Milk standardized to **3.5 – 4.0 % fat, 8.5 % SNF**.
2. Heated to **82 – 90 °C** (no holding).
3. Coagulant (citric acid 1 % solution) added until pH 5.4 – 5.5.
4. Whey drained through muslin cloth.
5. Curd pressed in a **paneer press** (load of 7 – 8 kg per kg curd) for 20 – 30 min.
6. Final product: 70 % moisture max, 50 % fat on dry basis.

---

## 2.10 Butter Manufacture

### 2.10.1 Principles
- **Cream is an oil-in-water emulsion** (O/W).
- **Butter is a water-in-oil emulsion** (W/O).
- Churning causes **phase inversion** by mechanically disrupting the fat globule membrane (FGM).
- Fat globules aggregate into butter grains; buttermilk drains off.

### 2.10.2 Process Flow

1. **Cream separation** from milk (separator at 6,000 rpm).
2. **Neutralization** of cream acidity (Na₂CO₃ or Ca(OH)₂).
3. **Pasteurization** of cream (72 – 85 °C).
4. **Aging of cream** – cooling to 5 – 8 °C for **12 – 18 h** (crystallization of fat).
5. **Churning** in butter churn at **8 – 12 °C** until butter grains form.
6. **Draining buttermilk**.
7. **Washing** butter grains with cold water.
8. **Working (Kneading)** – distributes water droplets finely.
9. **Salting** (optional) – 2 % salt added during working.
10. **Packaging and storage** (≤ 4 °C).

### 2.10.3 Butter Churn – Engineering Design

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Wooden/Stainless Steel Churn | Mechanical agitation chamber |
| Dasher / Rollers | Stirring mechanism |
| Vent | For release of air (de-aeration prevents foaming) |
| Sight Glass | To observe butter grain formation |
| Drain plug | To remove buttermilk |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial types of churns:**
> 1. **Batch churn** (traditional) – wooden box with rollers.
> 2. **Continuous churn** (e.g., **Westfalia, Alfa Laval, Fritz, Contimab**).
> 3. **Margarine process** (CJS – Cherry-Burrell process).

---

## 2.11 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Water activity** | $a_w = p / p_0$ (vapour pressure of product / vapour pressure of pure water) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Concentration ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{TS_{in}}{TS_{out}} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ (for evaporator) | — |
| 3 | **Total solids balance** | $F_{in} \cdot TS_{in} = F_{out} \cdot TS_{out}$ | kg/h |
| 4 | **Mass balance (drying)** | $F_{in} = F_{out} + W$ where $W$ is evaporated water | kg/h |
| 5 | **Solids recovery** | $\eta = \dfrac{P \cdot TS_P}{F \cdot TS_F} \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | **Specific energy (evap.)** | $E_s = \dfrac{1}{N_{eff}}$ where $N_{eff}$ is no. of effects | kg steam/kg water |
| 7 | **Lactose / Casein ratio** | In milk: Casein ≈ 80 % of total protein; Whey protein ≈ 20 % | % |
| 8 | **Isoelectric pH of casein** | pH 4.6 | — |
| 9 | **Centrifugal force** | $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 r$ | N |
| 10 | **Butter fat composition (FSSAI)** | Fat ≥ 80 %, Water ≤ 16 %, SNF ≤ 2 % | % w/w |
| 11 | **Churn speed** | 25 – 35 rpm (batch churn) | rpm |
| 12 | **Paneer composition (FSSAI)** | Moisture ≤ 70 %, Fat (DMB) ≥ 50 %, Protein (DMB) ≥ 9 % | % w/w |
| 13 | **Yoghurt pH end-point** | 4.4 – 4.6 | — |
| 14 | **Spray dryer outlet humidity** | $RH_{out} = 8 - 12$ % typically | % |
| 15 | **Milk fat content in cream** | $Fat_{cream} = \dfrac{Fat_{milk} \times V_{milk}}{V_{cream}}$ | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL ALERT:** In material balance problems, students often confuse **TS (Total Solids) basis** with **wet weight basis**. Always clarify whether the answer is on **dry basis (DB)** or **wet basis (WB)**.

---

## 2.12 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Evaporator design** in dairy plants uses the multiple-effect principle to recover latent heat.
- **Spray dryer** design uses mass and heat balance coupled with psychrometrics.
- **Centrifugal separation** of cream relies on Stokes' law: $v = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$ (modified for centrifugal field).
- **Churn design** balances mass, momentum, and fluid shear to ensure phase inversion without excessive fat loss.
- These principles are also applied in **edible oil refining, beverage processing, and biopharmaceutical** industries.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Material Balance on an Evaporator (For Concentrated Milk)

### Problem Statement
A dairy plant processes **10,000 kg/h of whole milk** containing **12 % total solids (TS)** in a multiple-effect evaporator. The concentrate leaving the evaporator contains **50 % TS**. Calculate the **concentrate flow rate** and the **water evaporated**.

### Step 1: Define the variables

- $F_{in} = 10{,}000$ kg/h (feed milk)
- $TS_{in} = 0.12$ (fraction)
- $TS_{out} = 0.50$ (fraction)
- $F_{out} = ?$ (concentrate flow)
- $W = ?$ (water evaporated)

### Step 2: Apply overall mass balance

$$
F_{in} = F_{out} + W
$$

$$
10{,}000 = F_{out} + W
$$

### Step 3: Apply solids balance (no solids are removed in evaporation)

$$
F_{in} \cdot TS_{in} = F_{out} \cdot TS_{out}
$$

$$
10{,}000 \times 0.12 = F_{out} \times 0.50
$$

### Step 4: Solve for $F_{out}$

$$
F_{out} = \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 0.12}{0.50} = \dfrac{1{,}200}{0.50} = 2{,}400 \text{ kg/h}
$$

### Step 5: Solve for $W$

$$
W = F_{in} - F_{out} = 10{,}000 - 2{,}400 = 7{,}600 \text{ kg/h}
$$

### Step 6: Concentration ratio (CR)

$$
CR = \dfrac{TS_{out}}{TS_{in}} = \dfrac{0.50}{0.12} \approx 4.17
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The evaporator concentrates the milk to **4.17 times** its original concentration, removing **7.6 tonnes of water per hour**.

---

## 3.2 Spray Dryer Water Balance (Two-Stage Drying with Fines Return)

### Problem Statement
A spray dryer receives **2,000 kg/h of pre-concentrated milk at 48 % TS** and produces powder at **96 % TS**. The fines return system reduces wastage; the powder collected from the chamber has 3.5 % moisture, and after fluidized bed drying, final moisture is 3.0 %.

### Step 1: Solids balance

$$
F_{in} \cdot TS_{in} = F_{out} \cdot TS_{out}
$$

$$
2{,}000 \times 0.48 = F_{out} \times 0.97
$$

### Step 2: Solve for $F_{out}$

$$
F_{out} = \dfrac{2{,}000 \times 0.48}{0.97} = \dfrac{960}{0.97} \approx 989.7 \text{ kg/h}
$$

### Step 3: Water evaporated

$$
W = 2{,}000 - 989.7 = 1{,}010.3 \text{ kg/h}
$$

### Step 4: Energy required (rough estimate)

Assuming $2{,}300$ kJ/kg water evaporated (with dryer efficiency ≈ 60 %):

$$
Q = \dfrac{W \times 2{,}300}{0.60} = \dfrac{1{,}010.3 \times 2{,}300}{0.60} \approx 3{,}873{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = 1{,}075 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** This shows why pre-concentration is economically critical — drying 10,000 kg/h of 12 % milk would require **2.4 times** the energy used here.

---

## 3.3 Butter Composition Check (FSSAI Verification)

A butter sample has the following composition by analysis: **Fat = 82 %**, **Moisture = 15.5 %**, **SNF (Curd) = 2.5 %**.

### Step 1: Verify compliance

- FSSAI requires: Fat ≥ 80 %, Moisture ≤ 16 %, SNF ≤ 2 % (typically).
- Sample values: Fat = 82 % ≥ 80 % ✓
- Moisture = 15.5 % ≤ 16 % ✓
- SNF = 2.5 % > 2 % (slightly out of range for FSSAI, but acceptable per Codex)

### Step 2: Theoretical yield from cream

If cream contains **40 % fat** and butter contains **82 % fat**:

$$
\text{Yield} = \dfrac{Fat_{cream}}{Fat_{butter}} = \dfrac{40}{82} \approx 0.488
$$

So, **1 kg of 40 % fat cream** yields approximately **0.488 kg of butter** (rest is buttermilk + losses).

---

## 3.4 Paneer Yield Calculation

### Problem Statement
How much paneer is obtained from 100 kg of milk standardized to 3.5 % fat and 8.5 % SNF? Assume moisture in paneer = 70 %, fat in paneer = 50 % (on dry matter basis).

### Step 1: Calculate total solids in milk

$$
TS_{milk} = 3.5 + 8.5 = 12.0 \text{ \%}
$$

### Step 2: Total solids input

$$
TS_{input} = 100 \times 0.12 = 12 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 3: Total solids in paneer (assuming 95 % solids recovery)

$$
TS_{paneer} = 12 \times 0.95 = 11.4 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 4: Paneer weight (since 30 % is TS)

$$
W_{paneer} = \dfrac{11.4}{0.30} = 38 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 5: Verify fat content

Fat in paneer = $3.5 \times 0.95 = 3.325$ kg

$$
\% Fat (DMB) = \dfrac{3.325}{11.4} \times 100 \approx 29.2 \text{ \%}
$$

This is **less than 50 %**, indicating this was very low-fat milk. To meet 50 % fat (DMB), milk must be standardized higher or with **6 % fat**.

---

## 3.5 Yoghurt Acid Development Kinetics

### Problem Statement
The pH of milk drops from 6.6 to 4.5 over 5 hours during yoghurt fermentation. Assuming first-order kinetics for lactic acid production:

$$
\dfrac{dC}{dt} = k \cdot C
$$

Estimate the rate constant $k$.

### Step 1: Hydrogen ion concentration change

- At pH 6.6: $[H^+]_i = 10^{-6.6} = 2.51 \times 10^{-7}$ M
- At pH 4.5: $[H^+]_f = 10^{-4.5} = 3.16 \times 10^{-5}$ M

### Step 2: First-order rate constant

$$
k = \dfrac{1}{t} \ln \left( \dfrac{[H^+]_f}{[H^+]_i} \right)
$$

$$
k = \dfrac{1}{5} \ln \left( \dfrac{3.16 \times 10^{-5}}{2.51 \times 10^{-7}} \right) = \dfrac{1}{5} \ln(125.9)
$$

$$
k = \dfrac{1}{5} \times 4.836 = 0.967 \text{ h}^{-1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value is the basis for **starter culture activity tests** in dairy plants.

---

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key functional insight:** The **churn must balance three competing requirements**:
> 1. **Mechanical impact** for phase inversion (agitation).
> 2. **Gentle handling** to avoid fat loss.
> 3. **Temperature control** (±1 °C) for proper fat crystallization.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between whole milk powder and skim milk powder. State the major steps in the manufacture of milk powder.**

**Model Answer:**
| Parameter | Whole Milk Powder (WMP) | Skim Milk Powder (SMP) |
|---|---|---|
| Fat content | 26 – 28 % (min 26 %) | < 1.0 % |
| Protein | 24 – 27 % | 34 – 37 % |
| Lactose | 36 – 38 % | 49 – 52 % |
| Use | Beverage, chocolate, bakery | Infant formula, recombined milk, confectionery |

**Major steps in milk powder manufacture:**
1. Standardization and pasteurization.
2. Pre-concentration in a multi-effect evaporator (12 % → 50 % TS).
3. Homogenization.
4. Spray drying (inlet 180 – 220 °C, outlet 80 – 100 °C).
5. Optional agglomeration (for instant powder).
6. Packaging under nitrogen.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is the role of sugar in sweetened condensed milk? What is the standard composition as per FSSAI?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Role of sugar:**
  1. Acts as a preservative by lowering water activity ($a_w$ to ~0.83).
  2. Provides characteristic sweet flavor.
  3. Inhibits microbial growth, especially *Clostridium* and yeasts.
- **FSSAI Composition:** Total milk solids ≥ 28 %, milk fat ≥ 9 %, sugar ≥ 40 % (in sweetened condensed milk).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) – KTU Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe in detail the manufacturing process of milk powder by spray drying. State the role of each unit operation.**

**Model Answer:**

The spray drying process converts liquid milk into stable powder through a series of coordinated unit operations:

1. **Reception and Standardization:** Milk is received, tested for quality, and standardized to desired fat:SNF ratio. This ensures uniformity in the final product.

2. **Pasteurization/Preheating (HTST):** 72 °C for 15 seconds or 85 °C for 5 minutes. Kills pathogens, inactivates lipases, and improves powder keeping quality.

3. **Concentration by Multiple-Effect Evaporation:** Milk is concentrated from 12 % to 45 – 52 % TS. This reduces the water to be removed in the spray dryer, making the process energy-efficient. Triple-effect evaporators are common in industry.

4. **Homogenization:** Concentrate is passed through a two-stage homogenizer at 150 – 200 bar. This breaks fat globules uniformly, prevents creaming, and reduces free fat in the powder.

5. **Spray Drying:**
   - Concentrate is atomized through a nozzle (pressure or centrifugal) into a drying chamber.
   - Hot air (180 – 220 °C) contacts the fine droplets.
   - Water evaporates almost instantaneously, leaving dried particles (60 – 200 µm).
   - Outlet air is typically 80 – 100 °C.
   - Powder is discharged at the bottom of the chamber and from cyclones.

6. **Agglomeration (for Instant Powder):** Particles are wetted and re-dried to form larger porous aggregates for quick wettability.

7. **Packaging:** Under nitrogen to prevent oxidation of fat. Pack sizes: 25 kg bags (industry) or consumer pouches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role of pre-concentration:** Direct spray drying of milk to powder would require evaporation of 8.4 kg water per kg powder. With pre-concentration to 50 % TS, only 0.96 kg water needs to be removed per kg powder — a **9-fold reduction in dryer load**.

**(b) [7 Marks] With a neat flow diagram, explain the manufacture of butter from cream. Discuss the principles of phase inversion in churning.**

**Model Answer:**

**Flow Diagram:**
$$\text{Milk} \rightarrow \text{Separation} \rightarrow \text{Cream (40 \% fat)} \rightarrow \text{Neutralization} \rightarrow \text{Pasteurization} \rightarrow \text{Cooling} \rightarrow \text{Aging (12-18 h, 5-8 °C)} \rightarrow \text{Churning} \rightarrow \text{Butter Grains} \rightarrow \text{Buttermilk Drainage} \rightarrow \text{Washing} \rightarrow \text{Working (Salt Addition)} \rightarrow \text{Butter}$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Separation:** Whole milk is centrifugally separated to obtain cream with **35 – 45 %** fat.

2. **Neutralization:** Acidic cream is neutralized with Na₂CO₃ to prevent fat loss during churning. Acidity should be below **0.15 % lactic acid**.

3. **Pasteurization:** 72 – 85 °C to kill pathogens.

4. **Aging:** Cream is cooled to 5 – 8 °C and held for **12 – 18 hours** to allow partial crystallization of fat (this is essential for proper churning and texture).

5. **Churning:** Cream is agitated in a churn at **8 – 12 °C** (optimum for fat crystallization). Air is whipped in, foam forms, and the fat globule membrane (FGM) is destabilized mechanically. Fat globules collide, agglomerate, and form butter grains of 1 – 2 cm.

6. **Drainage of buttermilk:** After butter grains form, the watery phase (buttermilk) is drained.

7. **Washing:** Butter grains are washed with cold water to remove residual buttermilk (improves keeping quality).

8. **Working (Kneading):** Butter is worked to evenly distribute the remaining water as fine droplets. Salt (2 %) may be added.

9. **Packaging and storage:** At 4 °C or below.

**Principle of Phase Inversion:**
- **Cream** is an **oil-in-water (O/W) emulsion** — fat globules are dispersed in a continuous water phase.
- **Butter** is a **water-in-oil (W/O) emulsion** — water droplets are dispersed in a continuous fat phase.
- During churning, mechanical agitation **destabilizes the FGM**, fat globules aggregate (cluster) due to hydrophobic attraction, and at a critical fat concentration, **phase inversion** occurs.
- The continuous phase switches from water to fat — this is the point of butter formation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is aging important?** Without aging, fat is in a fully liquid state (post-pasteurization). Aging creates a mix of solid fat crystals + liquid fat that is essential for the agglomeration step.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 – Alternative) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the manufacture of condensed milk. What is the role of sugar in sweetened condensed milk?**

**Model Answer:**

**Manufacturing Steps of Condensed Milk:**

1. **Standardization:** Fat:SNF ratio adjusted to give final product with 9 % fat and 22 % SNF.

2. **Preheating:** Milk heated to **82 – 95 °C** for 5 – 10 min. This:
   - Kills bacteria and spores.
   - Inactivates enzymes (lipases, proteases).
   - Activates **-SH groups** in β-lactoglobulin to prevent **age-thickening** (which makes SCM unpourable).
   - Reduces viscosity.

3. **Addition of Sugar (for SCM):** Cane sugar (sucrose) is added as a syrup at **18 – 20 %** of the finished product. Critical step to ensure proper concentration.

4. **Vacuum Evaporation:** In multiple-effect evaporators at 45 – 55 °C. Concentrates milk from 12 % to **28 – 30 % TS** for SCM, or 25 – 26 % for evaporated milk.

5. **Cooling and Seeding (for SCM):** Concentrate is cooled to 30 – 35 °C and seeded with fine **lactose crystals** (1 – 2 mg/kg) to induce controlled crystallization. This prevents **sandiness** (large crystals) and ensures smooth texture.

6. **Packaging:** In lacquered cans or aseptic pouches.

**Role of Sugar in Sweetened Condensed Milk:**

1. **Preservative Action:** Sugar binds free water and reduces **water activity ($a_w$)** to ≈ 0.83. Below this level, bacterial growth is inhibited. *Clostridium* and most yeasts cannot grow.

2. **Energy and Sweetness:** Adds caloric value and characteristic sweet taste.

3. **Texture Modulation:** Influences viscosity and mouthfeel.

> [!WARNING]
> **FSSAI Pitfall:** In FSSAI, sugar is reported as a percent of finished product, **NOT** as percent of milk. Students often confuse this. **Sugar ≥ 40 %, Fat ≥ 9 %, MSNF ≥ 22 %**.

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the manufacture of yoghurt. Explain the role of starter culture and the importance of heat treatment.**

**Model Answer:**

**Yoghurt Manufacturing Process:**

1. **Milk Standardization:** Milk standardized to 3.0 – 3.5 % fat and **12 – 14 % MSNF** (milk solids not fat). Higher MSNF improves body and reduces syneresis (whey separation).

2. **Heat Treatment (Critical Step):** 90 – 95 °C for 5 – 10 minutes. This step:
   - **Denatures whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin)** — they bind casein micelles, increasing water-holding capacity and reducing syneresis.
   - Kills competing spoilage organisms.
   - Improves texture and viscosity.

3. **Homogenization:** 50 – 100 bar, to improve stability and prevent fat separation.

4. **Cooling to Inoculation Temperature:** 42 – 45 °C (optimum for culture growth).

5. **Inoculation with Starter Culture:**
   - **Streptococcus salivarius subsp. thermophilus** — grows fast initially, produces acid and CO₂.
   - **Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus** — provides flavor (acetaldehyde) and continues acid production.
   - Symbiotic relationship: S. thermophilus generates formate that stimulates L. bulgaricus; L. bulgaricus releases amino acids that stimulate S. thermophilus.
   - Inoculation rate: **2 – 3 %** of milk volume.

6. **Incubation/Fermentation:** 42 – 45 °C for 4 – 6 h until pH **4.4 – 4.6** is reached. Two product types:
   - **Set yoghurt:** Fermented in the final cup.
   - **Stirred yoghurt:** Fermented in bulk, then fruit/flavor is mixed in.

7. **Cooling:** Rapid cooling to 5 – 10 °C to arrest fermentation and develop flavor.

8. **Packaging and Storage:** At 4 °C for a shelf life of 2 – 3 weeks.

**Role of Heat Treatment:**
- Whey protein denaturation: **β-lactoglobulin binds to κ-casein** to form a complex that increases water-holding capacity.
- Reduces syneresis (whey separation).
- Improves gel firmness and viscosity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbiotic Protocooperation:** S. thermophilus grows faster initially, lowers O.R. potential and produces formate; L. bulgaricus uses formate and produces CO₂ and pyruvate which in turn stimulates S. thermophilus. This is **"protocooperation"** — a classic KTU expected answer.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks in Module 4 questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "fat in cream" with "fat in butter"** — Many students forget that cream must be 35 – 40 % fat and butter has 80 % fat; a yield calculation must reflect this.
> 2. **Skipping the "preheating" step** in milk powder/condensed milk diagrams — Examiners specifically look for preheating. Skipping it costs 1 – 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to state operating temperatures** in butter churning (8 – 12 °C) and yoghurt incubation (42 – 45 °C). Examiner penalizes for "vague process description."
> 4. **Writing generic answers for "phase inversion"** — A complete answer must mention O/W → W/O and the role of FGM disruption.
> 5. **Not specifying FSSAI / Codex standards** — Always quote numeric standards (e.g., "≥ 80 % fat" for butter). A numeric answer is always rewarded over a qualitative one.
> 6. **Ignoring energy balance or material balance** in spray dryer questions. Examiner expects at least one numerical example.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Milk Powder:** Spray drying with pre-concentration (12 % → 50 % TS); outlet moisture < 4 %; types: WMP, SMP, Infant Formula.
- [ ] **Condensed Milk:** Two types — SCM (with sugar, $a_w$ 0.83) and Evaporated Milk (sterilized in can). Heat pretreatment to **82 – 95 °C** is mandatory to prevent age-thickening.
- [ ] **Skim Milk:** Fat < 0.5 %; produced by centrifugal separation at 6,000 – 8,000 rpm; base for casein, whey, and SMP.
- [ ] **Buttermilk:** Byproduct of butter churning; or cultured skim milk; tangy flavor due to lactic acid.
- [ ] **Flavoured Milk:** Milk + sugar (8 – 10 %) + stabilizer (0.1 – 0.2 %) + flavor; pasteurized or UHT-treated.
- [ ] **Whey:** Co-product of cheese/casein; contains lactose (4.8 %) and whey proteins (0.6 %); used for WPC, WPI, and lactose.
- [ ] **Casein:** 80 % of milk protein; precipitated at **pH 4.6** (isoelectric point); two types: acid casein and rennet casein.
- [ ] **Yoghurt:** Fermented milk; co-culture of *S. thermophilus* + *L. bulgaricus*; pH 4.4 – 4.6; heat treatment at 90 – 95 °C is critical.
- [ ] **Paneer:** Acid-coagulated (citric acid); hot milk (82 – 90 °C); pressed at 7 – 8 kg/kg; FSSAI: moisture ≤ 70 %, fat (DMB) ≥ 50 %.
- [ ] **Butter:** Fat ≥ 80 %, water ≤ 16 %, SNF ≤ 2 %; O/W → W/O phase inversion; aging at 5 – 8 °C for 12 – 18 h.
- [ ] **Butter Churn:** Wooden or stainless steel; dasher for agitation; aging critical for fat crystallization; operates at 8 – 12 °C.
- [ ] **Concentration Ratio (CR):** $CR = TS_{out} / TS_{in} = V_{in} / V_{out}$.
- [ ] **Material Balance:** Always use $F_{in} \cdot TS_{in} = F_{out} \cdot TS_{out}$ for any dairy process calculation.
- [ ] **FSSAI Standards:** Always quote numeric thresholds in your answer (e.g., Fat ≥ 80 %, Moisture ≤ 16 % for butter).

### High-Frequency KTU Mnemonics

- **YMPCL-BF-PW** = **Y**oghurt, **M**ilk Powder, **C**ondensed, **L**assi = **B**utter, **F**lavoured, **P**aneer, **W**hey.
- **Yoghurt C-D-I** = **C**ool 42 °C, **D**enature 90 °C, **I**noculate 2 – 3 %.
- **Butter 8-12-16-80** = **8**% temp (°C), **12**% time, **16**% max water, **80**% min fat.
- **Casein 4-6-80** = pH **4.6**, **80**% of milk protein, isoelectric.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When answering 14-mark questions, **always include a flow diagram** (Mermaid-style in your draft or block diagram in your answer sheet). Examiners give 1 – 2 marks extra for neat, labeled diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation Methods and Equipment for Manufacture of Cheese, Ghee, Ice Creams and Frozen Desserts - Standards for Milk and Milk Products - Packaging of Milk and Milk Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT602 - DAIRY AND FOOD ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Preparation Methods and Equipment for Manufacture of Cheese, Ghee, Ice Creams and Frozen Desserts - Standards for Milk and Milk Products - Packaging of Milk and Milk Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cheese — Formal Definition and Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheese** is a concentrated dairy product obtained by the **coagulation of casein** (the principal milk protein) through the action of selected **starter cultures** (lactic acid bacteria) and **milk-coagulating enzymes** (rennet, microbial coagulants, or vegetable coagulants), followed by separation of whey, shaping, salting, and ripening under controlled conditions.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine milk as a basket holding two different "ingredients" — a sponge-like **protein network (casein)** floating in a watery **liquid (whey)**. Cheese-making is essentially using gentle "knitting needles" (rennet enzymes and acid from bacteria) to weave those protein sponges together into a continuous mesh. The mesh traps fat, water, and minerals; the watery whey is drained off. The remaining mesh is the curd — the basis of cheese. Ripening (aging) is simply letting that mesh slowly "rest" so that enzymes inside it (and from the cultures) gradually chop and reshape the proteins and fats into flavourful fragments.

> [!NOTE]
> The **moisture-to-protein ratio**, **fat-in-dry-matter (FDM)** percentage, and **pH at milling** are the three pillars that decide cheese variety — Cheddar, Mozzarella, Gouda, Swiss, etc.

### 1.2 Ghee — Formal Definition and Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ghee** (clarified milk fat) is a heat-cleared, nearly water-free dairy fat obtained by separating butter-oil from milk solids (curdy particles, lactose, and moisture) through controlled heating until the moisture is evaporated and the milk solids turn golden brown.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Ghee-making is like refining crude oil. Butter (or cream) is heated gently. Water in it evaporates as steam, the milk solids settle at the bottom and caramelise (giving the characteristic nutty aroma), and the pure fat rises to the top. The golden top layer is decanted — that is ghee. In one sentence: **Ghee = Butter − Water − Lactose − Curdy Solids, all cooked out by controlled heat.**

### 1.3 Ice Cream and Frozen Desserts — Formal Definition and Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ice Cream** is a frozen dairy dessert produced by simultaneously **freezing** and **agitating (scraping/whipping)** a pasteurised mix of milk, cream, sugar, stabilisers, emulsifiers, and optional flavouring, to incorporate **40–50 % air (overrun)** and produce fine **ice crystals (≤ 50 µm)** and a stable **fat-globule partial coalescence network**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of ice cream as a three-phase foam: (i) tiny ice crystals (the "bricks"), (ii) fat droplets partially clumped (the "mortar"), and (iii) a thick sugar-water syrup containing dissolved milk proteins (the "cement"), with millions of tiny air bubbles (the "air pockets"). The freezer does the freezing; the dasher (scraper-blade) inside the freezer does the whipping. The emulsifier keeps fat droplets from merging completely; the stabiliser keeps the syrup from becoming rock-hard ice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Overrun (%)** is the percentage increase in volume of ice cream compared to the original mix volume. Standard overrun for commercial ice cream = **70–100 %**. Premium brands = **20–50 %**.

### 1.4 Standards for Milk and Milk Products

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standards define the **legal compositional, microbiological, and hygienic quality** parameters. In India, dairy standards are governed by the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** under the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011** (and amendments), aligned with **Codex Alimentarius** and **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** IS specifications.

### 1.5 Packaging of Milk and Milk Products

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dairy packaging** is the science of enclosing milk and milk products in suitable containers to **protect them from physical, chemical, microbiological, and environmental deterioration**, while ensuring **consumer appeal, convenience, regulatory compliance**, and **extended shelf life**.

---

### 1.6 Quick Reference Block — Key Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Pasteurisation (HTST) | 72 °C / 15 s | Minimum legal in FSSAI |
| Pasteurisation (Batch) | 63 °C / 30 min | Holder method |
| UHT | 135–150 °C / 2–4 s | Sterile shelf life |
| Cheddar moisture | ≤ 39 % | FSSAI |
| Process cheese moisture | ≤ 47 % | FSSAI |
| Ghee moisture | ≤ 0.5 % | FSSAI |
| Ice cream overrun | 70–100 % | Standard commercial |
| Ice crystal size (good) | ≤ 50 µm | Smooth texture |
| Milk fat (standardised) | 4.5 % / 6.0 % | Toned / Double-toned |
| SNF (solids-not-fat) | 8.5 % | Cow milk baseline |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory anchor:** *Pasteurise at 72/15 OR 63/30 — pick one, never mix.* These are the only two legal times given in FSSAI Regulation 2.1.2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cheese Manufacturing — Unit Operations in Detail

The general cheese-making line consists of **seven sequential operations**:

1. **Standardisation of milk** — fat:casein ratio adjusted to cheese variety (e.g., Cheddar needs casein:fat ≈ 0.70).
2. **Pasteurisation (HTST 72 °C / 15 s)** — destroys pathogens, inactivates undesirable enzymes, but preserves the natural **rennet-clotting ability** of casein.
3. **Cooling to inoculation temperature (30–32 °C)** — for mesophilic starters; for thermophilic (e.g., Mozzarella/Swiss) it is **38–42 °C**.
4. **Starter addition** — lactic acid bacteria such as *Lactococcus lactis subsp. lactis*, *L. lactis subsp. cremoris*, and adjuncts (*Leuconostoc*, *Lactobacillus helveticus*) ferment **lactose → lactic acid**, lowering pH from ~6.6 to **6.4–6.0** for ripening cheeses.
5. **Coagulation with rennet** — chymosin cleaves **κ-casein** at the Phe105–Met106 bond, destabilising the casein micelle so it aggregates into a gel.
6. **Cutting the curd** — vertical and horizontal wires (1–2 cm spacing) slice the gel into cubes, expelling whey.
7. **Cooking, draining, cheddaring, milling, salting, hooping, pressing, ripening.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **yield of cheese** from milk is governed by:
> - Casein content of milk (genetic + feeding)
> - Fat recovery in the curd (~ 90 %)
> - Moisture retained in curd
> - Salt and lactose loss in whey

### 2.2 Cheese Varieties — Manufacturing Method Summary

| Variety | Coagulation | pH at hooping | Moisture % | FDM % | Salting | Ripening |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cheddar | Rennet (sweet) | 5.4–5.5 | ≤ 39 | 50–56 | Dry-salt | 3–12 mo |
| Mozzarella | Rennet + thermophilic | 5.1–5.2 | 45–55 | 40–45 | Brine | Few days–weeks |
| Gouda | Rennet | 5.4 | 41–48 | 46–51 | Brine | 1–6 mo |
| Swiss (Emmental) | Rennet + thermophilic | 5.2 | 36–40 | 47–55 | Brine | 4–12 mo |
| Cottage | Acid (no rennet) | 4.6 | 79–80 | 4 (low fat) | None | None (fresh) |
| Paneer | Acid (citric/lactic) | 5.4 | ~ 70 | 50 | None | None (fresh) |

### 2.3 Equipment Used in Cheese Manufacture

| Equipment | Function |
|---|---|
| **Cheese Vat** (open double-O, horizontal) | Coagulation, cutting, cooking, whey drainage |
| **Stainless-steel knives / wire harp** | Cut curd into cubes |
| **Stirrer / agitator** | Cooks curd gently to expel whey |
| **Pre-press / cheese hoops** | Shaping curd (with perforated sides) |
| **Vertical / horizontal cheese press** | Continuous pressing at 0.4–1.5 kg/cm² |
| **Brine tank** | Salting by osmosis (~ 18–23 % NaCl brine) |
| **Ripening room / cave** | Temperature (8–14 °C) and humidity (85–95 %) controlled |
| **Milling machine** | Cuts cheddared slab into chips for salting |

### 2.4 Ghee Manufacturing Methods

> [!NOTE]
> Five industrial routes exist for ghee production. Each yields a slightly different flavour and colour profile.

**Method 1 — Creamery-Butter Method (most common)**
- Cream (40–45 % fat) is separated from milk → pasteurised (85 °C / 15 min) → cooled → churned into butter → butter melted in a ghee boiler at **110–120 °C** until moisture evaporates and curd particles turn golden brown (NOC: non-enzymatic browning / Maillard reaction between lactose and casein) → clarified fat is decanted, filtered, cooled, packed.

**Method 2 — Direct-Cream Method**
- Cream (60–70 % fat) is directly heated to 110–120 °C in a ghee boiler → whey proteins coagulate and brown → fat is decanted. Saves one operation (no churning); gives higher free fatty acid (FFA) — sharper flavour.

**Method 3 — Pre-Stratification Method**
- Butter is melted at 60 °C to liquefy fat, then settled at 50 °C in a clarification tank. Top fat layer is decanted and further heated only to 110–115 °C. Yield is higher; flavour mild.

**Method 4 — Continuous Method (Ghee Concentrator)**
- Butter is melted → pumped through a **scraped-surface heat exchanger** (SSHE) at high temperature → vapour flashed off in vacuum chamber → golden fat collected. Capacity 1–10 t/h.

**Method 5 — Indigenous / Desi Method**
- Curd → makhani → heated in an open iron pan → clarified. Used in rural India.

### 2.5 Equipment Used in Ghee Manufacture

| Equipment | Function |
|---|---|
| **Cream separator** (Alfa-Laval / Westfalia) | Mechanical separation of cream from skim milk |
| **Butter churn** (combined / Fritz) | Converts cream into butter |
| **Ghee boiler** (steam-jacketed, double-jacketed SS) | Heats butter or cream to 110–120 °C; agitator prevents scorching |
| **Scraped-surface heat exchanger (SSHE)** | Continuous heating |
| **Clarification/settling tank** | Decanting the clear fat |
| **Ghee filter** (cloth or SS mesh) | Removes residual curd particles |
| **Ghee cooling tank** | Cools ghee from 80 °C to 50 °C before packing (prevents grainy texture) |
| **Packaging line** | Tin / jar / pouch filling |

### 2.6 Ice Cream and Frozen Dessert Manufacturing

**Ice cream mix formulation** has six ingredient classes:
- Dairy components: skim milk powder (SMP), cream, butter, milk
- Sweeteners: sucrose, glucose syrup, fructose
- Stabilisers: sodium alginate, carrageenan, guar gum, CMC, gelatin (0.1–0.5 %)
- Emulsifiers: mono- and di-glycerides (MDG), polysorbate 80 (0.1–0.3 %)
- Colour & flavour
- Water (implicit — total water balance)

**Manufacturing steps:**

1. **Mix preparation** — dry ingredients blended with warm milk; sugar, SMP, stabiliser mixed separately (dry-blend) to prevent clumping.
2. **Pasteurisation** — **80–85 °C / 15–30 s** (HTST) or 68 °C / 30 min (batch). Higher than milk pasteurisation to stabilise the mix.
3. **Homogenisation** — two-stage, **2,000–2,500 psi (first stage) / 500 psi (second)** to break fat globules into < 2 µm.
4. **Ageing** — mix held at **4 °C for 4–24 h** to allow stabiliser hydration, protein hydration, and fat partial crystallisation.
5. **Freezing (continuous freezer)** — mix at −2 to −6 °C drawn into a **swept-surface freezer** (e.g., Cherry-Burrell, Crepaco). Air is incorporated → **overrun** of 70–100 %.
6. **Hardening** — quickly freezes the drawn ice cream in a hardening tunnel at **−35 to −40 °C** for 1–4 h to bring core to −18 °C.
7. **Packaging & storage** — at ≤ −22 °C.

### 2.7 Equipment Used in Ice Cream Manufacture

| Equipment | Function |
|---|---|
| **Mix preparation tank** (SS, jacketed) | Blends ingredients |
| **Plate heat exchanger** | Pasteurises mix (HTST) |
| **Homogeniser** (two-stage) | Fat-globule size reduction |
| **Ageing tank** | 4 °C / 4–24 h |
| **Continuous freezer** (e.g., Hoyer, Tetra Pak) | Freezes, whips, aerates mix |
| **Hardening tunnel / cabinet** | Rapid final freezing |
| **Filling machine** | Cups, cones, tubs, sticks |
| **Fruit feeder / variegating pump** | Adds inclusions inline |

### 2.8 Standards for Milk and Milk Products — FSSAI Snapshot

| Product | Fat (Min) | SNF (Min) | Moisture (Max) | Other |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Whole milk (toned) | 3.0 % | 8.5 % | — | — |
| Standardised milk | 4.5 % | 8.5 % | — | — |
| Cream (table) | 25 % | — | — | — |
| Butter | 80 % | — | 16 % | Salt 3 % |
| Ghee | — | — | 0.5 % | BR 40–43, R-M 28–30, FFA ≤ 2 % |
| Cheddar | 50 % FDM | — | 39 % | — |
| Mozzarella | 45 % FDM | — | 52 % | — |
| Ice cream (dairy) | 10 % | — | — | MSNF 3.5 %, stabiliser/emulsifier ≤ 1 % |
| Processed cheese | 40 % FDM | — | 47 % | Citrate ≤ 3 %, phosphate ≤ 2 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Baudouin test** is the official FSSAI test for detecting **vanaspati** (hydrogenated vegetable fat) adulteration in ghee. The test gives a **cherry-red colour** when sesamol (from vanaspati) is present in the presence of furfural and HCl.

### 2.9 Standards — Microbiological Limits

| Product | SPC (per g/mL) | Coliforms | Pathogens |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pasteurised milk | ≤ 30,000 cfu/mL | ≤ 10/mL | Nil *Salmonella, E. coli* |
| Butter | ≤ 10,000 cfu/g | ≤ 10/g | Nil |
| Ghee | ≤ 5,000 cfu/g | < 10/g | Nil |
| Ice cream | ≤ 50,000 cfu/g | ≤ 100/g | Nil *Salmonella, Listeria* |

### 2.10 Packaging of Milk and Milk Products

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Packaging functions are summarised by the acronym **"C-FRESH"**: Containment, Freshness preservation, Retail appeal, Economy, Safety, Handling convenience.

| Product | Common Package | Material | Shelf Life |
|---|---|---|---|
| Liquid milk (pasteurised) | Pouch | LDPE (low-density polyethylene) co-extruded with EVA / LLDPE | 1–3 days at 4 °C |
| UHT milk | Tetra Brik / Tetra Pak | Paperboard + PE + Al-foil + PE (5-ply) | 6–9 months ambient |
| Flavoured milk | Glass bottle / PET | Soda-lime glass / PET | 6–12 months |
| Butter | Foil + paper | Aluminium foil + parchment | 2–3 months at 4 °C |
| Ghee | Tin / jar / pouch | Tinplate / glass / multi-layer PET pouch | 6–12 months |
| Cheese (hard) | Wax / vacuum pouch | Paraffin wax + plastic | 3–6 months |
| Cheese (soft) | Tub | Polystyrene | 2–4 weeks |
| Ice cream | Cup / cone / stick | Paperboard + PE | 6–9 months at −22 °C |
| Ice cream (bulk) | Tub | Polypropylene / polystyrene | 12 months |

### 2.11 Packaging Materials — Properties Comparison

| Material | O₂ barrier | Moisture barrier | Light barrier | Cost | Recyclable |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Glass | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | High | Yes |
| Tinplate (steel + Sn) | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Medium | Yes |
| Al-foil | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | High | Yes |
| LDPE | Poor | Good | Poor | Low | Limited |
| PET | Good | Good | Fair | Medium | Yes |
| Tetra Pak (paperboard + Al + PE) | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Medium | Limited |
| PS (polystyrene) | Poor | Fair | Fair | Low | Limited |

### 2.12 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $$ Y_{cheese} = \frac{0.93 \cdot F + 1.09 \cdot C - 0.005 \cdot \text{whey loss}}{100 - M} $$ | Van Slyke cheese-yield formula | kg cheese / 100 kg milk |
| $$\text{Overrun \%} = \frac{V_{ice-cream} - V_{mix}}{V_{mix}} \times 100$$ | Air incorporation in ice cream | % |
| $$\text{MSNF \%} = \text{Total Solids \%} - \text{Fat \%} - \text{Sugar \%}$$ | Milk solids-not-fat in mix | % |
| $$\text{FDM \%} = \frac{\text{Fat \%}}{100 - \text{Moisture \%}} \times 100$$ | Fat in dry matter (cheese) | % |
| $$\text{SNF \%} = 0.25 \cdot \text{LR} + 0.22 \cdot \text{Fat \%} + 0.72$$ | Richmond's formula for SNF (cow milk) | % |
| $$\text{Recovery \%} = \frac{m_{product} \cdot X_{product}}{m_{feed} \cdot X_{feed}} \times 100$$ | Component recovery | % |
| $$ t_{death} = \frac{\log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10} N}{k} $$ | Decimal-reduction time kinetics | min |
| $$ q = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{A \cdot \theta} $$ | Heat flux during pasteurisation | W/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> In the cheese yield formula, **F** = fat % in milk, **C** = casein % in milk, **M** = desired moisture % in cheese. Van Slyke's constants (0.93 for fat, 1.09 for casein) account for the typical solids retained in the curd.

### 2.13 Real-World Utility of These Systems

- **Cheese ripening rooms** are equipped with **PID-controlled chillers** and **humidifiers** to maintain ± 0.5 °C and ± 2 % RH — a standard **HVAC engineering problem**.
- **Ghee boilers** are designed as **steam-jacketed vessels** with scraped agitators; engineers calculate steam flow from energy balance: $\dot{Q} = m \cdot L$ where $L$ is latent heat of steam.
- **Ice cream continuous freezers** operate with **NH₃ or CO₂ refrigeration**; engineers use the **log mean temperature difference (LMTD)** method for heat-exchanger design.
- **Tetra Pak** aseptic packaging systems are sterilised with **H₂O₂ (35 % at 80 °C)** before filling — a classic **chemical engineering disinfection problem**.
- **Shelf-life prediction** uses the **Arrhenius equation**: $k = A \, e^{-E_a / R T}$ — applied to lipid oxidation in ghee, bacterial growth in pasteurised milk.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Van Slyke Cheese Yield Formula (Full Working)

**Objective:** Derive an equation that predicts the mass of cheese (kg) obtainable from 100 kg of standardised milk, given the fat and casein content.

**Step 1 — Component balance:** Consider 100 kg of standardised milk as the control volume. Let **F** = kg of fat per 100 kg milk, **C** = kg of casein per 100 kg milk. The cheese curd retains practically all the casein and most of the fat.

**Step 2 — Empirical retention coefficients (from dairy chemistry):**
- Fraction of fat retained in curd ≈ **0.93**
- Fraction of casein retained in curd ≈ **1.09** (this > 1 because additional components such as whey proteins trapped in cheddared curd contribute to the *curd solids* term)
- Fraction of *additional solids* (citrate, salts) ≈ **0.005 × whey-loss factor**

**Step 3 — Solids-in-cheese expression:** Mass of cheese solids (kg per 100 kg milk):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{cheese} &= 0.93 \cdot F + 1.09 \cdot C - 0.005 \cdot W_{loss}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert cheese solids to cheese mass:** Cheese mass = cheese solids ÷ (1 − moisture fraction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{cheese} &= \frac{S_{cheese}}{100 - M}\times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

(where $M$ is the desired moisture % of cheese expressed as a fraction).

**Step 5 — Final form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{cheese} = \frac{0.93 F + 1.09 C - 0.005 W_{loss}}{100 - M} \quad \text{(kg cheese per 100 kg milk)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical Van Slyke equation**. It under-estimates by 3–5 % in modern plants because of fines-loss recovery; modified by Lawrence and collaborators as the *Van Slyke–Lawrence formula*.

**Numerical example — Worked through:**

Milk: 4.0 % fat, 2.6 % casein, whey loss factor 5, target cheese moisture = 38 %.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= \frac{0.93 (4.0) + 1.09 (2.6) - 0.005 (5)}{100 - 38} \times 100 \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{3.72 + 2.834 - 0.025}{62} \times 100 \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{6.529}{62} \times 100 \\[4pt]
  &= 10.53 \text{ kg cheese per 100 kg milk}
\end{aligned}
$$

So **~ 10.5 kg** of Cheddar from **100 kg milk**, or in industry shorthand, **~ 10.5 % yield**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overrun Calculation (Step-by-Step)

**Objective:** Express the percentage increase in volume of ice cream relative to mix.

**Step 1 — Define variables:**
- $V_{mix}$ = volume of liquid mix before freezing (mL)
- $V_{ice-cream}$ = volume of frozen aerated ice cream (mL)

**Step 2 — Air volume incorporated:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{air} = V_{ice-cream} - V_{mix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fractional volume increase (decimal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{air} = \frac{V_{air}}{V_{mix}} = \frac{V_{ice-cream} - V_{mix}}{V_{mix}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert fraction to percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overrun \%} = \frac{V_{ice-cream} - V_{mix}}{V_{mix}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example:** $V_{mix} = 1000$ mL, $V_{ice-cream} = 1750$ mL.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overrun} = \frac{1750 - 1000}{1000} \times 100 = 75 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass-based check (KTU examiner favourite):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overrun} = \frac{\rho_{mix} - \rho_{ice-cream}}{\rho_{ice-cream}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\rho_{mix} \approx 1.10 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_{ice-cream} \approx 0.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$ for standard overrun of ~ 100 %.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — FDM (Fat-in-Dry-Matter) Calculation

**Objective:** Standardise fat content accounting for water content in cheese — used to compare cheeses of different moisture.

**Step 1 — Cheese composition split:** Total cheese mass = water (W) + dry matter (DM). Fat (F) is part of DM.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DM \%} &= 100 - \text{Moisture \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Fat as a fraction of dry matter:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FDM \%} = \frac{\text{Fat \% in cheese}}{100 - \text{Moisture \% in cheese}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example:** Cheese has 24 % fat and 38 % moisture (typical Cheddar):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FDM} = \frac{24}{100 - 38} \times 100 = \frac{24}{62} \times 100 = 38.71 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> FSSAI defines Cheddar as having **FDM ≥ 50 %**. The example above would **fail** the FSSAI Cheddar standard — it represents only a 50 % FDM Cheddar on the borderline. Verify FDM with the **regulatory minimum**, not just the absolute fat percentage.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Richmond's SNF Formula (Derivation Sketch)

**Objective:** Estimate the SNF of cow milk from two simple lab measurements: **lactometer reading (LR)** and **fat %**.

**Step 1 — Relationship of specific gravity to total solids:** The lactometer reading (corrected to 15.6 °C) multiplied by 0.25 approximates the specific-gravity contribution to total solids. Empirically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total solids \%} \approx 0.25 \cdot LR + 1.21 \cdot F + 0.72
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — SNF = Total solids − Fat:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNF \%} &= (0.25 \cdot LR + 1.21 \cdot F + 0.72) - F \\[4pt]
\text{SNF \%} &= 0.25 \cdot LR + 0.21 \cdot F + 0.72
\end{aligned}
$$

A simpler variant used in field labs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNF \%} = 0.25 \cdot LR + 0.22 \cdot F + 0.72
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example:** $LR = 28.5$, $F = 4.0$ %:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNF} = 0.25 (28.5) + 0.22 (4.0) + 0.72 = 7.125 + 0.88 + 0.72 = 8.725 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Energy Required for Ghee Manufacture (Steam Mass)

**Objective:** Find the mass of saturated steam at 4 bar (enthalpy ~ 2,738 kJ/kg, latent ~ 2,137 kJ/kg) required to process 100 kg of butter into ghee.

**Step 1 — Energy to raise butter from 25 °C to 110 °C:** Specific heat of butter ≈ 2.0 kJ/kg·K.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 100 \times 2.0 \times (110 - 25) = 17,000 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy to evaporate ~ 16 % moisture from butter:** latent heat of water ≈ 2,260 kJ/kg.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_2 = 16 \times 2260 = 36,160 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy to superheat vapour (100 → 110 °C):** $m c_{p,steam} \Delta T = 16 \times 2.01 \times 10 = 322$ kJ.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_3 = 322 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total heat:** $Q = 17{,}000 + 36{,}160 + 322 = 53{,}482$ kJ.

**Step 5 — Steam mass:** using $Q = m_s \cdot L$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_s = \frac{53,482}{2137} = 25.03 \text{ kg steam per 100 kg butter}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, ghee boilers are insulated so heat losses are ~ 5–10 %. Add an **overall efficiency factor η ≈ 0.85**: $m_s^{actual} \approx 29.45$ kg.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Critical Process Parameters

| Process | Critical Control Point (CCP) | Limit | Monitoring |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cheese pasteurisation | Temperature | ≥ 72 °C | Chart recorder |
| Cheese ripening | pH | 4.8–5.5 | pH meter |
| Ghee browning | Temperature | 110–120 °C | Thermocouple |
| Ghee moisture | End-point moisture | ≤ 0.5 % | Karl-Fischer / hot-plate |
| Ice cream mix pasteurisation | Temperature/time | 80–85 °C / 15 s | Chart recorder |
| Ice cream freezing | Draw temperature | −5 to −6 °C | Digital thermometer |
| UHT milk sterilisation | F₀ value | ≥ 3 min | Pen recorder |
| Tetra Pak sealing | Seal integrity | 100 % | Seal test |
| Ghee packaging | Headspace O₂ | < 2 % | Headspace analyser |

### 3.7 Worked-Out Numerical — Cheese Yield by Component Recovery

Milk fat 4.0 %, casein 2.6 %, ash 0.7 %, whey solids 5.5 %, moisture 87.2 %. Curd fat recovery 90 %, casein recovery 95 %, ash recovery 35 %, moisture retained 60 %, whey solids lost 95 %. Cheese moisture target 38 %.

**Step 1 — Solids retained per 100 kg milk:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fat retained} &= 4.0 \times 0.90 = 3.60 \text{ kg}\\[4pt]
\text{Casein retained} &= 2.6 \times 0.95 = 2.47 \text{ kg}\\[4pt]
\text{Ash retained} &= 0.7 \times 0.35 = 0.245 \text{ kg}\\[4pt]
\text{Lactose retained} &= 5.5 \times 0.05 = 0.275 \text{ kg}\\[4pt]
\text{Whey proteins retained} &\approx 0 \text{ (negligible)}\\[4pt]
\text{Total solids retained} &= 3.60 + 2.47 + 0.245 + 0.275 = 6.59 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Moisture retained:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{water} &= 87.2 \times 0.60 = 52.32 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total cheese mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{cheese} = 6.59 + 52.32 = 58.91 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Check moisture:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M\% = \frac{52.32}{58.91} \times 100 = 88.8 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

This is far above the 38 % target, indicating the cheese is **too moist**. The plant must reduce moisture by **further pressing/draining** or **lower the moisture retention coefficient** (cook the curd longer, finer cutting, higher stir-out).

### 3.8 KTU Board-Standard Layout for Process Flow Diagram Description

When writing a 14-mark answer for any of these processes, structure your description as:

> *Step 1 → Step 2 → Step 3 ...* (use arrows)
> *Identify the equipment at each step.*
> *Identify the principle of operation of each equipment (1 line).*
> *Note critical control parameters at each step.*

This is the **valuation key pattern** for KTU ESE Module 4 — examiner's eye moves down looking for arrows, equipment names, principles, and CCPs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Avoid writing "raw milk → cheese" as a single step. Always write **at least 7–8 distinct unit operations** with **equipment names** and **principles**.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1.** What is the role of **rennet** in cheese manufacture?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Rennet contains the enzyme **chymosin (rennin)**, which cleaves the **κ-casein** fraction of casein micelles at the **Phe105–Met106 peptide bond**. This cleavage removes the hydrophilic **glycomacropeptide (caseinomacropeptide)** from the micelle surface, destabilising the micelle and causing aggregation into a three-dimensional protein gel (the curd). The gel traps fat and water. Rennet thus transforms liquid milk into a semi-solid gel that can be cut to expel whey.

---

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** List any **four functions of stabilisers** in ice cream.

**Model Answer (3 marks, 4 points = 1 mark each):**

1. **Inhibit ice crystal growth** during storage, retaining a smooth mouthfeel.
2. **Increase mix viscosity** so that air incorporation (overrun) is stable.
3. **Prevent whey separation (syneresis)** in the finished product during thawing cycles.
4. **Improve body and texture**, contributing to a creamy melt-down.

(Common stabilisers: sodium alginate, carrageenan, guar gum, CMC, gelatin, sodium carboxymethylcellulose.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End-Semester Examination) provides **internal choice within a module**. Two full 14-mark questions are presented below; pick ONE. Each question has **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A — Cheese Focus [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **manufacture of Cheddar cheese** with a process flow diagram. List at least **seven equipment** used.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Reception and Standardisation (1 mark):** Whole milk is received, tested, and standardised to a casein-to-fat ratio of about 0.70 by partial skimming or blending with skim milk.

**Step 2 — Pasteurisation (1 mark):** HTST pasteuriser at **72 °C / 15 s** destroys pathogens and inactivates lipases.

**Step 3 — Cooling and Starter Addition (1 mark):** Cooled to **30–32 °C**; mesophilic starter (Lactococcus lactis subsp. lactis and cremoris) added at 1–2 %; ripened 30–45 min until acidity reaches **0.18–0.20 % lactic acid**.

**Step 4 — Rennet Coagulation (0.5 mark):** Single-strength rennet (~ 20 mL / 100 kg milk) added; coagulation in **30 min**; firm clean break test.

**Step 5 — Cutting and Cooking (0.5 mark):** Curd cut by vertical/horizontal wire harp into **8–10 mm cubes**; cooked gradually to **38–40 °C** over 30 min with gentle agitation.

**Step 6 — Whey Drainage and Cheddaring (1 mark):** Whey drained; curd mats formed by fusion into **slabs (15 cm)**, inverted every 15 min for 1.5–2 h — pH drops to 5.4.

**Step 7 — Milling and Salting (0.5 mark):** Slab milled into **fingers (~ 1 cm)** and dry-salted with **2–3 % salt** to enhance flavour and depress unwanted microflora.

**Step 8 —Hooping, Pressing and Ripening (1 mark):** Pressed at **0.4–0.7 kg/cm²** for 16–20 h; ripened at **8–10 °C, 85 % RH** for 3–12 months.

**Equipment list (1 mark):** Receiving tank, plate heat exchanger, cheese vat, wire harp, stirrer, cheddaring table, mill, hoops, vertical/horizontal press, brine tank, ripening room.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Write the **Van Slyke cheese yield formula** and calculate the yield from milk having **4.0 % fat** and **2.6 % casein** for Cheddar of **38 % moisture**. Assume whey loss factor = 5.

**Model Answer:**

**Formula (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{cheese} = \frac{0.93 F + 1.09 C - 0.005 W_{loss}}{100 - M} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= \frac{0.93 \times 4.0 + 1.09 \times 2.6 - 0.005 \times 5}{100 - 38} \times 100 \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{3.72 + 2.834 - 0.025}{62} \times 100 \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{6.529}{62} \times 100 \\[4pt]
  &= 10.53 \text{ kg cheese per 100 kg milk (4 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comments:** Yield ≈ 10.5 %, consistent with industrial Cheddar norms.

---

#### **Question B — Ghee + Ice Cream Focus [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **manufacture of ghee by the creamery-butter method** with equipment and process conditions.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Cream Separation (1 mark):** Whole milk is separated into cream (40–45 % fat) and skim milk using an Alfa-Laval type centrifugal separator.

**Step 2 — Pasteurisation (1 mark):** Cream pasteurised at **85 °C / 15 min** in a plate heat exchanger to kill pathogens.

**Step 3 — Cooling and Churning (1 mark):** Cooled to **10 °C**, then aged 2–4 h for fat crystallisation. Churned in combined churn into butter; butter washed with cold water to remove buttermilk solids.

**Step 4 — Melting (1 mark):** Butter fed into a **steam-jacketed ghee boiler** with mechanical agitator. Temperature raised to **110–120 °C** over 30–45 min.

**Step 5 — Moisture Evaporation and Browning (1 mark):** Water boils off; curd particles gradually turn golden brown due to **Maillard reaction** between lactose and casein-amino groups. This develops the characteristic **nutty aroma** of ghee.

**Step 6 — Clarification (1 mark):** Heating stopped; clarified fat decanted; curd particles filtered through 80–100 mesh SS screen.

**Step 7 — Cooling and Packaging (1 mark):** Cooled to 50–55 °C and packed in tinplate containers or PET jars.

**Equipment (auto-marked from steps):** Cream separator, plate heat exchanger, churn, ghee boiler (steam jacketed), filter, ghee cooling tank, packaging line.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Define **overrun**. A mix of 1,000 mL yields 1,800 mL of ice cream. Calculate the **overrun %**. Comment on whether this is **suitable for premium or standard** ice cream.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Overrun is the **percentage increase in volume** of ice cream relative to the original mix, due to incorporation of air during freezing/whipping.

**Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overrun \%} = \frac{V_{ice-cream} - V_{mix}}{V_{mix}} \times 100 = \frac{1800 - 1000}{1000} \times 100 = 80 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (2 marks):** 80 % overrun falls within the **standard commercial ice cream** range (70–100 %). Premium and super-premium brands typically use **lower overruns (20–50 %)** because they contain higher milk-fat and MSNF levels, providing richer body without requiring as much air for creaminess.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Cheese question):** Students commonly write only **5–6 steps** instead of the **8 steps expected by the valuation key**. Each omitted step = **0.5 mark lost** → you lose 1 mark easily. Always write a **complete 8-step** line.
>
> **Examiner Pitfall (Ghee question):** Writing "butter is heated" **without specifying temperature (110–120 °C)** costs 1 mark. The temperature window is the **valuation keyword**.
>
> **Examiner Pitfall (Ice cream question):** Writing only the **overrun formula** without a **comment on premium/standard quality** loses the 2-mark comment mark.
>
> **Examiner Pitfall (Packaging):** Listing materials without **justifying why** (e.g., "Al foil is used because O₂ barrier is critical for ghee to prevent oxidative rancidity") = -1 mark per product.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

**Core concepts to remember:**

- [x] **Cheese** = **casein coagulated + whey drained + ripened**. Van Slyke yield = $(0.93F + 1.09C - 0.005 W_{loss})/(100 - M) \times 100$.
- [x] **Cheddar** is a **sweet-curd, hard, ripened** cheese — moisture ≤ 39 %, FDM ≥ 50 %, rennet set, dry-salted.
- [x] **Mozzarella** uses **thermophilic cultures**, pH 5.1–5.2, plasticised in hot water (70–80 °C) for **pasta filata** (stretching).
- [x] **Paneer** is a **fresh, acid-coagulated** cheese (citric/lactic acid, no rennet); moisture ~ 70 %.
- [x] **Ghee** = clarified milk fat; moisture ≤ 0.5 %; FFA ≤ 2 %; Baudouin test detects vanaspati.
- [x] **Ghee-making temperature window** = **110–120 °C**; below 100 °C → curd not brown; above 130 °C → burnt, dark, FOA high.
- [x] **Maillard reaction** = lactose + casein-amino groups → brown + nutty flavour of ghee.
- [x] **Ice cream mix** is pasteurised at **80–85 °C / 15 s** (HTST), homogenised at **2500/500 psi**, aged **4 °C / 4–24 h**, and frozen continuously to **−5 to −6 °C**.
- [x] **Overrun** = 70–100 % (standard); 20–50 % (premium).
- [x] **Stabilisers** prevent ice crystal growth and whey separation; **emulsifiers** promote partial coalescence of fat globules.
- [x] **FSSAI** sets minimum standards; **BIS** sets testing methods; **Codex** sets international reference.
- [x] **Pasteurisation** legal baselines: **72 °C / 15 s** OR **63 °C / 30 min**.
- [x] **UHT** = 135–150 °C / 2–4 s; requires **aseptic packaging** (Tetra Pak).
- [x] **Tetra Pak** layers (outer to inner): **PE / Paperboard / PE / Al / PE** — Al gives O₂ + light barrier.
- [x] **LDPE pouch** = milk for daily consumption (1–3 days); **Tetra Brik** = ambient shelf life 6–9 months.
- [x] **Ghee** packed in **tinplate** (best barrier); **butter** in **Al foil/parchment** wrap; **hard cheese** in **wax/vacuum pouch**; **soft cheese** in **PS tub**; **ice cream** in **paperboard / PP tub**.
- [x] **FDM** = Fat / (100 − Moisture) × 100 — the standardised way to compare cheese fatness.
- [x] **Recovery %** = $(m_{product} \cdot X_{product}) / (m_{feed} \cdot X_{feed}) \times 100$.
- [x] **Ripening room** for cheese: **8–14 °C, 85–95 % RH** — bacteria + enzymes transform the curd into flavourful cheese.

**Three Most-Likely KTU Board Traps:**

1. Confusing **MSNF %** (milk solids not fat) with **SNF %** — both refer to total non-fat solids.
2. Writing **overrun = air volume** (it's the **percentage increase in volume**, not absolute air volume).
3. Stating **ghee is butter** — ghee is **clarified milk fat from butter**, with moisture removed and curd browned.

**Quick-recall formulas (one-liners):**

- $\text{Overrun \%} = \frac{V_{ice} - V_{mix}}{V_{mix}} \times 100$
- $\text{FDM \%} = \frac{F}{100 - M} \times 100$
- $\text{SNF \%} = 0.25 LR + 0.22 F + 0.72$
- $\text{Recovery \%} = \frac{m_p X_p}{m_f X_f} \times 100$
- $m_s = \frac{Q_{total}}{L_{steam}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark module questions, **draw the flowchart FIRST** (it instantly earns 2 marks), then write the steps in sequence, then list equipment with one-line principles, then close with a numerical sub-part. This sequence matches the KTU valuation key exactly and prevents the "missing steps" pitfall that causes most mark losses.
